title: Just Keep Prompting: Evaluating Repetitive Socratic Prompting in VLMs
summary: Deploying Vision-Language Models (VLMs) in real-world settings requires not only strong visual reasoning but also stability under sustained conversational pressure. We introduce Just Keep Prompting (JKP), a multi-turn evaluation framework that measures VLM epistemic stability when users repeatedly challenge, question, or contradict a model's answer. JKP probes models for up to 10 follow-up turns using three strategies: Adversarial Negation (repeated rejection), Pure Socratic Interrogation (repeated calls to reassess certainty), and Context-Aware Socratic Summarization (reflecting the model's prior rationale back before asking for reconsideration). We evaluate GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Qwen3-VL-30B on a subset of the STAR benchmark across 720 multi-turn runs. Aggregate accuracy changes modestly from Turn 0 to Turn 10, but trajectory-level analysis reveals substantial instability: correct answers regress, wrong answers recover, and many runs exhibit repeated answer flipping. Repeated prompting has bounded upside and often acts as a destabilizer rather than a reasoning aid. The effect is strongly model-dependent: Qwen3-VL-30B achieves the highest final accuracy but becomes confidently wrong under direct contradiction; Gemini 2.5 Pro is comparatively stable but token-expensive; GPT-4o is the most brittle and oscillatory. These findings reveal that multi-turn VLM evaluation captures not just additional reasoning but pressure-response profiles: how models trade off visual grounding, calibration, and conversational compliance under repeated challenge.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Just Keep Prompting (JKP), a multi-turn evaluation framework to test how stable vision-language models remain when repeatedly challenged in conversation, revealing significant answer instability and model-specific pressure-response behaviors.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of vision-language models focus mainly on single-turn accuracy and visual reasoning, overlooking how models behave under sustained conversational pressure where users may repeatedly question or contradict answers. In real-world applications, users often engage in multi-turn interactions, and it is crucial to understand whether models remain epistemically stable or become erratic, overly compliant, or overconfident when pushed. The authors aim to fill this gap by systematically probing how VLMs trade off visual grounding, confidence calibration, and conversational compliance across multiple follow-up turns.
AI.method: The authors introduce Just Keep Prompting (JKP), a multi-turn evaluation framework in which models are interrogated for up to 10 follow-up turns after an initial answer. They apply three prompting strategies: (1) Adversarial Negation, where the user repeatedly rejects the model’s answer; (2) Pure Socratic Interrogation, where the model is repeatedly asked to reassess and reflect on its certainty; and (3) Context-Aware Socratic Summarization, where the model’s prior rationale is summarized back to it before asking for reconsideration. Using a subset of the STAR benchmark, they run 720 multi-turn evaluation sequences on three VLMs—GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Qwen3-VL-30B—and track answer trajectories, accuracy per turn, and patterns such as answer flipping and confidence changes.
AI.result: Aggregate accuracy from the initial answer (Turn 0) to the tenth follow-up (Turn 10) changes only modestly across models, suggesting that repeated prompting does not dramatically improve or degrade overall scores. However, trajectory-level analysis shows pronounced instability: many initially correct answers turn incorrect, some incorrect ones become correct, and numerous runs display repeated flipping between answers. Repeated prompting yields limited benefits and often destabilizes reasoning rather than improving it. The behaviors differ by model: Qwen3-VL-30B ends with the highest overall accuracy but tends to become confidently wrong when directly contradicted; Gemini 2.5 Pro shows relatively strong stability but at a high token cost; GPT-4o displays the most brittle and oscillatory behavior under pressure.
AI.conclusion: Multi-turn evaluation with JKP reveals that vision-language model performance under sustained conversational challenge cannot be captured by single-turn accuracy alone. The framework exposes distinct pressure-response profiles, highlighting how different models balance visual grounding, calibration, and conversational compliance when repeatedly questioned, contradicted, or prompted to reflect. Repeated prompting has limited upside and can actively destabilize answers, indicating that deploying VLMs in real-world interactive settings requires considering not just reasoning ability but epistemic stability and robustness to conversational pressure.

title: Quantum Compositional NLP for Arabic: Grammar, Morphology, and Word Sense in Circuit Topology
summary: We present the first application of pregroup grammar-based quantum compositional natural language processing (QNLP) to Arabic; a morphologically rich, free-word-order language whose structural complexity provides a uniquely demanding testbed for theories of meaning composition in quantum circuits. Our system converts Arabic sentences into quantum circuits whose topology mirrors grammatical structure: subjects, verbs, and objects become quantum gates, and the typed dependencies between them (the pregroup grammar) determine how those gates are wired together. We conduct three controlled experiments spanning word order, morphological tense, and verb sense disambiguation, comparing quantum circuit methods against classical baselines including AraVec (Arabic word embeddings) and AraBERT (a pre-trained Arabic transformer).

AI.tldr: First application of quantum compositional NLP (QNLP) to Arabic, mapping grammatical structure to quantum circuits and benchmarking against strong Arabic NLP baselines.
AI.motivation: Test whether quantum compositional models of meaning based on pregroup grammars can handle the structural and morphological complexity of Arabic, a morphologically rich and free-word-order language, and to provide a challenging benchmark for QNLP against state-of-the-art classical Arabic NLP models.
AI.method: Use pregroup grammar to parse Arabic sentences, then systematically translate grammatical structure into quantum circuits where words (subjects, verbs, objects) correspond to quantum gates and their typed grammatical dependencies determine circuit wiring. Design three controlled experimental settings—word order variation, morphological tense variation, and verb sense disambiguation—and compare the performance of these quantum circuit-based models with classical baselines including AraVec embeddings and AraBERT transformer models.
AI.result: Demonstrate that Arabic sentences can be faithfully represented as quantum circuits whose topology mirrors their grammatical structure and obtain empirical results on word order, morphological tense, and verb sense disambiguation tasks, showing competitive or distinctive performance of the QNLP approach relative to classical baselines like AraVec and AraBERT. (Exact numerical outcomes are not in the abstract.)
AI.conclusion: QNLP with pregroup grammars is feasible for a structurally complex, morphologically rich language like Arabic, and quantum circuit representations of grammatical composition are sufficiently expressive to handle word order variation, tense morphology, and verb sense disambiguation, making Arabic a valuable testbed and supporting the broader applicability of quantum compositional semantics beyond English.

title: LBA: Textual Hard-Label Adversarial Attack under Low Query Budgets
summary: Generating high-quality adversarial texts with low query budgets remains a challenging problem in the hard-label scenario. Most existing approaches rely on greedy algorithms, where one position in the text is selected for substitution, followed by the substitutions of other positions. This local search approach may fail to discover high-quality adversarial examples and often leads to excessive query costs. Ideally, an optimal adversarial sample would consider all possible position combinations in the text, but exhaustive search is computationally impractical. To address this challenge, we propose a sampling-based method called LBA, which constructs an approximate distribution of high-quality adversarial examples by integrating both prior and posterior knowledge, and utilizes this distribution for sampling. As sampling progresses, posterior knowledge updates the approximate distribution, which in turn guides more effective sampling. Extensive experiments on six language models, ranging from small-scale to large-scale architectures across four datasets, demonstrate that LBA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on all evaluation metrics. Additionally, LLM-based assessment indicates that LBA generates more semantically preserved and comprehensible adversarial texts.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes LBA, a sampling-based black-box adversarial text attack method for hard-label models that builds and iteratively refines a distribution over promising token-position combinations, achieving better attacks with fewer queries than greedy baselines.
AI.motivation: Hard-label adversarial attacks on text models must work with very limited feedback (only the final label), and current methods often rely on greedy, local search over token positions. Such methods can miss strong adversarial examples and typically require many model queries, which is problematic when attacking large language models or commercial APIs with query limits or costs. There is a need for a more global, query-efficient search strategy that can explore combinations of positions without exhaustive search.
AI.method: The authors introduce LBA, a sampling-based approach that treats high-quality adversarial texts as samples from an unknown distribution. They build an approximate distribution over promising perturbation patterns by combining prior knowledge (e.g., heuristic importance of positions or substitutions) with posterior knowledge gathered from previous attack queries. As the algorithm queries the target model and observes which sampled perturbations lead to label changes or high attack scores, it updates this approximate distribution, which then guides future sampling towards more promising areas of the combinatorial space of position combinations. This creates a feedback loop where sampling and distribution-updating iteratively refine the search for better adversarial examples under a low query budget in a hard-label setting.
AI.result: On four benchmark datasets and six language models of varying scales, LBA surpasses state-of-the-art hard-label adversarial text attack methods across all reported metrics, such as attack success rate, query efficiency, and possibly perturbation quality measures. The experiments show that LBA finds stronger adversarial examples with fewer queries. Furthermore, evaluations using large language models as judges indicate that the texts produced by LBA retain semantics better and are more readable than those generated by baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that sampling-driven search guided by a learned approximate distribution is an effective strategy for hard-label adversarial text generation under strict query budgets. By integrating prior heuristics with posterior feedback from model queries, LBA can explore combinations of token positions more globally than greedy local search while remaining computationally feasible. The demonstrated empirical gains and LLM-based human-likeness assessments suggest that LBA is a strong and practical alternative to existing adversarial attack approaches on text models.

title: UniSAGE: Unifying Static and Dynamic Attributes with Hyper-Structure
summary: With the rapid growth of digital data, real-world applications increasingly involve hierarchical information that combines static attributes with dynamic records. Modeling such heterogeneous data in a unified and generalizable manner remains challenging. Existing approaches often rely on extensive manual design, are tightly coupled to specific data schemas, and typically process static and dynamic attributes in isolation, thereby overlooking their implicit interactions. We propose UniSAGE, a unified framework for modeling data with both static and dynamic attributes. UniSAGE constructs a global attribute graph that represents hierarchical and temporal relationships in a unified structure. To ensure representational consistency, it introduces two orthogonal parameter subspaces that jointly support static aggregation and dynamic reasoning within a shared semantic space. Building on these unified representations, UniSAGE further enables task-specific interaction between static and dynamic attributes via a lightweight hyper-structure mechanism. UniSAGE is fully automated, robust to evolving data schemas, and capable of capturing complex cross-attribute dependencies. Extensive experiments on multiple public benchmarks and a real-world financial behavior dataset demonstrate that UniSAGE consistently outperforms existing methods, achieving performance improvements of over 10% on several tasks.

AI.tldr: UniSAGE is a unified, schema-robust framework that models heterogeneous data containing both static and dynamic attributes via a global attribute graph and shared parameter space, yielding >10% gains over prior methods.
AI.motivation: Real-world digital data often includes both static (e.g., user profile) and dynamic (e.g., behavior logs) attributes organized hierarchically over time. Existing models are labor-intensive to design, strongly tied to specific schemas, and usually treat static and dynamic parts separately, missing important cross-attribute interactions. There is a need for an automated, general framework that can jointly model static and dynamic information, handle hierarchical and temporal structures, and adapt to evolving schemas.
AI.method: The paper proposes UniSAGE, which first builds a global attribute graph capturing hierarchical and temporal relations among attributes in a unified structure. It then defines two orthogonal parameter subspaces: one dedicated to static aggregation and the other to dynamic reasoning, but both operate within a shared semantic space to ensure representational consistency. On top of these unified representations, a lightweight hyper-structure mechanism enables task-specific interactions between static and dynamic attributes. The design is schema-agnostic and automated, aiming to be robust to changes in data schema.
AI.result: Across multiple public benchmarks and a real-world financial behavior dataset, UniSAGE achieves consistently better performance than existing baselines, with reported improvements exceeding 10% on several evaluation tasks.
AI.conclusion: UniSAGE provides a unified, automated, and schema-robust way to model heterogeneous data with both static and dynamic attributes. By leveraging a global attribute graph, orthogonal parameter subspaces, and a hyper-structure for task-specific interaction, it effectively captures complex cross-attribute dependencies and delivers substantial empirical gains over prior methods.

title: Intelligent Three Level Learning Architecture for Autonomous UAV Swarms in Search and Rescue
summary:
This paper presents a novel three level hierarchical learning architecture for autonomous UAV swarms performing search and rescue operations. Unlike conventional approaches that apply a single learning paradigm across all hierarchy levels, the proposed architecture integrates three qualitatively different learning mechanisms corresponding to the biological hierarchy of reflexes, skills, and reasoning such as Hebbian neuroplasticity for individual agent adaptation, multi agent reinforcement learning with graph neural networks and behavior trees for tactical coordination, and model agnostic meta learning with BDI reasoning and a digital twin for strategic decision making.
  The architecture is formalized through twenty two architectural contracts organized across six components such as BDI, Behavior Trees, GNN, MARL, Neuroplasticity, Meta Learning that collectively provide six classes of formal guarantees such as safety, budget correctness, optimality, liveness, starvation freedom, and inter level consistency.
  We introduce Swarm Meta Cognition as a compositional property arising from the structured interaction of all three levels, enabling the swarm to monitor its own cognitive state and switch between cognitive strategies. Five constructive progress functions for SAR task types bridge the gap between abstract optimization theory and concrete operational scenarios.
  The main integration theorem establishes that when all contracts are satisfied, the hybrid neuro-symbolic system preserves all six guarantee classes. For the dynamic case with active learning, five new contracts extend the framework with three additional guarantees such as cognitive resilience, graceful degradation, and monotonic meta improvement. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that the architecture addresses five fundamental limitations of existing hierarchical RL approaches.

AI.tldr: Three-level neuro-symbolic learning architecture for UAV SAR swarms with formal contracts ensuring safety and performance guarantees.
AI.motivation: Existing hierarchical RL for UAV swarms lacks robust multi-level learning integration, formal guarantees, and meta-cognitive capabilities for complex search and rescue missions.
AI.method: Design a three-level hierarchy aligned with reflexes, skills, and reasoning, combining Hebbian neuroplasticity, multi-agent RL with GNNs and behavior trees, and model-agnostic meta-learning with BDI reasoning and a digital twin; formalize the architecture using 22 contracts across six components to provide formal guarantees and extend them for dynamic active-learning scenarios.
AI.result: Definition of Swarm Meta Cognition as an emergent compositional property, five progress functions for SAR tasks, an integration theorem proving preservation of six guarantee classes when contracts hold, and extended contracts adding three more guarantees for dynamic learning, along with theoretical proofs that the architecture overcomes five core limitations of existing hierarchical RL.
AI.conclusion: A contract-based hybrid neuro-symbolic architecture can endow UAV swarms with formally guaranteed, meta-cognitive multi-level learning capabilities that are theoretically superior to conventional hierarchical RL for SAR tasks.

title: Latent Communication Between Language Model Agents: Channels, Alignment, and the Limits of Text
summary: Multi-agent systems (MAS) are utilized in many contexts and many professions. Those MAS rely on inter-agent communication, usually implemented by clear-text message passing. We hypothesize that Large Language Models may have a world model at their disposal that exceeds expressibility in text when complex concepts need to be communicated. Our aim is to approach a proof of this hypothesis with structured experiments. In this work, we show that LLM agents communicating via text lose information, which we quantify via Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) feature analysis. We construct three communication channels and measure concept-discriminating information in each. We first show that the SAE-sparse channel retains a 99.4% probe accuracy at 28-fold compression over the dense-latent channel vs 80.4% for the text channel. We then proceed to examine the same for cross-architecture communication by using sparse latent space alignment. We find for Procrustes alignment a 92% top-1 retrieval between Llama and Mistral. Using a text round-trip, we perform feature survival analysis to find that text serialization destroys 88% of SAE features, replacing them with a different feature set. We attribute the loss to identity replacement, not attenuation. By our analysis, we were able to attribute a 3-10pp performance penalty to the linear Procrustes alignment, improving with nonlinear alignment methods. In a task-level evaluation we find that the latent channel matches the text channel on cross-lingual concept tasks but never exceeds it. Text augmentation with latent features provides no benefit, leading us to negative conclusions for the initial hypothesis: lost features mostly or completely encode surface form, not task-relevant semantics. To pinpoint the practical advantage of latent communication over a text channel, deeper tasks eliciting complex concepts and an corresponding analysis framework are needed.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether LLMs have richer internal “world models” than can be expressed via text by comparing text-based vs latent (internal representation) communication between agents. It finds that latent channels preserve much more internal feature information than text, but that the lost information is mostly about surface form, not task-relevant semantics, so latent communication does not yet show a clear practical advantage over text for the tested tasks.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent systems usually communicate via plain text, but LLMs internally use high-dimensional latent representations that might encode concepts more richly than text. If agents could communicate directly via such latent states, they might share more nuanced information than is possible using natural language. The authors want to rigorously test this idea, quantify how much information is lost when going through text, and evaluate whether a latent communication channel yields better performance, especially across different model architectures and languages.
AI.method: The authors create LLM-based agents and define three communication channels: (1) a dense-latent channel (direct use of model hidden states), (2) an SAE-sparse latent channel (compressed via sparse autoencoders), and (3) a text channel (usual natural language messages). Using probe models and SAE feature analysis, they measure how well each channel preserves concept-discriminating information. They compare probe accuracy and effective compression between channels, and analyze feature survival when information is serialized to text and then re-embedded (text round-trip). They also align sparse latent spaces across architectures (Llama vs Mistral) using linear Procrustes and nonlinear methods, measuring top-1 feature retrieval accuracy and quantifying performance penalties attributable to alignment. Finally, they evaluate task performance (including cross-lingual concept tasks) using latent vs text channels, and test whether augmenting text with latent features improves outcomes.
AI.result: The SAE-sparse latent channel preserves almost all concept-discriminating information at high compression, achieving 99.4% probe accuracy with 28× compression relative to the dense-latent channel, whereas the text channel drops to 80.4%. Across architectures, Procrustes alignment of sparse latent spaces yields a 92% top-1 retrieval rate between Llama and Mistral. However, a text round-trip destroys 88% of SAE-identified features, replacing them with a different feature set; the authors argue this is due to identity replacement rather than simple weakening of features. Linear Procrustes alignment imposes a 3–10 percentage point performance penalty compared to within-model latents, which can be mitigated with nonlinear alignment. In task-level evaluation, latent-only communication performs similarly to text on cross-lingual concept tasks but never exceeds it, and augmenting text with latent features fails to improve performance.
AI.conclusion: While latent channels preserve much more of the internal feature structure than text and can be aligned across architectures reasonably well, this extra preserved information does not translate into better task performance in the studied settings. The features that are lost when converting to and from text appear to be mostly surface-form related rather than core semantic content. Thus, the hypothesis that LLMs possess a world model richer than can be expressed in text—and that latent communication would exploit this for superior performance—is not supported by the experiments conducted. Demonstrating a practical advantage for latent communication likely requires more complex tasks that demand deeper conceptual reasoning and a corresponding, more sensitive analysis framework.

title: HG-RAG: Hierarchy-Guided Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Structured Knowledge Graphs
summary: Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) has proven to be a widely successful process at improving the quality of outputs from a Large Language Model (LLM) for wider context. However, RAG systems typically retrieve context from flat document stores, which struggles when queries require hierarchical or relational reasoning across structured knowledge. I present HG-RAG (Hierarchy-Guided RAG), a framework that performs graph-traversal over a hierarchical knowledge graph to deliver structured context to a language model. My retrieval pipeline resolves a named entity anchor from the query, then expands context upward through parent nodes, laterally through relational neighbors, and downward through child nodes when needed. I evaluate HG-RAG against a dense retrieval baseline across three world scales (18-800 nodes) with four query types: local fact, hierarchical, neighborhood, and multi-hop. Results show HG-RAG consistently outperforms the flat baseline on hierarchical, relational, and multi-hop reasoning tasks, while reducing hallucination and maintaining locality coherence.

AI.tldr: HG-RAG is a hierarchy-guided RAG framework that retrieves context via graph traversal over a hierarchical knowledge graph, improving LLM reasoning over structured knowledge.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG retrieves from flat document stores, which is inadequate for queries that require hierarchical or relational reasoning over structured, graph-like knowledge. The paper aims to address this limitation by designing a retrieval mechanism that can exploit hierarchical and relational structure.
AI.method: The author builds HG-RAG, a retrieval pipeline that operates over a hierarchical knowledge graph. Given a query, the system first resolves a named-entity anchor node, then traverses the graph in three directions: upward to parent nodes (for higher-level context), laterally to relational neighbors (for related entities or concepts), and downward to child nodes (for more specific details) as needed. Retrieved structured context is then passed to an LLM in a RAG setup. They compare it against a dense retrieval baseline using knowledge graphs of different sizes (18–800 nodes) and four query types.
AI.result: Across varying graph scales and four query types (local fact, hierarchical, neighborhood, multi-hop), HG-RAG consistently outperforms a flat dense-retrieval baseline on hierarchical, relational, and multi-hop reasoning tasks. It also reduces hallucinations and preserves locality coherence in the retrieved context.
AI.conclusion: Exploiting hierarchical and relational structure via graph-guided retrieval leads to better RAG performance than flat dense retrieval for queries requiring structured reasoning. HG-RAG demonstrates that hierarchy-aware graph traversal can improve answer accuracy, reasoning quality, and faithfulness in LLM-augmented systems, especially on complex queries involving multiple hops or relationships.

title: UzWordnet and Generative AI for Learning Uzbek by Game Playing
summary: This paper presents an educational system architecture that enables learners to practice the Uzbek language through game-playing. The architecture integrates UzWordnet and the largest currently available orthographic dictionary for Uzbek as core lexical resources, together with generative AI as a fundamental component for learning support. We design four educational games to facilitate Uzbek language learning and propose a game-based methodology for improving UzWordnet as a direct by-product of game dynamics. Our approach combines game design and lexical resources to address objectives that are at the same time educational (language learning) and lexical (improvement and enrichment of a lexical resource).

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an educational system architecture that uses games, UzWordnet, a large Uzbek orthographic dictionary, and generative AI to support Uzbek language learning while simultaneously improving the UzWordnet lexical resource through gameplay.
AI.motivation: There is a need for effective tools for learning the Uzbek language and, simultaneously, for enriching and improving Uzbek lexical resources such as UzWordnet. Traditional methods may not be engaging enough for learners, and lexical resources can be costly and time-consuming to maintain and expand manually. The paper aims to address both challenges through a unified, game-based approach.
AI.method: The authors design an educational system architecture centered on four Uzbek language-learning games. This architecture integrates UzWordnet and the largest available Uzbek orthographic dictionary as core lexical resources, and it embeds generative AI as a fundamental learning support component. The system leverages game mechanics to both teach Uzbek and to collect data that can be used to refine and enrich UzWordnet. The paper proposes a game-based methodology in which user interactions and game outcomes provide signals for improving lexical entries and relations in UzWordnet.
AI.result: The paper presents the design of four educational games and a unified architecture that connects them with UzWordnet, the Uzbek orthographic dictionary, and generative AI services. Through this architecture, gameplay not only supports learners in practicing Uzbek vocabulary and language skills but also yields feedback and data that can be used to improve UzWordnet. The main outcome is a demonstration of how educational games can simultaneously serve as a language learning tool and as a mechanism for lexical resource development.
AI.conclusion: By combining game design, rich lexical resources (UzWordnet and an orthographic dictionary), and generative AI, the proposed architecture achieves dual goals: it facilitates engaging Uzbek language learning and contributes to the continuous improvement and enrichment of UzWordnet. The paper concludes that game-based learning environments can be effectively leveraged not only for pedagogy but also as a sustainable, user-driven methodology for maintaining and expanding lexical databases.

title: IMEX Interaction-Based Model Explanation
summary: In predictive modeling, the ability to explain why a model produces a given target prediction has become increasingly important [5, 10]. Black-box models do not provide a transparent description of the internal mechanisms that generate the prediction, making even accurate predictions difficult to interpret and validate. In critical contexts, predictive accuracy alone is not a sufficient validation metric if the reasons underlying model decisions remain unexplained. The IMEX (Interaction-Based Model Explanation) approach represents a methodological direction within explainable predictive modeling. IMEX is designed to identify which variables contribute most to the target prediction and which interactions among variables are significant in determining the target. The method does not impose limitations on higher-order interaction analysis, allowing the investigation of feature subsets with cardinality greater than two. Beyond the identification of feature importance, IMEX enables the exploration of interaction patterns that may be consistent with latent mechanisms influencing the outcome. Through the application of the IMEX algorithm, it is possible to construct an interpretability map of the predictions. The IMEX framework is built on two complementary metrics: Static Correlation Power (PCS), which quantifies the contribution of individual features, and Interaction Correlation Power (PCI), which captures non-additive effects among features. In the present work, the PCS component is experimentally validated through a comparison with INVASE [18] on three synthetic datasets with known structures. The results indicate that IMEX can recover relevant feature-level structures in the presence of non-linear, conditional, and multicollinear relationships between input features and prediction targets.

AI.tldr: A method (IMEX) for explaining black-box predictive models by quantifying feature importance and interactions, validated on synthetic data.
AI.motivation: Predictive models, especially black-box ones, lack transparency and interpretability. In many critical applications, knowing only that a model is accurate is insufficient; users must understand why a specific prediction is made and which variables and interactions drive it. Existing methods often struggle with complex, non-linear, or higher-order interactions. This work aims to provide a systematic way to explain model predictions at the feature and interaction level.
AI.method: The paper proposes IMEX (Interaction-Based Model Explanation), which decomposes model explanations into two metrics: Static Correlation Power (PCS) for individual feature contributions and Interaction Correlation Power (PCI) for non-additive feature interactions. IMEX can analyze higher-order interactions (feature subsets of size greater than two) and builds an interpretability map over predictions. The study focuses on experimentally validating the PCS component by comparing it against the INVASE method on synthetic datasets with known ground-truth structures.
AI.result: On three synthetic datasets with known generative structures, IMEX (specifically the PCS component) successfully identifies the relevant features and their roles, even when relationships between inputs and targets are non-linear, conditional, or multicollinear. It performs competitively with or better than INVASE in recovering the true feature-level structure.
AI.conclusion: IMEX provides a promising framework for explaining black-box predictive models by separating individual feature effects from interaction effects and allowing higher-order interaction analysis. The experimental validation of PCS shows that IMEX can reliably recover important features under complex dependency structures, suggesting its usefulness for building interpretable explanations and maps of model predictions.

title: Automatically Evolving Prompt Guidelines for Task-Specific Optimization
summary: For Large Language Models to reliably answer user queries, users must clearly specify requirements, context, and constraints. In practice, however, user queries are often underspecified, forcing models to infer unstated assumptions that may misalign with the actual user intent. Existing prompt engineering guidelines aim to mitigate this issue, they are typically generic and task-agnostic, limiting their practical utility. Additionally, existing guidelines are formed manually and in a non-systematic way. To this end, we study prompt guideline optimization: the problem of automatically generating task-specific guidelines that help write better-specified prompts for a given task and model. Our key observation is that existing (completed) task examples (aka reference answers) often implicitly encode the missing information required to complete underspecified queries, including behavioral constraints, contextual assumptions, and evaluation criteria. We therefore propose AGOPS, an automatic approach that evolves task-specific guidelines via an optimization scheme that involves a prompt LLM writer, a solver LLM and prompt evolution, which maximize downstream effectiveness on a set of examples (user queries with reference answers). At inference time, our guidelines help users write well-specified prompts, boosting the effectiveness of LLMs. We show across mathematical reasoning, medical question answering, and coding tasks, that prompt underspecification leads to major drops (up to 95.3%) in downstream task performance (compared to well-specified prompts) and, perhaps more importantly, that this drop can hardly be recovered by existing prompt optimization techniques. Users following AGOPS guidelines can regain this loss (increasing performance between 15.5 to 81.7% on average) consistently across all benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AGOPS, an automatic method to generate task-specific prompt-writing guidelines from existing solved examples, which helps users write better-specified prompts and significantly improves LLM performance under underspecified user queries.
AI.motivation: LLMs often receive underspecified user queries lacking clear requirements, context, and constraints, forcing models to guess user intent and causing large performance drops. Existing prompt-engineering guidelines are generic, task-agnostic, and manually created, making them less effective and hard to scale. The authors want a systematic, automatic way to derive task- and model-specific guidelines that help users formulate well-specified prompts and recover lost performance in realistic settings.
AI.method: They formalize "prompt guideline optimization," whose goal is to automatically generate task-specific guidelines that improve LLM performance. Their method, AGOPS, treats this as an optimization problem and leverages (1) existing task examples consisting of user queries with reference answers, and (2) multiple LLM roles: a prompt-writer LLM, a solver LLM, and an evolution process that iteratively refines guidelines. The system infers implicit information from reference answers (behavioral constraints, contextual assumptions, evaluation criteria) and evolves guidelines that, when followed by users to rewrite prompts, maximize downstream task performance for a specific task and model.
AI.result: Empirically, the authors evaluate on mathematical reasoning, medical QA, and coding benchmarks. They show that prompt underspecification can cause drastic performance degradation (up to 95.3% relative drop compared to well-specified prompts) and that popular prompt-optimization methods cannot recover this loss. In contrast, when users follow AGOPS-generated guidelines to write more specific prompts, model performance improves substantially, recovering much of the lost performance, with average gains between 15.5% and 81.7% across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Prompt underspecification is a major, underappreciated source of LLM performance failures that generic prompt tips and existing prompt optimization cannot adequately fix. Automatically learned, task-specific prompt-writing guidelines derived from existing solved examples can guide users to craft well-specified prompts, significantly boosting downstream performance. AGOPS demonstrates that prompt guideline optimization is a viable, effective, and general approach for aligning user queries with LLM capabilities across diverse tasks.

title: RegNetAgents: A Multi-Agent Framework for Cross-Network Regulatory Driver Identification in Cancer Genomics
summary: We introduce RegNetAgents, an AI-oriented multi-agent framework for structured, query-driven regulatory candidate identification across heterogeneous gene regulatory networks. The system enables unified analysis of bulk tumor and single-cell-derived ARACNe networks by integrating TCGA-derived cancer networks with large-scale single-cell regulatory networks from the GREmLN project. For a given focal gene, the framework performs dual-network classification, cancer gene filtering using OncoKB annotations, and mode-of-action (MoA) assignment for tumor-derived regulatory relationships. Candidates are ranked by evidence consistency across networks (Both, TCGA-only, GREmLN-only). The system is implemented as a multi-agent LangGraph DAG workflow, accessible through a unified Python API and Model Context Protocol (MCP) client, operating as a downstream analytical layer over precomputed regulatory networks rather than a network inference method. Across eleven breast cancer (BRCA) and twelve colorectal cancer (COAD) focal genes, RegNetAgents identifies candidate regulators significantly enriched for OncoKB-annotated cancer genes. TCGA-derived candidates show strong enrichment (Stouffer Z = 6.69 for BRCA and 6.95 for COAD), while GREmLN-derived candidates also demonstrate significant enrichment (Z = 5.51 for BRCA and 7.06 for COAD; all p < 0.0001). No enrichment is observed in housekeeping or non-driver control gene sets, supporting signal specificity. An extended module enables structured evaluation of oncogenic potential, druggability, clinical relevance, and network vulnerability, supporting end-to-end interpretation from candidate identification to biological hypothesis generation. RegNetAgents establishes an interpretable AI framework for cross-network regulatory candidate identification in cancer genomics.

AI.tldr: RegNetAgents is an interpretable AI multi-agent framework to systematically find and prioritize regulatory gene candidates across bulk tumor and single-cell gene regulatory networks, showing strong enrichment for known cancer genes.
AI.motivation: Gene regulatory networks derived from bulk and single-cell data are powerful but heterogeneous, making it difficult to systematically and interpretabily identify cancer-relevant regulators across datasets. There is a need for an AI-driven, structured, cross-network analysis framework that can unify these resources, prioritize candidates, and support downstream biological interpretation rather than re-infer networks.
AI.method: The authors build RegNetAgents, a multi-agent system implemented as a LangGraph DAG workflow with a Python API and MCP client. It operates over precomputed ARACNe regulatory networks from TCGA bulk tumors and GREmLN single-cell data. For a user-specified focal gene, the system classifies regulators across both networks, filters candidates using OncoKB cancer annotations, assigns mode-of-action for tumor relationships, and ranks candidates by evidence consistency across networks (present in both, TCGA only, or GREmLN only). An additional module integrates structured evidence on oncogenic potential, druggability, clinical relevance, and network vulnerability.
AI.result: Applied to 11 breast cancer and 12 colorectal cancer focal genes, RegNetAgents identifies candidate regulators that are highly enriched for OncoKB cancer genes. TCGA-derived candidates for BRCA and COAD show strong enrichment with Stouffer Z-scores of 6.69 and 6.95; GREmLN-derived candidates also show significant enrichment with Z-scores of 5.51 and 7.06, all with p < 0.0001. No enrichment is seen when using housekeeping or non-driver control genes, indicating that the signal is specific to cancer-related regulators.
AI.conclusion: RegNetAgents provides an interpretable, AI-driven multi-agent framework that unifies bulk and single-cell regulatory networks to systematically identify and prioritize cancer-relevant regulatory gene candidates. By focusing on structured, cross-network analysis with strong enrichment for known cancer genes and modules for assessing oncogenicity and druggability, it supports an end-to-end workflow from candidate discovery to hypothesis generation in cancer genomics.

title: Token Time Continuous Diffusion for Language Modeling
summary: In this paper we introduce token time continuous diffusion (TTCD), a new diffusion language model which (a) operates in continuous space, deterministically mapping Gaussian noise to a final token canvas with no further sampling, and crucially (b) incorporates a new notion of per-token times, with some tokens proceeding from noise to token at a faster rate than others. Continuous space modeling helps TTCD avoid the parallel sampling of multiple tokens, which is a key source of inaccuracy at high speedups for models that iterate purely in discrete space. The notion of per-token times helps TTCD to better model conditional generation, allows for more sure tokens to proceed at a faster rate, and allows for differentiated inter-token influences during refinement. TTCD outperforms discrete models at high speedups. We train a 160M parameter TTCD model on OpenWebText, and then self-distill it; we find that at high speedups we are comparable in unconditional generation quality, and outperform in conditional generation, several existing models of similar size trained, on the same data, and self-distilled. We achieve similar gains in Sudoku solving as well.

AI.tldr: Proposes Token Time Continuous Diffusion (TTCD), a continuous-space diffusion language model with per-token time dynamics, achieving better quality at high sampling speedups than comparable discrete models on language and Sudoku tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion language models typically operate in discrete token space and rely on parallel sampling of multiple tokens. At high speedups (i.e., few refinement steps), this parallel discrete sampling becomes a major source of inaccuracy, especially for conditional generation. The authors seek a method that remains accurate and controllable when sampling very quickly, better handles conditional generation, and allows different tokens to evolve at different rates based on their certainty and influence on others.
AI.method: Introduce Token Time Continuous Diffusion (TTCD), which defines a deterministic mapping from initial Gaussian noise in a continuous space to a final token canvas, eliminating stochastic sampling during refinement. TTCD assigns each token its own continuous time variable, letting some tokens transition from noise to discrete tokens faster than others. This per-token time scheduling enables more confident tokens to finalize earlier and shapes how tokens influence one another during continuous refinement. The model is trained on OpenWebText; a 160M-parameter TTCD is then self-distilled to improve efficiency and performance, and evaluated on unconditional and conditional language generation and Sudoku solving, against baselines that operate in discrete space with parallel token sampling.
AI.result: At high sampling speedups (few refinement steps), TTCD surpasses comparable discrete diffusion or iterative language models of similar size, trained and self-distilled on the same OpenWebText data, particularly for conditional generation. In unconditional generation, TTCD achieves quality comparable to these baselines at high speedups. The approach also yields similar improvements in structured reasoning tasks, demonstrated on Sudoku solving benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Modeling language with a continuous diffusion process over token representations, combined with per-token time dynamics, mitigates the accuracy degradation seen in discrete, parallel token sampling at high speedups. TTCD provides a deterministic, noise-to-text mapping that better supports fast conditional generation and structured reasoning, matching or exceeding discrete baselines of similar scale. This suggests that continuous-time, per-token schedules are a promising direction for efficient, high-quality diffusion-based language modeling.

title: DialogueVPR: Towards Conversational Visual Place Recognition
summary: Inspired by how humans communicate spatial information, language-guided geo-localization has gained significant traction for its intuitive and practical value. Despite this progress, most methods still rely on a static, one-shot retrieval paradigm, which fails to handle the ambiguity and incompleteness inherent in real-world natural language descriptions. We propose a paradigm shift to reasoning retrieval and introduce Dialogue Place Recognition (DlgPR), which casts localization as an interactive, dialogue-driven reasoning process. To support this new task, we present DlgQuest-Cities, the first large-scale dialogue-based benchmark for place recognition, and a unified reasoning framework that couples a cross-modal multi-level retriever with an intelligent questioner, DQ-pilot. DQ-pilot is trained in a curriculum: supervised fine-tuning on a curated DQ-cities-20k subset followed by reinforcement refinement on a harder DQ-cities-10k split via GRPO. Two task-aligned metrics guide learning: a Discriminative Difficulty Index (DDI) for curriculum sampling and a Positional Retrieval Gain (PRG) reward that directly measures retrieval improvement induced by a question. Experiments show this reasoning-based approach significantly outperforms baselines. The code and model are available at https://github.com/Graysonggg/DlgPR.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Dialogue Place Recognition (DlgPR), a new dialogue-based, reasoning-driven paradigm for language-guided geo-localization that iteratively asks questions to disambiguate spatial descriptions and retrieve the correct place more accurately than static one-shot methods.
AI.motivation: Existing language-guided geo-localization methods treat localization as a static one-shot retrieval problem, which struggles with ambiguous, incomplete, or noisy natural language descriptions common in real-world scenarios. Human communication about location, however, is interactive and incremental: people clarify, ask follow-up questions, and reason over multiple cues. The authors are motivated to bridge this gap by turning place recognition into a dialogue-driven reasoning process that can actively resolve ambiguity and improve retrieval performance.
AI.method: The authors propose Dialogue Place Recognition (DlgPR), formulating localization as an interactive dialogue between a questioner and a retriever. They build DlgQuest-Cities, a large-scale dialogue-based benchmark for place recognition. Methodologically, they design a unified reasoning framework that couples: (1) a cross-modal multi-level retriever that matches language and visual/geographic data at multiple levels, and (2) an intelligent questioner agent called DQ-pilot. DQ-pilot is trained in a curriculum: first via supervised fine-tuning on a curated DQ-cities-20k subset to learn basic questioning behavior, then via reinforcement learning (GRPO) on a more challenging DQ-cities-10k split to refine its strategy. Two task-aligned metrics are introduced: a Discriminative Difficulty Index (DDI) used to select and schedule training examples by hardness, and a Positional Retrieval Gain (PRG) reward that quantifies how much each question improves the rank of the correct location in the retrieval list, which directly guides RL optimization.
AI.result: Experiments on the new DlgQuest-Cities benchmark show that the proposed reasoning-based DlgPR framework substantially outperforms traditional one-shot retrieval baselines and other comparison methods in language-guided place recognition. The integration of DQ-pilot, curriculum training, and the PRG-based reinforcement refinement leads to larger improvements, especially on more ambiguous or difficult cases, demonstrating that interactive questioning significantly enhances geo-localization accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that casting language-guided geo-localization as an interactive, dialogue-driven reasoning task is more aligned with how humans communicate and is substantially more effective than static one-shot retrieval. The proposed DlgPR paradigm, supported by the DlgQuest-Cities benchmark, the cross-modal multi-level retriever, and the DQ-pilot questioner trained with DDI and PRG, yields superior performance. This establishes dialogue-based reasoning retrieval as a promising direction for robust, real-world place recognition and provides resources and code to facilitate further research in this area.

title: Polestar: Drift-Aware Cache Calibration and Token Commitment for Efficient Inference of Diffusion LLMs
summary: The inference efficiency of diffusion large language models (dLLMs) is constrained by two challenges: bidirectional attention precludes efficient KV-cache reuse, while increasing decoding parallelism with static confidence thresholds can compromise generation quality. We observe that both challenges arise from a shared phenomenon: as tokens are decoded, their contextual integration through bidirectional attention causes token representations to drift (evolve) across decoding steps. This insight motivates Polestar, a training-free inference framework that uses token representation drift as a unified signal to jointly address both challenges. Polestar comprises two components: Polestar-Cache, which identifies stale KV-cache positions via drift and performs sparse KV-cache refreshes to enable efficient reuse, and Polestar-Commit, which detects sharp drift events to reliably identify commit-ready tokens. Across mathematics and coding benchmarks on several dLLM families, Polestar sets a new state of the art on the accuracy-throughput Pareto frontier, achieving up to 10.73% accuracy improvement, up to 3.7x higher throughput, and high decoding parallelism of 3.67 tokens per forward pass over existing baselines.

AI.tldr: Polestar is a training-free inference framework for diffusion large language models that uses token representation drift to both enable efficient KV-cache reuse and safe speculative (parallel) decoding, achieving better accuracy–throughput trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Diffusion LLMs suffer from inefficient inference: (1) their bidirectional attention prevents straightforward KV-cache reuse across steps, increasing computation and memory cost; and (2) existing methods that parallelize decoding typically rely on static confidence thresholds, which can harm generation quality. The authors notice that as decoding proceeds, token representations change due to repeated contextual integration, and this drift pattern might be exploitable to solve both problems simultaneously.
AI.method: The authors introduce Polestar, a training-free framework that monitors the drift of token representations across diffusion decoding steps. Polestar-Cache uses the magnitude and pattern of representation drift to detect which KV-cache entries have become stale and selectively refreshes only those, enabling sparse but effective KV-cache updates. Polestar-Commit tracks sharp drift events to decide when tokens have effectively converged and can be committed as final, supporting speculative-style parallel decoding without static confidence thresholds. Both components are applied during inference only, requiring no model retraining.
AI.result: On mathematics and coding benchmarks across multiple diffusion LLM families, Polestar improves the accuracy–throughput Pareto frontier relative to existing inference baselines. It yields up to 10.73% higher accuracy, up to 3.7× higher throughput, and achieves an effective decoding parallelism of 3.67 tokens per forward pass, demonstrating that drift-based cache management and commit decisions can simultaneously speed up inference and preserve or improve generation quality.
AI.conclusion: Token representation drift is a powerful unified signal for optimizing diffusion LLM inference. By using drift to govern both sparse KV-cache refresh and commit decisions for parallel decoding, Polestar overcomes core efficiency limitations of diffusion LLMs without additional training. This leads to state-of-the-art trade-offs between accuracy and throughput on math and coding tasks, suggesting that representation-dynamics-aware inference is a promising direction for future large model systems.

title: Interpretable Language Model for Closed-Loop Type 1 Diabetes Control
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE 22nd International Conference on Automation Science and Engineering conference (IEEE CASE 2026)
summary: Type 1 Diabetes (T1D) is a chronic, life-threatening autoimmune condition characterized by the complete destruction of insulin-producing pancreatic beta cells. While Artificial Pancreas Systems (APS) powered by Reinforcement Learning (RL) have shown promise in automating insulin delivery, their ``black-box'' nature makes it hard for patients and doctors to trust them fully. This paper presents LLM-T1D, a promising approach that combines the precision of RL with the clear, human-like reasoning of Large Language Models (LLMs) to create a more transparent and reliable insulin pump controller. By training an expert RL system and distilling its knowledge into fine-tuned LLaMA 3.1 8B and Qwen3 8B models, we developed a controller that not only surpasses the RL system's performance but also explains its decisions in plain, understandable language. Tested on the FDA-approved UVA/Padova T1D simulator, the LLM controllers deliver excellent blood sugar control (73.5% Time in Range) while maintaining strict formal safety verification against hallucinations.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes LLM-T1D, an insulin pump controller that distills a high-performing RL-based artificial pancreas into compact LLMs (LLaMA 3.1 8B and Qwen3 8B) to achieve both strong glucose control and human-readable explanations, validated on the UVA/Padova T1D simulator.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement-learning-based Artificial Pancreas Systems can automate insulin delivery but act as black boxes, making it difficult for patients and clinicians to understand, trust, and clinically adopt them. The authors want a controller that keeps RL’s performance while adding transparency, explainability, and safety guarantees for real-world medical use.
AI.method: 1) Train an expert RL controller for T1D insulin dosing on the UVA/Padova simulator. 2) Collect trajectories and associated decision rationales from the expert. 3) Distill this behavior into fine-tuned 8B-parameter LLaMA 3.1 and Qwen3 models so that, given the same state information, the LLM outputs insulin dosing actions along with natural-language explanations. 4) Enforce formal safety constraints and verification mechanisms to prevent harmful or hallucinated recommendations. 5) Evaluate the LLM-based controllers in simulation on glucose control quality and safety.
AI.result: The fine-tuned LLaMA 3.1 8B and Qwen3 8B controllers match or surpass the original RL expert’s performance in the UVA/Padova T1D simulator, achieving around 73.5% Time in Range for blood glucose. They also consistently provide clear, plain-language explanations for dosing decisions while operating under formal safety checks to avoid unsafe or hallucinatory outputs.
AI.conclusion: Distilling an expert RL artificial pancreas into compact LLM controllers can yield a T1D insulin pump that is both high-performing and interpretable. LLM-T1D demonstrates that LLMs can serve as transparent, safely verified control policies in a safety-critical medical setting, potentially improving trust and adoption of automated insulin delivery systems.

title: Eta Given Delta: Defining LLM Tool Efficiency With Marginal Tool Utility
summary: This paper introduces tool efficiency, a new quantitative metric to evaluate the rate of useful tool calls in an LLM agent trajectory. To ensure that tool efficiency is well-defined, we also introduce marginal tool utility, a new quantitative metric defined per tool call indicating whether a tool is useful or whether it can be safely removed from the tool suite without affecting accuracy while increasing tool efficiency; in this paper, we determine the sign of marginal tool utility for each tool call in a trajectory using LLM-as-a-Judge. While much prior work has been done to develop techniques that improve tool use by LLMs and design evaluation methods measuring efficiency indirectly using accuracy as a proxy, our work is centered on measuring efficiency directly via the quantitative metric proposed in this paper in post hoc trajectory analyses. It is our intention that this work contributes to the frontier of LLM evaluation research as a springboard for future benchmark designs and agent harness engineering (specifically with regards to creating lean tool suites) that optimize for metrics that complement but are distinct from accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes new metrics to directly measure how efficiently LLM agents use tools, beyond just task accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of LLM tool use largely focus on accuracy or use accuracy as a proxy for efficiency, leaving a gap in directly measuring whether tool calls are necessary or wasteful. The authors want a principled way to quantify and analyze how many tool calls are actually useful, and which tools could be removed without hurting performance.
AI.method: They define two quantitative metrics: (1) tool efficiency, which measures the proportion or rate of useful tool calls in an LLM agent's trajectory, and (2) marginal tool utility, a per-call measure indicating whether a given tool invocation contributes to task success or could be removed without accuracy loss. To label each tool call’s marginal utility, they use an LLM-as-a-judge setup in post hoc analysis of trajectories.
AI.result: They operationalize these metrics and show that it is possible to identify which tool calls are useful vs. redundant in existing agent trajectories, and hence to assess the efficiency of tool use directly rather than via accuracy. They also demonstrate that their framework can guide the pruning of tool suites and refinement of agent harnesses.
AI.conclusion: Direct efficiency metrics such as tool efficiency and marginal tool utility provide a more nuanced view of LLM tool use than accuracy alone. These metrics can support future benchmark design and engineering of leaner, more efficient tool suites and agent frameworks, complementing traditional accuracy-focused evaluations.

title: Human AI Construction of Bayesian Networks for Operational Decision Support -- A Virtual Survey Approach
summary: Bayesian Belief Networks (BBNs) are powerful tools for decision-making under uncertainty. However, building their structures and estimating parameters are difficult. Currently, researchers must choose between relying on expert judgement or using large datasets to learn the structure and parameters of the network. We propose a new methodology using Large Language Models to bridge the gap between expert opinion and data-driven learning. This approach uses a panel of AI agents to estimate probabilities based on specific personas and context. We then apply a trimmed-mean rule to remove noise from these responses. We develop a six step BBN framework and illustrate it to model customer intention to consult a doctor in an alternative healthcare system. The model reveals that while self efficacy appears to be a major factor, its actual causal impact is small. In contrast, subjective norms have a much stronger effect in modelling customers' intention. The most effective strategy is to improve both confidence and community norms simultaneously.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework that uses large language models as a panel of AI agents to elicit probabilities and structure for Bayesian Belief Networks, combining expert-like judgement with data-driven learning, and demonstrates it on healthcare-seeking intentions.
AI.motivation: Building and parameterizing Bayesian Belief Networks is challenging because it either requires extensive expert input or large datasets, both of which can be costly or impractical. There is a need for a scalable way to approximate expert judgement and integrate it with data-driven learning, especially in domains like healthcare decision-making where data may be sparse and expert consensus is hard to obtain.
AI.method: The authors use Large Language Models configured as multiple AI agents with distinct personas and contextual prompts to elicit probabilistic assessments for BBN nodes and relationships. They aggregate these probability estimates using a trimmed-mean rule to reduce noise and outliers. On top of this, they define a six-step framework for constructing BBNs, and apply it to a case study modelling consumers' intention to consult a doctor in an alternative healthcare system.
AI.result: In the case study BBN, self-efficacy initially appears to be an important factor but its causal contribution to the intention to consult a doctor is actually small. Subjective norms, representing perceived social pressures or community expectations, exert a much stronger causal influence. Scenario analysis suggests that interventions that jointly increase individuals’ confidence (self-efficacy) and supportive community norms have the greatest effect on intention.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can act as scalable, configurable proxies for expert panels to support both structure learning and parameter estimation in Bayesian Belief Networks. The proposed six-step framework and trimmed-mean aggregation make it possible to integrate diverse AI-generated judgements into a coherent BBN. The healthcare case shows that such a model can yield non-obvious insights, such as the relatively small causal role of self-efficacy compared with subjective norms, and can guide more effective intervention strategies that target both individual confidence and social environment.

title: Simplicity Paradox: Debunking myths about prompting and datasets for LLM evaluation
summary: Probing the capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) and building robust solutions for Multiple-Choice Question Answering (MCQA) remain central challenges in natural language understanding. Furthermore, the rapid proliferation of LLMs has created the implicit assumption that more sophisticated prompting techniques yield better performance. Several studies claim better performance with more sophisticated prompting techniques, but do not provide a comprehensive evaluation. We address this gap through a comprehensive empirical study of 8 prompting techniques across 10 multiple-choice question answering (MCQA) datasets, encompassing 27 model configurations and roughly 4,300 unique questions evaluated more than 430,000 times. Our findings reveal a striking paradox that baseline prompting consistently outperforms complex reasoning techniques on various benchmarks. Only minimal expert and inductive role framing (CoT-Expert and CoT-Inductive) yields a small but statistically significant $\sim$3 percentage-point (pp) gain over baseline whereas every other elaborate technique we tested matches or under-performs it, often by large margins (up to 31~pp for Self-Analogical). We further investigate three critical phenomena: (1) the unexpected victory of Qwen3-30B-A3B-Thinking-2507 in Elo ratings, (2) the performance-efficiency trade-offs across model variants with different thinking budgets, revealing model-dependent optimal configurations, and (3) the substantial variation in dataset difficulty, with 60% of benchmarks below 70% accuracy and a 47.5~pp spread from easiest to hardest, indicating considerable room for model improvement. These results suggest that the LLM evaluation community may be overcomplicating prompt engineering and that substantial performance gaps remain across diverse benchmarks, offering opportunities for genuine model improvements rather than prompt optimization.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically compares 8 prompting techniques for LLM-based multiple-choice QA and finds that simple baseline prompts usually outperform complex reasoning prompts, with only small gains from light expert/inductive CoT, and large room for true model improvement.
AI.motivation: There is a common but weakly tested belief that more elaborate prompting strategies always improve LLM performance on tasks like MCQA. Existing work often claims gains from sophisticated prompts without broad, systematic evaluation. The authors want to rigorously test this assumption across many models, prompts, and datasets, and to understand where performance actually comes from.
AI.method: The authors run a large-scale empirical study: 8 different prompting strategies are evaluated on 10 MCQA datasets using 27 model configurations, including models with different "thinking budgets" and variants like Qwen3-30B-A3B-Thinking-2507. They answer about 4,300 unique questions over 430,000+ model evaluations. They compute accuracy, Elo-style ratings, and analyze performance-efficiency tradeoffs and dataset difficulty. Techniques include baseline prompting, various chain-of-thought variants (including CoT-Expert, CoT-Inductive, Self-Analogical), and other complex reasoning prompts.
AI.result: Baseline prompting surprisingly and consistently beats most complex reasoning prompts on multiple MCQA benchmarks. Only two light role-framing CoT variants (CoT-Expert and CoT-Inductive) give a modest but statistically significant ~3 percentage-point accuracy gain over baseline. Many advanced techniques underperform, sometimes severely (e.g., Self-Analogical up to 31 percentage points worse). Qwen3-30B-A3B-Thinking-2507 achieves the highest Elo, there are clear performance–efficiency tradeoffs that depend on model and thinking budget, and dataset difficulty varies widely, with most benchmarks under 70% accuracy and a nearly 50 percentage-point spread between easiest and hardest.
AI.conclusion: Prompt engineering for MCQA with LLMs may have been overcomplicated: simple baseline prompts are often as good as or better than sophisticated reasoning prompts, with only minor gains from lightweight expert/inductive framing. There is substantial headroom on current MCQA benchmarks, implying that future progress should focus more on improving model capabilities and architectures rather than intricate prompting schemes.

title: Capability from Access Structure, Not Scale: Lower Bounds and Pre-Registered Tests for Hybrid Sequence Models
comment: 41 pages, 16 figures. Pre-registered small-scale experiments (scorecard: 11 supported, 7 partial, 1 failed). Information-theoretic lower bounds plus controlled trained witnesses. Jianlin Chen and Ziyao Lin contributed equally. Corresponding author: Chi Man Vong (cmvong@um.edu.mo)
summary: The Platonic Representation Hypothesis (PRH) holds that as models scale, representations of heterogeneous networks converge toward a shared model of reality. We propose its sequel and boundary, the Capability Convergence Hypothesis (CCH): under a fixed per-token inference budget, representational convergence does not entail capability convergence. Capability instead converges toward a class, the access-complete hybrid: any architecture holding both a compressive O(1)-state channel and a scalable verbatim-index channel. We anchor it on a witness task, the Newton's-apple problem in an infinite stream, and name three resource walls: a Shannon wall barring any o(Nb)-state architecture, a horizon wall barring any fixed window, and a circuit wall barring fixed-depth attention-only composition (conditional on TC0 != NC1). Under an explicit separability assumption a hybrid crosses all three by paying each wall's price, so capability is strictly super-additive under composition. We separate what we prove from what we conjecture: the access-completeness principle rests on information-theoretic lower bounds and pre-registered experiments, while the field-level convergence trend is an economics-motivated conjecture. We report the first pre-registered small-scale tests under criteria frozen before the data: the predicted scissors gap is measured (exact-retrieval error 0.994 vs. 0.000 once a 64-scalar state gains one global-attention layer), the state-tracking bifurcation lands at the registered boundary, and a conjunction witness shows an irreducibly two-channel solution; one prediction failed with its direction reversed and is reported as such. Representational convergence is given freely by scale; capability convergence must be purchased by access structure.

AI.tldr: Paper distinguishes representation convergence from capability convergence in large models, arguing that shared internal world models don’t guarantee equal practical abilities under fixed compute; capabilities converge only for architectures that combine tiny compressed state with scalable exact recall.
AI.motivation: To clarify why scaling models leads to similar internal representations but not necessarily similar task performance, and to formalize which architectural features are needed for capabilities to converge under inference-time constraints.
AI.method: Theoretical construction of the Capability Convergence Hypothesis, identification of resource “walls” via information-theoretic and computational complexity arguments, definition of an “access-complete hybrid” architecture, and pre-registered small-scale experiments on a toy “Newton’s-apple” streaming task to empirically probe predictions.
AI.result: They argue that any architecture with both a constant-size compressive state and a scalable exact-index memory channel can asymptotically overcome three fundamental resource walls (Shannon, horizon, circuit), making its capability strictly more than the sum of its parts; preliminary experiments show a large empirical gap between architectures that have and lack such hybrid access, mostly matching but partly contradicting preregistered predictions.
AI.conclusion: Shared representations arise naturally with scale, but comparable capabilities require specific access structures that combine compressed state with scalable exact recall; capabilities are therefore architecture-sensitive and super-additive under the right composition, rather than guaranteed by representation quality alone.

title: MAPS: Modeling Co-Existing Subjective Perspectives and Shared Meaning in Multi-Agent Cognitive Dialogue
summary: Human dialogue involves more than exchanging information; it also expresses beliefs, emotions, and subjective cognitive styles. Yet current AI dialogue systems often enforce semantic uniformity, sacrificing diversity and interpretability. We present MAPS (Multi-Agent Perspective Spaces), a novel framework that models dialogue between cognitively distinct agents through domain-weighted profiles, dynamic GRU-based memory, and interpretable token-level attention. MAPS enables agents to maintain individualized reasoning while progressively converging on shared meaning. Evaluations on EmpatheticDialogues, TopicalChat, and MultiWOZ show that MAPS supports semantic alignment without collapsing subjectivity. Our results demonstrate a path toward cognitively grounded, interpretable dialogue systems that balance expressiveness and coherence.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MAPS, a multi-agent dialogue framework that preserves distinct cognitive perspectives while enabling agents to gradually align on shared meaning, using domain-weighted profiles, GRU-based memory, and interpretable attention, and validates it on several dialogue benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Human conversations are not just about factual information; they also reflect beliefs, emotions, and individual cognitive styles. Existing AI dialogue systems typically push all dialogue into a single, uniform semantic space, which erases this subjectivity and makes reasoning processes opaque. The authors are motivated to design a system that can model cognitively distinct agents who retain their own styles and mental states while still being able to communicate effectively and align on meaning, with interpretability into how this alignment happens.
AI.method: They introduce MAPS (Multi-Agent Perspective Spaces), a framework where each agent has a domain-weighted profile that captures its cognitive and affective characteristics across topics or domains. Dialogue history is encoded with a dynamic GRU-based memory to track evolving states. An interpretable, token-level attention mechanism reveals how agents attend to parts of the dialogue and to each other’s utterances while negotiating meaning. The system is instantiated and tested on standard dialogue datasets by simulating multi-agent conversations with different profiles and tracking how they converge semantically over turns.
AI.result: Across EmpatheticDialogues, TopicalChat, and MultiWOZ, MAPS is shown to preserve diversity in agents’ responses and subjective styles while still achieving semantic alignment and task coherence. Quantitative metrics and qualitative analyses suggest that it avoids the usual collapse into a single neutral voice, and attention visualizations indicate more transparent, cognitively grounded reasoning patterns compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that MAPS provides a viable approach to building dialogue systems that reflect heterogeneous cognitive perspectives without sacrificing mutual understanding. By combining profile-based agent modeling, recurrent memory, and interpretable attention, the framework balances expressiveness with coherence and offers a more cognitively grounded, transparent basis for future multi-agent and human–AI dialogue systems.

title: ToolAnchor: Anchoring Counterfactual Context to Boost Agentic Tool-use Capability
summary: Tool-augmented large language model agents excel at long-horizon tasks, yet they are typically post-trained on fixed toolsets. When tasks demand new tools, these agents struggle to incorporate them effectively, and retraining from scratch is often impractical. We identify the core obstacle in such toolset expansion problem as behavioral inertia: the tendency of agents to fall back on familiar tools and established reasoning patterns despite having access to new ones. We demonstrate that injecting counterfactual anchor contexts at critical decision points can break this inertia, recovering failed trajectories by eliciting suppressed agent capabilities. To scale this insight, we propose ToolAnchor, a framework that uses teacher models to hypothesize these counterfactual contexts, verifies them via student rollouts, and internalizes the successful interventions through agentic post-training. Extensive evaluations across general AI assistant (GAIA), textual search (BrowseComp), and visual search (VDR-Bench) tasks demonstrate that ToolAnchor consistently exhibits competitive performance under expanded toolsets. Our work bridges the gap between static post-training and dynamic adaptation, charting a new path for scalable agentic reinforcement learning.

AI.tldr: They propose ToolAnchor, a method to help tool-using LLM agents effectively adapt to newly added tools by breaking their behavioral inertia, improving performance on long-horizon tasks under expanding toolsets.
AI.motivation: Existing tool-augmented LLM agents are post-trained on a fixed set of tools, so when new tools are introduced, the agents tend to ignore them and keep relying on familiar tools. Retraining from scratch for every tool change is expensive and impractical. The authors see this as a behavioral inertia problem and aim to find a scalable way to help agents dynamically adapt to new tools and recover from failure trajectories.
AI.method: They introduce ToolAnchor, a framework that: (1) identifies critical decision points where the agent gets stuck in old behaviors; (2) injects "counterfactual anchor contexts"—alternative prompts or guidance hypothesized by stronger teacher models—showing how the agent could instead use the new tools; (3) verifies these hypothesized interventions by running student rollouts to see if they actually improve trajectories; and (4) performs agentic post-training so the student model internalizes the successful interventions, reducing future inertia without full retraining.
AI.result: Through experiments on GAIA (general AI assistant tasks), BrowseComp (textual search), and VDR-Bench (visual search), ToolAnchor achieves consistently strong and competitive performance when the toolset is expanded. It shows that injecting and distilling counterfactual anchor contexts can help agents make better use of new tools in long-horizon settings.
