AI.conclusion: Behavioral inertia is a key barrier to enabling tool-augmented LLM agents to adapt to new tools. By using teacher-generated counterfactual anchor contexts, validated via student rollouts and distilled through post-training, ToolAnchor allows agents to break this inertia and dynamically integrate new tools. This provides a scalable direction for agentic reinforcement learning that moves beyond static post-training toward continual, tool-aware adaptation.

title: Introspection Fine-Tuning (IFT): Training Small LLMs to Introspect
summary: Can small language models detect and report on perturbations their own internal activations? We investigate this question through the lens of activation steering: injecting concept vectors into a model's residual stream and measuring whether the model can accurately report on the perturbation. We first show that the binary detection paradigm used in prior work -- prompting the model to answer Yes'' or No'' to whether it detects an injected thought -- is confounded in small models, as steering biases the model toward affirmative responses regardless of the question content. We therefore propose two confound-free evaluation paradigms: sentence localization (identifying which of $N$ sentences was perturbed, chance $= 1/N$) and strength comparison (identifying which of two sentences received a stronger injection, chance $= 50\%$). Evaluating across six models from two families (Llama-3.2 and Gemma-4), we find that models as small as 2B parameters introspect reliably well above chance, and that introspective ability generally increases with scale. Llama-1B, however, performs at or below chance. We then introduce \emph{Introspection Fine-Tuning} (IFT): supervised fine-tuning on sentence-localization examples constructed from the model's own perturbed forward passes. IFT raises Llama-1B sentence-localization accuracy from $9.6\%$ to $60.6\%$ (a $6\times$ improvement), with gains generalizing zero-shot to the held-out strength-comparison task ($30.2\% \to 52.2\%$). IFT also improves introspection for 3B and 8B models, while inducing negligible degradation on standard capability benchmarks. Our results suggest that introspective ability is not fixed by scale alone: it can be directly trained, and doing so unlocks latent self-monitoring capacity with implications for AI transparency and alignment. Our code is \href{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/IFT-introspection-2092/README.md}{here}.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether small language models can detect and describe perturbations to their own internal activations, proposes better evaluation setups, and introduces a fine-tuning method that significantly improves this introspective ability without hurting normal performance.
AI.motivation: Prior work on models “noticing” activation steering relied on a binary Yes/No detection setup, which turns out to be confounded in small models because steering itself pushes the model to say “Yes.” The authors want a cleaner, quantitative way to measure models’ self-monitoring of internal changes, and to test whether this ability can be systematically enhanced rather than just emerging with scale.
AI.method: 1) Analyze why the existing binary detection paradigm is confounded in small models: activation injections bias the model’s outputs toward affirmative responses. 2) Propose two new evaluation paradigms that avoid this confound: (a) sentence localization—among N candidate sentences, identify which one had a concept vector injected; (b) strength comparison—between two sentences, identify which received a stronger injection. 3) Apply activation steering to several models from two families (Llama-3.2 and Gemma-4) across parameter scales, measure performance on these tasks, and compare to chance. 4) Introduce Introspection Fine-Tuning (IFT), where models are fine-tuned on supervision derived from their own perturbed forward passes (constructed sentence-localization datasets), then test generalization to both localization and strength-comparison metrics as well as standard capability benchmarks.
AI.result: 1) Small models (around 2B parameters and above) already perform reliably above chance on both sentence localization and strength comparison, and introspective accuracy tends to improve with model size. 2) A very small Llama-1B model sits at or below chance, indicating weak baseline introspection. 3) After Introspection Fine-Tuning, Llama-1B’s sentence-localization accuracy jumps from 9.6% to 60.6%, a roughly sixfold improvement, and performance on the unseen strength-comparison task rises from 30.2% to 52.2%, indicating transfer. 4) IFT also improves introspection for 3B and 8B models. 5) Across models, IFT causes negligible drops on standard capability benchmarks, suggesting that introspective training does not significantly harm general utility.
AI.conclusion: Introspective ability—models’ capacity to detect and report on perturbations to their own activations—is not solely determined by scale or emergent properties. It can be directly trained via Introspection Fine-Tuning using self-generated, activation-perturbed examples. This training substantially unlocks latent self-monitoring capacity even in very small models, while leaving normal performance largely intact. These findings support the view that explicit introspection training can be a practical tool for improving AI transparency and alignment by enabling models to more reliably report on their internal states and interventions applied to them.

title: Enhancing Small Language Models Reasoning through Knowledge Graph Grounding
comment: Presented at the 2nd Causal Neuro-symbolic Artificial Intelligence (Causal NeSy): Toward Agentic LLMs with Neuro-Symbolic and Graph Based Reasoning Workshop @ ESWC2026
summary: Although large language models (LLMs) have set benchmarks for zero-shot reasoning, their deployment remains cost-prohibitive and environmentally taxing. Small Language Models (SLMs) offer a sustainable alternative, but prone to errors, on tasks requiring complex, multi-hop logical grounding. We investigate a neuro-symbolic agentic framework to enhance the reasoning capabilities of SLMs, specifically Gemma 3 (1B, 4B) and Llama 3.2 (3B), using the CLUTRR kinship benchmark. Our approach transforms the SLM into a minimalist agent utilizing two specialized tool calls: extract_facts for symbolic triplet extraction and get_hint for expert reasoning via a Relational Graph Convolutional Network (RGCN). We evaluate these models across two configurations, both in an Oracle scenario with ground-truth triplets and a Realistic scenario relying on self-extracted knowledge. Our results reveal that while RGCN-derived hints provide a 1.5 - 2x performance gain over story-only baselines, the system is constrained by the extraction bottleneck and sequential deductive fragility, where early extraction errors compound over multi-hop chains. Furthermore, we identify a "distraction effect" in specific architectures where noisy, self-generated facts degrade performance despite the presence of expert hints. This work characterizes the challenges of symbolic grounding in low-resource agentic systems and provides a roadmap for iterative verification in neuro-symbolic agentic pipelines.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to boost reasoning skills of small language models using a neuro-symbolic agent framework with tools for fact extraction and graph-based hints, evaluated on the CLUTRR kinship benchmark.
AI.motivation: Large LLMs are powerful but expensive and energy-intensive, while small language models are cheaper yet weak on multi-hop reasoning. The authors want a sustainable way to get strong logical reasoning from small models, understanding where neuro-symbolic, tool-augmented agents help and where they break down.
AI.method: They wrap small models (Gemma 3 1B/4B and Llama 3.2 3B) into an agent that can call two tools: one to extract symbolic triplets (extract_facts) from stories, and one to get expert reasoning hints from an RGCN trained on CLUTRR (get_hint). They test two configurations and evaluate under an Oracle setting with gold triplets and a Realistic setting where triplets come from the model’s own extraction. They analyze performance gains, error propagation, and interaction between noisy symbolic facts and RGCN hints.
AI.result: RGCN hints significantly improve performance (roughly 1.5–2x better than story-only baselines). However, performance is heavily limited by inaccuracies in the model’s fact extraction and by sequential deduction where early mistakes cascade over multi-hop reasoning chains. In some architectures, adding noisy self-generated facts actually hurts performance, even when expert hints are available (the “distraction effect”).
AI.conclusion: Neuro-symbolic agentic augmentation can substantially enhance reasoning in small language models, but its effectiveness is bottlenecked by symbolic extraction quality and fragility of stepwise deduction. The authors highlight the need for iterative verification and more robust grounding mechanisms, and they position their findings as a roadmap for improving low-resource agentic neuro-symbolic pipelines.

title: Information-Theoretic Limits of Reliability and Scaling in Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are evaluated as though perfect reliability is achievable for any task given sufficient scale. We show this assumption is information-theoretically unjustified. Every generative task has a reliability ceiling that no model can exceed, determined by how much output uncertainty is resolvable from observable context. The gap decomposes into a resolvable component closable with additional context and a subjective component inherent to task ambiguity. Autoregressive generation further degrades this ceiling at a rate governed by the task's dependency kernel, which quantifies inter-token correlations in the output. From these two primitives, we derive a first-principles scaling law where LLM performance is bottlenecked by the scarcer resource: training data or model capacity. This law recovers the Chinchilla scaling law as a special case and provides a structural account of when scaling improves reliability. Beyond scaling, our framework unifies diverse practical phenomena, such as the benefits of retrieval-augmentation and the spectral mechanics of catastrophic forgetting. Our work formalizes the resource-complexity tradeoffs that govern model performance across domains, offering a unified theory of performance limits in generative language models.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that perfect reliability in LLMs is fundamentally impossible for many generative tasks and derives an information-theoretic ceiling on performance, yielding a general scaling law and unifying several empirical phenomena.
AI.motivation: Current LLM evaluation and scaling narratives often assume that with enough data and parameters, models can achieve arbitrarily high reliability on generative tasks. This assumption lacks a principled justification and fails to explain why certain errors persist, why retrieval helps, or why catastrophic forgetting occurs. The paper aims to provide a unified, first-principles theory that explains performance limits, scaling behavior, and practical phenomena in LLMs via information-theoretic and statistical structure of tasks.
AI.method: The authors model generative tasks using information theory, defining a reliability ceiling based on how much uncertainty about the desired output can be resolved from the observable context. They decompose the irreducible gap between perfect performance and this ceiling into (1) resolvable uncertainty that could, in principle, be reduced with more context, and (2) subjective uncertainty inherent to task ambiguity. They then analyze how autoregressive generation further reduces achievable reliability using a task-specific dependency kernel capturing token interdependencies. From these primitives, they derive a general scaling law that relates performance to the limiting resource (training data vs. model capacity), showing that known laws like Chinchilla emerge as special cases. They also interpret phenomena such as retrieval-augmentation and catastrophic forgetting through the same framework.
AI.result: The paper introduces: (1) an information-theoretic notion of a reliability ceiling for generative tasks, (2) a decomposition of performance gaps into resolvable and subjective components, (3) an analysis of autoregressive degradation governed by a dependency kernel, and (4) a first-principles scaling law where performance is limited by the scarcer of data or model capacity. This scaling law recovers the Chinchilla law as a special case and explains when scaling delivers real reliability gains. The framework is further shown to account qualitatively for improvements from retrieval-augmented models and for the spectral characteristics of catastrophic forgetting.
AI.conclusion: Perfect reliability on many generative language tasks is fundamentally unattainable due to inherent information-theoretic limits. Task structure—specifically, resolvable vs. subjective uncertainty and inter-token dependencies—sets a reliability ceiling that cannot be surpassed by scaling models alone. The derived scaling law clarifies when and how additional data or capacity yields gains, subsuming existing laws like Chinchilla. By also explaining phenomena like retrieval benefits and catastrophic forgetting within a single resource-complexity framework, the work proposes a unified theory of performance limits and tradeoffs for generative language models across domains.

title: Orchestrating Power Grid Studies with Multi-Agent AI and MCP Servers
summary: This position paper explores how Agentic AI and Model Context Protocol (MCP) can support power-grid studies in a Transmission System Operator (TSO) context. We focus on integrating Large Language Models with numerical simulation tools, structured workflows, and human supervision. We identify key industrial requirements for agent assisted grid studies and introduce pypowsybl-mcp, an MCP-based interface exposing selected capabilities of our simulation tool, pypowsybl to AI agents. This first step provides a testbed to study how agents can setup simulations, execute analyses, retrieve results, and interact with power-system simulators through standardized tool calls. We also discuss principles for human-in-the-loop, multi-agent workflows and outline an evaluation strategy combining technical metrics and practitioner feedback. The paper positions MCP-based tool integration as a step toward more interactive, auditable, and scalable grid-study environments.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using Agentic AI with Model Context Protocol to connect large language models to power-grid simulation tools, presenting an initial MCP-based interface (pypowsybl-mcp) and outlining workflows, requirements, and evaluation strategies for human-in-the-loop grid studies in a TSO context.
AI.motivation: Transmission System Operators need more interactive, scalable, and auditable ways to conduct power-grid studies. Traditional tools are powerful but often complex, siloed, and not easily accessible to non-experts or cross-functional teams. With the emergence of Agentic AI and standardized tool-calling frameworks like MCP, there is an opportunity to leverage LLMs as orchestrators of simulations, analyses, and reporting while keeping humans in control. The paper aims to explore what is required, from both an industrial and technical standpoint, to safely and effectively integrate AI agents into critical power-system workflows.
AI.method: The authors take a position-paper approach: (1) they analyze industrial requirements for AI-agent-assisted grid studies in a TSO environment; (2) they design and implement pypowsybl-mcp, an MCP-based interface that exposes selected capabilities of the pypowsybl power-system simulation tool to AI agents; (3) they use this setup as a testbed to investigate how agents can set up simulations, run analyses, retrieve and interpret results, and communicate with simulators via standardized tool calls; (4) they propose principles for human-in-the-loop and multi-agent workflows; and (5) they outline an evaluation strategy that blends technical performance metrics with qualitative feedback from practitioners.
AI.result: The concrete outcome is the introduction of pypowsybl-mcp, an MCP-compatible interface that connects AI agents to the pypowsybl power-system simulator, enabling standardized tool calls for tasks like simulation setup, execution, and result retrieval. Additionally, the paper provides a structured set of industrial requirements, proposed workflow patterns (including human oversight and multi-agent setups), and an initial evaluation framework, though comprehensive empirical results are not the main focus given the position-paper nature.
AI.conclusion: MCP-based integration of Agentic AI with power-system simulation tools is a promising path toward more interactive, transparent, and scalable grid-study environments in TSO contexts. By exposing carefully selected simulator capabilities through standardized tool calls and embedding them in human-in-the-loop, multi-agent workflows, TSOs can leverage LLMs to assist with simulations and analyses while preserving traceability and control. The pypowsybl-mcp interface represents a first practical step and a testbed for further research on requirements, safety, evaluation, and real-world deployment of AI-assisted grid studies.

title: T5-CSBoost: Adversarial Perturbation Resistant LLM Fingerprinting
comment: 14 pages, 3 datasets, code and data will be provided
summary:
While many AI-generated text (AIGT) detectors achieve strong performance on clean inputs, their accuracy degrades significantly under light paraphrasing, word substitutions, character edits, and distribution shifts. We present T5 Contrastive Style Boosted Classifier (T5-CSBoost), an extension to the T5-Sentinel framework that keeps the original next-token prediction objective for source attribution while introducing an auxiliary margin-based triplet loss over decoder embeddings.
  This contrastive style regularization encourages the learning of compact, perturbation-resistant stylistic representations, offering a lightweight yet effective alternative to prior approaches that rely on architectural modifications, adversarial training, or complex multi-task objectives without altering the underlying T5-small backbone. T5-CSBoost achieves state-of-the-art multiclass source attribution and binary human-vs-LLM detection on OpenLLMText and HC3 AIGT benchmarks. More importantly, T5-CSBoost demonstrates enhanced robustness to word and character level adversarial perturbations of up to 90% intensity, achieving state-of-the-art on the challenging MAGE/Deepfake stress-test suite, including unseen models, unseen domains, and extreme paraphrasing scenarios. Our results highlight that explicitly regularizing stylistic embeddings via contrastive learning is a practical and effective strategy for building more robust LLM fingerprinting systems in real-world adversarial settings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces T5-CSBoost, a contrastive-learning-enhanced T5-based detector that significantly improves robustness of AI-generated text detection under adversarial perturbations and distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Existing AI-generated text detectors work well on clean data but their performance collapses when texts are lightly paraphrased, modified at word/character level, or come from shifted domains or unseen models. Prior robustness methods often require heavy architectural changes, adversarial training, or complex multi-task setups. The authors want a lightweight way to make LLM fingerprinting more robust and perturbation-resistant without changing the base model architecture.
AI.method: They extend the T5-Sentinel framework by keeping the original next-token prediction objective for source attribution, but they add an auxiliary margin-based triplet loss applied to decoder embeddings. This contrastive loss pulls together stylistic embeddings from similar sources and pushes apart those from different sources, regularizing them into compact, perturbation-resistant representations. The model, named T5-CSBoost, uses a T5-small backbone without architectural changes. They evaluate the approach on multiple benchmarks and under strong adversarial perturbations, such as paraphrasing, word substitutions, and character-level edits, including unseen models and domains.
AI.result: T5-CSBoost attains state-of-the-art performance on multiclass source attribution and binary human-vs-LLM detection on the OpenLLMText and HC3 benchmarks. Under adversarial perturbations up to 90% intensity at word and character level, it remains robust and achieves state-of-the-art results on the MAGE/Deepfake stress-test suite, including in conditions with unseen models, unseen domains, and extreme paraphrasing. Overall, it substantially outperforms prior detectors, especially in adversarial and shifted settings.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly regularizing stylistic embeddings with a contrastive, margin-based triplet loss is an effective and practical way to improve robustness of T5-based AI-generated text detectors. This lightweight method, which leaves the T5-small architecture intact, yields more reliable LLM fingerprinting in real-world adversarial environments, outperforming prior approaches on multiple benchmarks and stress tests.

title: MemoHarness: Agent Harnesses That Learn from Experience
summary: An agent harness is the external control layer that turns a base LLM into an executable agent by managing context, tools, orchestration, memory, decoding, and output handling. While harness design strongly affects agent behavior, most automatic improvement methods optimize narrower artifacts such as prompts, pipelines, or workflows, and deployed agents usually reuse a single global harness for all cases. We introduce MemoHarness, an adaptive harness optimization framework that learns from its own executions. MemoHarness decomposes the harness into six editable control dimensions, stores per-case diagnoses and distilled global patterns in a dual-layer experience bank, and adapts the learned harness to each test case using retrieved experience without test-time labels, feedback, or additional search. In our evaluation across shell-agent, code-generation, and analytical-reasoning benchmarks, MemoHarness improves over the fixed harnesses we compare against and shows selective transfer to unseen suites and base models. Its additional context can also remain cost-competitive when much of the retrieved experience is cacheable. These results provide evidence that execution experience is a practical substrate for building agent harnesses that are more adaptive than a single static configuration, while leaving broader claims about statistical robustness and component attribution to future work.

AI.tldr: MemoHarness is a framework that adaptively optimizes an LLM agent’s harness using past execution experience, improving performance over fixed harnesses across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agents rely on a largely static, globally shared agent harness (the control layer that manages tools, memory, context, decoding, etc.), and most automatic improvement methods focus narrowly on prompts or workflows. This limits how well agents can adapt to diverse tasks or individual instances. The authors aim to leverage accumulated execution experience to make the harness itself adaptive per case, without requiring extra labels, feedback, or online search at test time.
AI.method: The authors formalize the ‘agent harness’ as an external control layer around a base LLM and decompose it into six editable control dimensions. They introduce MemoHarness, which maintains a dual-layer experience bank: (1) per-case diagnoses that record how a specific harness configuration performed and (2) distilled global patterns extracted from many cases. At test time, MemoHarness retrieves relevant experience from this bank and adaptively configures the harness for each new input, all without using test-time labels, human feedback, or additional search procedures. The framework is evaluated as a plug-in harness controller over different tasks and base models.
AI.result: Across shell-agent, code-generation, and analytical-reasoning benchmarks, MemoHarness achieves better performance than fixed, non-adaptive harness baselines. It also demonstrates selective transfer, meaning that experience learned in some suites and with some base models can improve performance on unseen benchmarks and models. The authors further show that the extra context introduced by retrieved experience can remain cost-competitive in practice when much of it is cacheable.
AI.conclusion: Execution experience can be systematically captured and reused to adapt not just prompts or workflows but the full agent harness, leading to more effective LLM agents than a single static configuration. MemoHarness offers a practical path toward adaptive harness design, though the authors note that fully characterizing its statistical robustness and carefully attributing gains to specific components are left to future work.

title: CoEvoT: Co-Evolving Chain-of-Thought Prompting for Graph-LLM Reasoning
summary: Graph learning under distribution shift presents a persistent challenge, where models adapt to new graphs with limited or even no supervision. Recent graph--LLM approaches move toward label-efficient prediction by linearizing graphs into prompts and using large language models (LLMs) as predictors, and can adopt Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting to exploit LLM's multi-step reasoning capability. However, existing CoT-based graph--LLM methods generate intermediate thoughts while conditioning on fixed graph tokens, limiting step-wise refinement of structural cues. In this paper, we propose CoEvoT, a simple yet effective co-evolving CoT prompting framework for graph--LLM reasoning. CoEvoT couples text-to-graph token rewriting and graph-to-text reasoning guidance in a closed loop: each intermediate textual thought is used to update the graph token evidence state via a lightweight condition network, and the updated tokens are fed back into the next-step instruction to guide subsequent LLM reasoning. This enables step-wise, state-aware evidence refinement, rather than reasoning over a fixed graph snapshot. Extensive experiments on eight datasets demonstrate that CoEvoT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: They propose CoEvoT, a co-evolving chain-of-thought prompting framework for graph–LLM reasoning under distribution shift, which iteratively refines graph token representations based on intermediate LLM thoughts and achieves better performance than prior methods on multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Graph learning under distribution shift is difficult, especially when adapting to new graphs with few or no labels. Recent graph–LLM methods improve label efficiency by converting graphs into text prompts and using LLMs for prediction, sometimes with chain-of-thought prompting to leverage multi-step reasoning. However, current CoT-based approaches keep the graph tokenization fixed throughout reasoning, preventing the model from refining structural evidence as new reasoning steps emerge. This limitation motivates a framework that allows evidence about the graph to evolve in tandem with the reasoning process.
AI.method: They introduce CoEvoT, a co-evolving CoT prompting framework for graph–LLM reasoning. CoEvoT links two processes in a closed loop: (1) text-to-graph token rewriting, where each intermediate textual thought produced by the LLM is passed through a lightweight condition network to update the graph token “evidence state”; and (2) graph-to-text reasoning guidance, where the updated graph tokens are fed back into the next instruction for the LLM, guiding subsequent reasoning. This iterative loop supports step-wise, state-aware refinement of the graph representation during the reasoning process instead of relying on a single, static graph snapshot.
AI.result: On eight benchmark datasets, CoEvoT consistently surpasses state-of-the-art baselines for graph learning under distribution shift using graph–LLM reasoning, indicating that co-evolving evidence and reasoning improves predictive performance in low- or zero-label regimes. Quantitative details are not given in the abstract but the improvements are described as consistent across datasets.
AI.conclusion: Allowing graph evidence tokens to co-evolve with chain-of-thought reasoning enables more effective, state-aware graph–LLM prediction under distribution shift. CoEvoT’s simple closed-loop design—with a lightweight condition network rewriting graph tokens based on intermediate thoughts—provides a practical way to move beyond fixed graph snapshots and yields consistent performance gains over existing graph–LLM and CoT-based approaches.

title: When a Verified World Model Still Loses: Play-Adequacy vs Prediction-Accuracy in LLM-Synthesized Code World Models
comment: 41 pages, 4 figures. Code and reproduction log: https://github.com/JaviMaligno/code-world-models
summary:
Large language models can synthesize a game's rules as executable code - a Code World Model (CWM) - which a classical planner then searches over. Such models are typically accepted when they reach high transition accuracy on sampled trajectories. We argue this is the wrong notion of adequacy for planning.
  We show four things. (1) An LLM-synthesized CWM can pass a sampling gate at 100% transition accuracy and be $\geq 98\%$ state-accurate on the planner's own search distribution, yet lose systematically at play, because the $<1\%$ it gets wrong is exactly the pivotal dynamics; the play cost of the omitted rule is $0.091$ (seed-clustered 95% CI $[0.065,0.117]$, $n=4800$). We call this the verified-vs-correct gap, and confirm it end-to-end through the synthesis pipeline. (2) The harm follows a quantitative law, $\mathrm{danger}=\mathrm{play\_cost}\times(1-\mathrm{rarity})^N$, whose $(1-\mathrm{rarity})^N$ gate-miss factor is proven exact and whose play cost is empirically bounded. (3) The failure is not repaired by more data: LLM synthesis behaves as rule translation, not rule inference, and did not infer the omitted rule across models (GPT-5.x) and data regimes (including DAgger and targeted examples). (4) The same mechanism recurs on the belief-inference function of imperfect-information CWMs: we prove a coverage bound (a size-$N$ gate is identifying when $N\gtrsim b^{d_{\max}}$), explaining why shallow games such as Kuhn poker show no gap, and hand-construct Beacon, a verified-but-wrong inference function that passes the gate yet loses every game.
  These results suggest adequacy for planning-oriented world models should be measured on the search distribution or by play directly, not by prediction accuracy on sampled transitions.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that using LLM‑generated code world models (CWMs) evaluated only by transition prediction accuracy can be dangerously misleading for planning: models that appear perfect on sampled data can systematically lose when actually used to play games.
AI.motivation: Current practice in planning with LLM‑generated world models accepts a model once it reaches high transition accuracy on sampled trajectories. The authors argue this criterion is theoretically and practically inadequate because rare but pivotal rules can be systematically missed while still yielding near‑perfect prediction scores, yet causing large performance losses in actual play.
AI.method: The authors construct and analyze LLM‑synthesized code world models for games, then stress‑test them under planning. They (1) empirically demonstrate a case where a CWM with ≥98–100% measured accuracy still performs poorly due to a missing key rule; (2) derive and validate a quantitative law relating the danger of such model errors to rule rarity and play cost; (3) test whether more data or different training regimes (including DAgger and targeted examples across GPT‑5.x models) can fix the problem, finding they cannot because the LLM does rule translation instead of true rule inference; and (4) extend the analysis to belief‑inference in imperfect‑information games, proving a coverage bound and constructing a failure example (Beacon) that passes verification but always loses.
AI.result: They identify a verified‑vs‑correct gap: a CWM can achieve 100% transition accuracy on a sampling gate and ≥98% state accuracy on the planner’s own search distribution, yet systematically lose in play due to a rare but pivotal omitted rule, with measured play cost about 0.091 (95% CI [0.065, 0.117], n=4800). They formalize the associated risk via the law danger = play_cost × (1 − rarity)^N, prove the gate‑miss factor and empirically bound play_cost. They further show that adding more data or demonstrations does not lead the LLM to infer the missing rule, and they prove and exhibit analogous verified‑but‑wrong behavior for belief‑inference in imperfect‑information games via a coverage bound and the Beacon construction.
AI.conclusion: High transition prediction accuracy on sampled trajectories is not an adequate measure of correctness for LLM‑generated world models used in planning. Rare but strategically crucial dynamics can be missed while still passing such gates, leading to large planning failures that are not fixed by more data. Adequacy criteria should instead focus on performance over the planner’s search distribution or directly on play outcomes, and world‑model evaluation needs to be redesigned accordingly for planning‑oriented use cases.

title: ReportMedSAM: Guiding Segmentation Through Radiology Reports
summary: Free-form radiology reports contain rich clinical descriptions, yet converting them for reliable segmentation remains challenging due to the inherent variability of natural language. Existing pipelines often rely on predefined organ phrases or brittle rule-based inference-time extraction, which limits their scalability to novel anatomical structures and makes them sensitive to linguistic variations. To address this, we propose ReportMedSAM, a report-driven framework that replaces discrete extraction with a learnable concept bank. By leveraging a frozen medical vision-language encoder (BiomedCLIP), we align organ-level concept embeddings with large-scale clinical corpora through contrastive learning, establishing mutually orthogonal semantic anchors. Our approach explicitly mitigates organ-level semantic collapse and ensures high robustness against diverse clinical synonyms (e.g., "renal" vs. "kidney" ). During inference, a clinical report is embedded and matched against this concept bank to dynamically activate task-specific Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) modules. This decoupled design allows new concepts and experts to be added without retraining existing components, providing a parameter-isolated extension mechanism while keeping previously learned experts unchanged. Evaluated on the AbdomenAtlas 3.0 dataset, ReportMedSAM effectively interprets free-form reports, achieves competitive segmentation accuracy, and demonstrates seamless, non-interfering extension to novel clinical tasks.

AI.tldr: A new framework, ReportMedSAM, uses a learnable concept bank and a medical vision-language encoder to robustly map free-form radiology reports to organ-level segmentation tasks, enabling extensible, report-driven medical image segmentation.
AI.motivation: Free-form radiology reports are rich in clinical details but hard to use for image segmentation because natural language is variable and ambiguous. Existing methods depend on fixed organ phrases or fragile rule-based text extraction at inference, which do not generalize well to new anatomy or linguistic variations. There is a need for a more robust and scalable way to connect clinical language with segmentation models.
AI.method: The authors introduce ReportMedSAM, a report-driven segmentation framework based on a learnable concept bank. They use a frozen medical vision-language encoder (BiomedCLIP) to obtain embeddings of organ-level concepts and align them with large-scale clinical text corpora via contrastive learning. This creates a set of mutually orthogonal semantic anchors that represent distinct organ concepts and mitigate semantic collapse and synonym issues. At inference, a clinical report is embedded and matched against this concept bank to determine which organ concepts are present. These activated concepts then select corresponding Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) segmentation modules. The architecture is decoupled so that new concepts and experts can be added in a parameter-isolated way without retraining or disturbing existing experts.
AI.result: On the AbdomenAtlas 3.0 dataset, ReportMedSAM successfully interprets free-form radiology reports and reaches competitive segmentation performance compared to existing methods. It also demonstrates the ability to add new clinical concepts and corresponding experts without degrading previously learned tasks, showing non-interfering, extensible behavior across different segmentation tasks.
AI.conclusion: ReportMedSAM provides a robust way to bridge free-form clinical language and organ-level segmentation. By replacing brittle rule-based extraction with a learnable, orthogonal concept bank and a MoE design, it handles linguistic variability, avoids organ-level semantic collapse, and supports modular, non-interfering extension to new anatomical concepts and tasks, all while maintaining strong segmentation accuracy on benchmark data.

title: ReasFlow: Assisting Reasoning-Centric Scientific Discovery in Applied Mathematics via a Knowledge-Based Multi-Agent System
summary: Recent advances in Large Language Models have fueled autonomous AI agents capable of tackling complex scientific tasks, yet existing automated research systems remain predominantly focused on empirically driven domains with quantitative benchmarks, leaving theory-driven discovery, particularly in mathematically grounded disciplines requiring rigorous proofs and synthesis of domain knowledge, largely underexplored. Key challenges include the difficulty of verifying theoretical reasoning at scale, insufficient reasoning ability for autonomous frontier exploration, and a scarcity of procedural heuristics in the literature. We introduce ReasFlow, an end-to-end autonomous agent system for reasoning-centric scientific discovery that operationalizes a collaborative paradigm where the human expert acts as Principal Investigator while the agent executes rigorous derivations as a capable graduate student. ReasFlow incorporates (i) a robust internal verification loop that audits logical coherence and corrects fundamental errors prior to human inspection, and (ii) an automated knowledge retrieval and self-improvement mechanism that proactively surfaces both declarative facts and overlooked procedural heuristics, substantially reducing expert intervention. The system unifies literature synthesis, algorithm design, theorem proving, experimentation, and manuscript preparation in a single system. Deployed to autonomously generate five complete research papers with rigorous theoretical and empirical content from minimal prompts, ReasFlow consistently achieves the highest evaluation scores among state-of-the-art open-access baselines under a curated LLM-based review rubric. ReasFlow is publicly accessible via the ReasLab platform, providing a collaborative workspace for AI-assisted theoretical research. Github repo: https://github.com/ReasLab/ReasFlow.git.

AI.tldr: ReasFlow is an autonomous LLM-based agent system for theory-focused scientific research that integrates verification, knowledge retrieval, and self-improvement to generate end-to-end research papers with strong automated review scores.
AI.motivation: Most existing autonomous research systems focus on empirical, benchmark-driven tasks and struggle with theory-heavy domains that demand rigorous proofs and deep synthesis of domain knowledge. In such domains, it is hard to automatically verify reasoning, current LLMs are weak at frontier-level exploration, and there is little procedural guidance in the literature. The authors want a system that can meaningfully assist in mathematically grounded, proof-intensive research while minimizing expert oversight.
AI.method: The authors design ReasFlow, an end-to-end autonomous agent architecture modeled on a PI–graduate-student collaboration. The system includes: (i) an internal verification loop that continuously audits and corrects logical reasoning before presenting results to humans; (ii) automated retrieval of both declarative knowledge and procedural heuristics from the literature; and (iii) integrated modules for literature synthesis, algorithm design, theorem proving, experimental validation, and manuscript drafting. ReasFlow runs iteratively, using self-improvement based on retrieved knowledge and internal checks to refine research artifacts.
AI.result: ReasFlow autonomously produced five full research papers containing both theoretical and empirical components from minimal textual prompts. Under a curated LLM-based review rubric, these papers received the highest evaluation scores relative to state-of-the-art open-access baselines, demonstrating superior performance of the system in reasoning-centric scientific discovery tasks.
AI.conclusion: An integrated, verification-centric LLM agent like ReasFlow can substantially advance autonomous theoretical research, reducing dependence on continuous expert guidance while maintaining rigor. By combining internal logical checks, active knowledge retrieval (including heuristics), and a full research pipeline in one system, ReasFlow establishes a practical framework for AI-assisted theory development and is made publicly available via the ReasLab platform for broader use and collaboration.

title: Heterogeneous Element-Aware Cross-Version Differencing of Scientific Documents via Layout-Aware Alignment and Structure-Aware Reasoning
summary: Cross-version differencing of scientific documents is essential in scholarly publishing and technical documentation, but remains challenging because scientific documents are page-structured artifacts containing heterogeneous elements such as text, tables, formulas, figures, and layout cues. Existing text-sequence-based methods often lose layout and structural information, while image-based methods lack semantic interpretability and are sensitive to rendering variation. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a layout-aware heterogeneous element-aware framework for scientific document differencing. The framework decomposes document versions into semantically typed elements, establishes cross-version correspondence through an alignment-first mechanism that jointly models spatial, content, and structural compatibility, and performs type-aware difference reasoning over aligned element pairs. It supports unified change detection, localization, structure-awareness analysis, and alignment/matching evaluation across text, tables, formulas, and figures. Experiments on real-world scientific PDF data from journal production proofreading workflows show that the proposed framework consistently outperforms element-specific baselines. It achieves detection F1 scores of 0.903, 0.855, 0.862, and 0.845 for text, tables, formulas, and figures, respectively, with further improvements in localization, structure awareness, and matching quality. Ablation and sensitivity analyses confirm the effectiveness of cross-version alignment, type-specific representations, structure-aware reasoning, and compatibility-weight design. These results demonstrate that heterogeneous element-aware differencing provides a robust and interpretable solution for scientific document comparison in realistic editorial production scenarios.

AI.tldr: They propose a layout- and element-aware framework to compare different versions of scientific PDFs, handling text, tables, formulas, and figures together, and show it beats specialized baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing differencing tools either work on plain text and ignore page layout and heterogeneous elements, or on images and lose semantics and robustness, which is problematic for scholarly publishing workflows that need accurate, interpretable change tracking in complex PDFs.
AI.method: They decompose each document version into semantically typed elements (text blocks, tables, formulas, figures), then use an alignment-first mechanism that jointly models spatial, content, and structural compatibility to match elements across versions, and finally perform type-aware difference reasoning on the aligned pairs, with a compatibility-weight design and structure-aware reasoning components.
AI.result: On real journal production PDF datasets, their method outperforms element-specific baselines on all element types, achieving detection F1 scores of 0.903 (text), 0.855 (tables), 0.862 (formulas), and 0.845 (figures), and also gives better localization, structure awareness, and matching quality; ablation and sensitivity studies validate the contributions of alignment, type-specific representations, structure-aware reasoning, and compatibility weighting.
AI.conclusion: A heterogeneous element-aware, layout-aware differencing framework is an effective, robust, and interpretable solution for cross-version comparison of scientific PDFs, suitable for realistic editorial and proofreading workflows across multiple element types.

title: RxBrain: Embodied Cognition Foundation Model with Joint Language-Visual Reasoning and Imagination
summary: Embodied cognition requires agents to connect high-level task reasoning with the physical states to be achieved. We introduce Hy-Embodied-RxBrain, an embodied cognition foundation model with joint language-visual reasoning and imagination. Unlike vision-language models that emphasize scene understanding and textual decision making, or generative world models that mainly predict future visual states, RxBrain represents embodied plans in a single planning sequence where language and visual imagination play complementary roles. Language provides the abstract structure of a plan, including task decomposition, planning primitives, constraints, temporal order, and decision logic, while visual imagination grounds this structure through world state prediction and joint subgoal planning, associating each planning step with intermediate and final physical states. RxBrain adopts a unified multimodal Mixture-of-Transformers architecture that supports language, image, and video understanding and generation within one model. To train this capability, we build an automatic pipeline that converts embodied videos into joint text-visual planning supervision by decomposing videos into planning steps and aligning them with visual state transitions. We further introduce RxBrain-Bench to evaluate whether models can represent embodied plans through joint textual and visual components rather than separate understanding or generation. Experiments show that RxBrain maintains embodied understanding and generation abilities, and produces plans with coupled textual reasoning, world state prediction, and joint subgoal planning. We also extend RxBrain to continuous robot action generation, where it shows promising real-robot performance without large-scale action-data pretraining. These results provide an initial step toward foundation models for embodied cognition.

AI.tldr: Hy-Embodied-RxBrain is a multimodal foundation model that jointly plans in language and images/videos for embodied cognition tasks, enabling coupled reasoning, imagination, and robot action generation.
AI.motivation: Most existing models for embodied agents either focus on language-based decision making without explicit grounding in predicted physical states, or on visual world models that predict future frames without high-level symbolic task structure. There is a need for a unified model that can represent, reason about, and generate embodied plans where textual reasoning and visual imagination are tightly integrated and aligned with physical execution.
AI.method: The authors propose Hy-Embodied-RxBrain, a unified multimodal Mixture-of-Transformers architecture that can both understand and generate language, images, and videos in a single model. Plans are represented as a single multimodal sequence where language tokens encode task decomposition, constraints, and decision logic, while visual tokens encode imagined intermediate and final world states. They build an automatic data pipeline that converts raw embodied videos into joint text-visual planning sequences by decomposing videos into steps and aligning each step with visual state transitions. They also design RxBrain-Bench to evaluate whether models can represent embodied plans via coupled textual and visual components.
AI.result: Experiments indicate that Hy-Embodied-RxBrain retains strong embodied understanding and generative capabilities, generating plans that combine textual reasoning with visual world state prediction and subgoal planning. On the newly introduced RxBrain-Bench, the model demonstrates the ability to represent joint language-visual plans rather than performing text-only or image-only tasks. When extended to continuous robot action generation, the model shows promising performance on real robots without relying on large-scale pretraining on action trajectories.
AI.conclusion: Hy-Embodied-RxBrain demonstrates that a single multimodal foundation model can jointly represent and generate embodied plans that couple linguistic reasoning with visual imagination and predicted world states. The work provides evidence that integrating language and vision in a unified planning sequence is a viable path toward foundation models for embodied cognition, and that such models can begin to transfer their planning representations to real-robot control even with limited action data.

title: Budgeted Subset Refinement for Execution-Aware LLM Research Ideation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can generate research ideas that appear novel to expert reviewers, but recent work also shows that such ideas often lack diversity, are difficult for LLMs to evaluate reliably, and may fail to translate into strong executed projects. This paper evaluates a controlled proxy benchmark for a pre-execution scaffolding problem: given a noisy pool of LLM-generated research ideas, how should a system allocate limited refinement effort to construct a stronger, more diverse, more execution-aware portfolio for human researchers under a fixed rubric? We introduce Budgeted Subset Refinement, a family of strategies that refine only a selected subset of candidates rather than refining all candidates uniformly. In a unified shared-candidate-pool evaluation across 10 random seeds and 10 research-ideation environments, raw generation and reranking alone produce no research-strong nonduplicate ideas under the benchmark rubric, while refinement is necessary for strong proxy-rated portfolios. Uniform refinement produces strong individual ideas but is not the best portfolio-level allocation of compute. Random-k refinement is a strong low-cost baseline, while diversity-aware MMR-k refinement gives the best overall proxy tradeoff: the highest research-strong nonduplicate yield, the lowest duplicate rate among successful methods, and the best cost per research-strong nonduplicate idea. A blinded external-judge robustness check on a balanced 72-item sample supports the broad refinement effect across independent model families, while showing that per-item rankings among refined strategies vary by judge. These results suggest that LLM research ideation systems should be evaluated not only as idea generators, but as budgeted support-allocation systems. The claims are scoped to proxy-rated portfolio quality and do not substitute for expert review or execution-grounded validation.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to best spend limited LLM “refinement” compute on a noisy pool of LLM-generated research ideas, showing that selectively refining a diverse subset (especially with diversity-aware MMR-k) yields stronger, more diverse, and more cost-effective idea portfolios than either uniform refinement or simple generation/reranking.
AI.motivation: LLMs can propose novel-looking research ideas, but these raw ideas often lack diversity, are hard for LLMs to evaluate, and don’t reliably turn into strong projects. Existing work typically treats ideation as just generation or reranking. In practice, there is a pre-execution stage where humans have limited bandwidth or compute to refine only some ideas before serious evaluation or execution. There is a need for a principled way to allocate this limited refinement effort so that the resulting portfolio is both strong (under a given rubric) and diverse, and so that LLM ideation systems are evaluated in this more realistic, resource-constrained setting.
AI.method: They define a controlled proxy benchmark for a pre-execution scaffolding task: starting from a noisy pool of LLM-generated ideas, decide which ones to refine under a fixed compute/effort budget to maximize portfolio quality. They introduce the Budgeted Subset Refinement framework, where only a subset of candidates is refined. They instantiate and compare concrete strategies—including uniform refinement (refine everything), random-k refinement (refine k randomly chosen ideas), and diversity-aware MMR-k refinement (refine k ideas chosen by a Maximal Marginal Relevance–style objective to promote both quality and diversity). Using a shared pool of candidates across 10 random seeds and 10 research-ideation environments, they evaluate each strategy under a standardized rubric, measuring strong-idea yield, diversity, duplicate rate, and compute cost. They also conduct a blinded external-judge robustness check over a balanced 72-item sample, using independent model families and multiple judges to assess consistency and sensitivity of per-item rankings.
AI.result: In the shared-candidate-pool benchmark, raw generation plus reranking alone fails to produce any research-strong, nonduplicate ideas under the specified rubric, showing refinement is crucial. Uniform refinement can produce individually strong ideas but is not the best use of compute at the portfolio level. Random-k refinement emerges as a surprisingly strong low-cost baseline. The diversity-aware MMR-k refinement strategy delivers the best overall tradeoff: it achieves the highest yield of research-strong nonduplicate ideas, has the lowest duplicate rate among successful methods, and offers the best cost per research-strong nonduplicate idea. The blinded external-judge study confirms that refinement itself robustly improves quality across different model families, but it also reveals that fine-grained rankings among different refined strategies vary depending on the judge.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based research ideation systems should be understood and evaluated not only as generators or rankers of ideas but as systems for allocating limited refinement resources across a pool of candidates. Budgeted Subset Refinement—especially diversity-aware selection strategies like MMR-k—can significantly improve the strength, diversity, and cost-effectiveness of LLM-generated research idea portfolios compared with uniform refinement or pure generation/reranking. However, their findings are explicitly scoped to proxy-rated portfolio quality and must not be treated as a replacement for expert review or validation grounded in actual research execution.

title: How Artificial Intelligence LLM Engines Shape the Global Conflict Information Environment
summary: Artificial Intelligence (AI) answer engines now field a growing share of the questions that analysts, scholars, and the public ask about issues of peace and conflict. Large Language Models (LLMs) are known to hallucinate under certain conditions, but do these errors have discernible patterns when they are asked about conflicts, and if so what can that teach us about the changing global conflict information environment? To answer, we first asked a battery of questions about 28 conflicts to five leading answer engines and scored their 5,460 answers against documented evidence. We found that the thinner the retrievable record around a given conflict, the more the engines invent, misattribute, and miscount. Thin records don't just encourage hallucination, but create structural exposure to mis- and disinformation, because they are the easiest records to warp through Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) to bias engine responses. Through an analysis of 1,048 websites that the AI LLMs pulled conflict facts from, we found that GEO source optimization is already happening, and while state-partisan digital capture remains incipient it is rapidly growing. We explain what these findings mean for scholarship with the rise of GEO information warfare, and for policy argue for a return to the deep local monitoring and translation-based research that AI tools cannot replicate, closing with a discussion of future research opportunities and challenges in this fast-moving space.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how AI answer engines and LLMs hallucinate and can be manipulated when answering questions about armed conflicts, showing that sparse information environments make them highly vulnerable to errors and targeted information warfare.
AI.motivation: AI answer engines are increasingly used to answer questions about peace and conflict, but LLMs are known to hallucinate. The authors want to know whether these hallucinations follow systematic patterns in the domain of conflicts and what that implies for the global conflict information environment, where misinformation and disinformation are major concerns.
AI.method: The authors queried five leading AI answer engines with a structured battery of questions about 28 distinct conflicts, yielding 5,460 individual answers. They systematically scored each answer against documented evidence to measure accuracy, hallucinations, misattributions, and miscounts. They also analyzed 1,048 websites that the LLMs cited or drew facts from to detect signs of Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) and to assess how online sources are being tuned to influence AI outputs.
AI.result: They find a strong relationship between the thickness of the retrievable information record and LLM errors: in conflicts with sparse, fragmented, or poorly documented online records, the engines hallucinate more, misattribute actors and events, and miscount key statistics. These thin-record conflicts are also the ones most structurally exposed to mis- and disinformation campaigns via GEO, because it is relatively easy to dominate or distort the small set of available sources. Their web-source analysis shows that GEO-style optimization targeted at conflict narratives is already occurring, and state-aligned or partisan attempts at digital capture of these narratives, while still early, are expanding quickly.
AI.conclusion: AI answer engines are not neutral mirrors of the conflict information environment; their vulnerabilities are patterned and most acute where information is already thin and contested. This creates systemic risks for scholarship, policy analysis, and public understanding when relying on LLMs for conflict information. The authors argue that in an era of GEO information warfare, robust conflict research must recommit to deep, local, human-led monitoring and translation-based methods that AI cannot substitute for. They outline an agenda for future work on measuring and mitigating GEO, improving transparency of AI sourcing, and protecting fragile information ecologies around lesser-known conflicts.

title: Semantic Register Compression in Multi-Agent LLM Cascades
summary: Multi-agent LLM systems commonly decompose complex tasks into specialized roles. However, this modularity introduces a representational risk: when intermediate agents transform text across linguistic registers, they can systematically compress the semantic distinctions needed for accurate downstream decisions. We term this phenomenon semantic register compression and characterize it as an observable failure mode in multi-agent cascades. Using a three-agent pipeline (Collector-Evaluator-Decider), we quantify compression via inter-label separation in sentence-transformer embedding space. Across political fact-checking (LIAR), sentiment analysis (SST-5), and medical triage (Triagegeist), critical evaluation consistently reduces label separability by 41.7% at the Evaluator stage, while identity passthrough preserves it nearly fully. Five architectural variants causally isolate oriented semantic transformation as the primary driver. A credibility-seeking variant produces minimal geometric compression yet shifts outputs toward mostly-true, demonstrating that transformation valence controls the direction of distributional collapse independently of compression magnitude. Compression generalizes across the three domains with varying intensity: 41.7% in fact-checking, 27.2% in sentiment, and 20.0% in triage. Prompt-level regression explains 78% of the variance, with operational constraints associated with lower compression. These results demonstrate that semantic register compression is a measurable and generalizable phenomenon in multi-agent LLM systems, with implications for safety evaluation in high-stakes domains.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and measures a new failure mode in multi-agent LLM pipelines, called semantic register compression, where intermediate textual transformations reduce the separability of classes in embedding space and harm downstream decisions.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent LLM systems often split work into specialized roles that communicate via free-form text. While this modularity is attractive, it poses a risk: intermediate agents may rewrite or reframe information in ways that systematically blur distinctions crucial for later decision-making, especially in high-stakes domains. The authors aim to formally characterize, quantify, and causally probe this representational risk, which has been underexplored relative to task performance metrics.
AI.method: They construct a three-agent pipeline with roles Collector, Evaluator, and Decider, and study how the Evaluator’s textual transformations affect the geometric structure of representations in a sentence-transformer embedding space. They define a metric of label separability (inter-label separation) and track how it changes across stages. They compare a critical evaluation variant to an identity-passthrough baseline, and design five architectural variants that alter how the Evaluator transforms content to causally attribute compression to oriented semantic transformation rather than other factors. They also introduce a credibility-seeking variant to study how changes in evaluative valence affect distributional shifts, and apply prompt-level regression to explain variance in compression across prompts and domains.
AI.result: Across three datasets/domains—political fact-checking (LIAR), sentiment analysis (SST-5), and medical triage (Triagegeist)—the Evaluator stage in the critical-evaluation pipeline reduces label separability in embedding space by 41.7% on average, while an identity passthrough preserves separability. The oriented semantic transformation is isolated as the main driver of this compression across five architectural variants. A credibility-seeking variant maintains high geometric separability (minimal compression) but biases the output labels toward mostly-true, showing that the direction of distribution shift (valence) can be decoupled from compression magnitude. Compression intensities differ by domain (41.7% fact-checking, 27.2% sentiment, 20.0% triage). Prompt-level regression explains 78% of the observed variance and suggests that prompts encoding stronger operational constraints are associated with lower compression.
AI.conclusion: Semantic register compression is a robust, quantifiable, and general failure mode in multi-agent LLM systems: when intermediate agents reframe content in particular registers (e.g., critical evaluation), they systematically reduce class separability in representation space, which can undermine downstream decisions. The phenomenon appears across diverse domains and is shaped both by the nature of semantic transformation and by prompt design. These findings imply that safety and reliability evaluations of multi-agent LLM architectures must go beyond task accuracy to include representational diagnostics, and that careful prompt and architecture design can mitigate compression, which is especially important in high-stakes applications such as political fact-checking and medical triage.

title: Align AI to Dynamic Human-AI Workflows
summary: Current alignment approaches typically focus on emulating human behavior using static representations of human preferences, failing to capture the dynamic, context-dependent nature of real-world human-AI interactions. In this paper, we argue for a shift from static and emulative to interactive and complementary alignment, where preferences emerge through interaction and alignment is defined not by satisfying preferences alone. We first formalize this gap by contrasting existing alignment with a trajectory-level view in which human and model behavior co-evolve over time. Because these interaction dynamics have not been adequately captured within existing ML formulations, we ground this perspective in insights from an interdisciplinary workshop. We draw on lessons from social-science accounts of human-human collaboration and then argue that human-AI systems amplify these dynamics, introducing new asymmetries that make reasoning about uncertainty harder and introduce new coordination challenges. Based on these lessons and new challenges, we conclude by outlining a research agenda for developing AI systems that align with humans in interaction, requiring an interdisciplinary synthesis of machine learning and the social and decision sciences.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes shifting from static, preference-imitating alignment to an interactive, trajectory-based notion of human-AI alignment where preferences and behaviors co-evolve over time.
AI.motivation: Most current AI alignment methods treat human preferences as fixed, static objects to be inferred and imitated, which misses how real human preferences are contextual, change over time, and are shaped by interaction. This gap becomes critical as AI systems are increasingly deployed in rich, ongoing collaborations with people, where alignment must account for evolving goals, uncertainty, and coordination rather than simple preference satisfaction.
AI.method: The authors conceptually formalize a trajectory-level view of alignment, where both human and model behaviors and preferences are modeled as co-evolving over time, in contrast to one-shot or static preference models. They complement this formal perspective with insights synthesized from an interdisciplinary workshop, drawing on social science theories of human-human collaboration and extending these concepts to human-AI systems that exhibit new asymmetries and coordination challenges.
AI.result: They identify and articulate key mismatches between existing alignment approaches and the needs of real-world, interactive human-AI systems; characterize amplified social and coordination dynamics introduced by AI (such as asymmetries and greater uncertainty); and distill these into requirements and desiderata for future alignment methods that can handle interactive, evolving preferences and joint behavior over time.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that alignment should be reconceived as an interactive, complementary process in which human and AI behaviors and preferences are jointly shaped through ongoing interaction rather than merely having models imitate static preferences. Achieving this requires a new research agenda that integrates machine learning with social and decision sciences to address uncertainty, asymmetry, and coordination in human-AI collaboration, and to design systems that remain aligned as both humans and AI adapt over time.

title: Cross-Dataset Generalization in Urdu Fake News Detection: An Empirical Study with XLM-RoBERTa and a Length Confound Analysis
comment: 10 pages, 7 figures, 5 tables, submitted to arXiv
summary: Urdu fake news detection remains under-resourced despite Urdu being spoken by over 231 million people worldwide. While prior work has demonstrated strong in-domain performance on individual Urdu datasets, cross-dataset generalisation has received little systematic attention. This paper presents the first cross-dataset generalisation study for Urdu fake news detection, using two publicly available balanced datasets: the Ax-to-Grind Urdu corpus (10,083 articles, 15 domains) and the Notri-Fact Urdu dataset (13,388 articles). We fine-tune xlm-roberta-base under four experimental conditions, in-domain on each dataset and two zero-shot cross-domain transfer directions, comparing against TF-IDF baselines using Logistic Regression and Support Vector Machines. Our experiments reveal a striking asymmetry: Notri-Fact to Ax-to-Grind transfer achieves a macro F1 of 0.771, while the reverse collapses to F1 of 0.005, with the model predicting fake for 99.7% of test articles. We demonstrate that this collapse stems from a systematic length confound in Ax-to-Grind, where fake articles average 117 words versus 35 for real articles, a 3.4x asymmetry inducing shortcut learning. A length ablation capping articles at 50 words yields only a 0.0067 F1 drop, confirming the confound inflates but does not solely drive in-domain performance. We provide a reusable diagnostic methodology that combines bidirectional transfer analysis and prediction-collapse inspection to identify confound-driven behavior in multilingual fake news detection settings.

AI.tldr: First systematic cross-dataset study of Urdu fake news detection reveals that strong in-domain results can hide shortcut learning driven by confounds like article length, and proposes diagnostics to detect such issues.
AI.motivation: Urdu is a major world language but remains under-resourced for fake news detection. Existing work mostly evaluates models within single datasets, missing how well they generalise across different sources. The authors aim to understand cross-dataset generalisation for Urdu fake news detection and to uncover potential dataset artifacts or confounds that may lead to misleadingly high performance.
AI.method: They fine-tune xlm-roberta-base under four settings: in-domain training/testing on each of two Urdu fake news datasets (Ax-to-Grind and Notri-Fact), and two zero-shot cross-dataset transfer directions (train on one, test on the other). They compare this transformer model to TF-IDF features with Logistic Regression and SVM baselines. They then analyse asymmetries in transfer performance, inspect prediction collapse patterns, and perform a length ablation by capping articles at 50 words to test the impact of article length confounds.
AI.result: The model shows a strong asymmetry in cross-dataset transfer. Training on Notri-Fact and testing on Ax-to-Grind yields good macro F1 (0.771), but the reverse transfer nearly fails (macro F1 0.005) with the model predicting the fake class for 99.7% of test items. They identify a major length confound in Ax-to-Grind: fake articles average 117 words, real ones 35 words (3.4x difference), encouraging shortcut learning based on length. When they cap article length at 50 words, in-domain performance drops only slightly (0.0067 F1), showing that length inflates but does not completely account for the strong in-domain scores.
AI.conclusion: High in-domain performance on Urdu fake news datasets can mask shortcut learning driven by confounds such as article length, which undermine cross-dataset generalisation. The authors propose a reusable diagnostic methodology—combining bidirectional transfer experiments and inspection of prediction collapse—to detect confound-driven behaviour in multilingual fake news detection setups. This work highlights the need for careful cross-dataset evaluation and dataset auditing when building robust fake news detectors for low-resource languages like Urdu.

title: The Steering Budget: Examples beat Knobs
summary:
Generative models are steered with knobs -- prompts, guidance scales, property tags. Turn one as hard as you like and, past a point, it stops moving the property you care about. We find that ceiling is not a shortcoming of the model but a budget, set by the training data before the model is trained: a property's movable range splits in two -- the part a knob can reach, and a second, significant part that only examples -- concrete instances of what you want more of -- can reach. That second part is usually much larger, but not always, and the same budget says so in advance.
  Reaching that second part takes a different move: instead of turning a knob, you show the model examples, composed from what it already learned rather than added to its training. A cheap audit of the training data measures the budget; we give a recipe for building the example set that reaches all of it.
  This buys two things a knob can't. Reach: it moves a property across the whole budget, not just the part a knob reaches. Expressiveness: it steers toward targets you can only specify by example -- including ones you can't put into words. We turn these into a handful of falsifiable claims and verify them in two unrelated domains, image and crystal-structure generation -- marking where a knob is enough, and where only examples will do.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that traditional control "knobs" for generative models (like prompts or guidance scales) hit an intrinsic ceiling dictated by the training data, and shows that using curated example-based steering can access a much larger range of controllable behavior.
AI.motivation: To understand why simple control mechanisms (prompts, guidance scales, tags) in generative models often saturate and stop giving additional control, and to determine whether this is due to model limitations or constraints set by the training data. The authors want a principled way to know in advance how far a given property can be steered, and to design better steering methods when knobs are insufficient.
AI.method: 1) Conceptualize a "budget" for each property in the training data that defines how far that property can be moved by steering. 2) Empirically analyze model behavior to show that knob-based control only reaches part of this budget, while example-based control can reach the rest. 3) Devise a cheap audit procedure on the training data to estimate this budget. 4) Provide a constructive recipe for assembling example sets (from what the model has already learned, not from adding new data) that can reach the full budget. 5) Test these ideas and predictions in two distinct domains: image generation and crystal-structure generation, formulating and verifying falsifiable claims about when knobs suffice and when examples are needed.
AI.result: They find that each property has a split movable range: (i) a region reachable by common control knobs (prompts, guidance scales, tags), and (ii) an often larger region that cannot be reached by turning knobs further but can be accessed by providing suitable examples. The size of these regions is determined by a "budget" that can be inferred from the training data. They also demonstrate a practical method to build example sets that allow steering across the full budget in both image and crystal-structure generation tasks.
AI.conclusion: The apparent saturation of control knobs in generative models is not primarily a limitation of the model itself but a manifestation of a training-data-defined budget for each property. Example-based steering, constructed from existing model knowledge, can access a larger portion of this budget than knob-based methods, enabling broader reach and more expressive control, including for targets that are difficult or impossible to describe in words. Their framework predicts when knob control is enough and when example-based methods are necessary, and these predictions hold across very different application domains.

title: Breaking Refusal in the First Half: A Mechanistic Study of the Prefill Jailbreak
comment: 31 pages, 3 figures. Code and derived artifacts: https://github.com/collapseindex/breaking-refusal
summary: Aligned language models refuse harmful requests, but a one-line prefill ("Sure, here is") strips the refusal. We ask where and how it fails. The harm representation stays intact: on the prompts the attack flips to compliance, a linear probe reads harm as high as on the refused ones (0.91-0.98), while behavioral refusal drops to chance. This holds across four models and three families (1.5-3.8B, and at 14B). Refusal is therefore a shallow, response-site computation. We localize it to an early window: a dose-matched position control shows the first half of the response suffices to break refusal, while the second half is nearly inert. Three causal probes converge on that window. Restoring the harm direction there partially re-engages refusal. Injecting the model's own refuse-state reverses the jailbreak (74%, held-out). And knocking out the early response's attention to the prefill, but not an equal attention mass elsewhere, selectively collapses the harmful continuation. A base-model control identifies the mechanism: the same knockout collapses the continuation prefill-specifically even in a non-safety-tuned base model (64% to 25% harmful content vs a matched control's 64%, replicated at 7B). So the prefill's grip is generic autoregressive conditioning, not safety-specific suppression, and "refusal restoration" is a model-dependent fallback. The dominant mechanism is passive. A small safety-specific attractor remains on top (logit-trace concentration 0.24 vs 0.03), whose active-vs-passive character we size but do not fully separate. No single direction or component is a clean handle either: the decision is decodable but distributed, and refusal tracks harm rather than scary surface. The consequence is structural: a monitor reading the untouched prompt-side representation is immune by construction, but only to response-site attacks. The mechanism is diffuse; the failure surface is local.

AI.tldr: This paper studies why simple "prefill" jailbreaks (like starting a response with "Sure, here is") bypass safety refusals in aligned language models, finding that refusal is a shallow mechanism in the early response tokens rather than a deep change in how the model represents harm.
AI.motivation: Aligned language models often refuse to answer harmful prompts, but trivial jailbreaks—such as prepending a short, compliant phrase to the model’s response—can make the model comply without changing the underlying prompt. The authors want to understand where in the model’s computation this failure occurs, whether the internal representation of harm actually changes, and what this reveals about the structure and robustness of safety alignment.
