AI.method: They test several LMs (1.5–14B parameters, across three model families) with and without one-line prefill jailbreaks and use linear probes to measure how strongly internal representations encode “harm.” They analyze how behavioral refusal changes under attacks, and they localize the effect to specific token positions by varying where the prefill appears. They apply causal interventions: restoring harm-related directions, injecting internal “refuse states,” and knocking out specific attention patterns to the prefill. They also compare aligned vs base (non-safety-tuned) models to distinguish generic autoregressive conditioning from safety-specific mechanisms, and use logit-trace / attribution tools to characterize active vs passive safety components and the degree of distribution of the safety signal.
AI.result: They find that even when a prefill jailbreak causes the model to comply with harmful requests, internal harm representations remain high (probe scores ~0.91–0.98), while refusal behavior collapses to chance. This shows that refusal is a shallow, response-side computation. The critical mechanism lies in the first half of the generated response: early tokens conditioned on the prefill are sufficient to break refusal, while later tokens have little effect. Causal probes and interventions show that manipulating this early window—such as restoring the harm direction or injecting the model’s own refusal state—can partially or largely undo the jailbreak, and that removing the early response’s attention to the prefill specifically knocks out the harmful continuation. Similar attention knockouts in a non-safety-tuned base model reduce harmful continuation only when driven by the prefill, indicating the mechanism is generic autoregressive conditioning, not unique to safety tuning. The safety mechanisms include a small, safety-specific attractor on top of this passive behavior, but refusal is distributed and tracks semantic harm instead of superficial cues.
AI.conclusion: Safety refusal in aligned LMs is mainly a localized, early-response mechanism layered on top of generic autoregressive conditioning, rather than a deep reconfiguration of how the model represents harmful content. Harm is still robustly encoded internally even when jailbreaks succeed, but a short compliant prefill can overwhelm refusal by steering early response tokens. Safety signals are distributed and not cleanly isolatable into single directions or components, limiting simple “knob-turning” control. However, because the harmfulness signal on the *prompt side* remains intact, external monitors that read prompt representations—rather than generated text—can in principle be robust to such response-site attacks, though they won’t address all possible failure modes.

title: Automatic Hard Example Synthesis with Multi-Level Agentic Data Curation
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are increasingly deployed for nuanced content safety and moderation tasks, yet they remain vulnerable to adversarial attacks and out-of-distribution edge cases. Traditional active learning and manual annotation fail to scale against the complexity and volume of novel multimodal threats. In this paper, we propose an automated, agentic red-teaming framework that systematically synthesizes difficult examples using an iterative strategy that proposes novel hypotheses as well as mutating on past attempts. Leveraging a multi-agent architecture that consists of a high-reasoning Architect agent, an advanced image generator, and a multi-level verification committee of LLM raters, our system autonomously uncovers boundary-pushing violations and ambiguous policy edge cases without any human intervention. By employing these carefully synthesized adversarial examples as in-context demonstrations via test-time Retrieval, we substantially improve the target model's robustness, reducing the False Negative Rate (FNR) from 41.2% to 24.5% in a public image safety benchmark without relying on any human labeling.

AI.tldr: They build an automated multi-agent red-teaming system that generates adversarial multimodal safety cases to harden MLLM content moderation without human labels, greatly cutting false negatives.
AI.motivation: MLLMs are used for complex safety and moderation, but are fragile to adversarial prompts and rare or unseen multimodal edge cases. Manual red-teaming and standard active learning do not scale to the high diversity and volume of attacks, especially when involving images and text. The authors want a scalable, automated way to discover boundary and ambiguous cases that existing systems miss, and to use these examples to improve robustness without expensive human annotation.
AI.method: They design an automated, agentic red-teaming framework using a multi-agent architecture. A high-level "Architect" agent with strong reasoning proposes novel adversarial hypotheses and attack directions. These ideas are combined with an advanced image generator to create challenging multimodal (image + text) examples. Past attempts are iteratively mutated and refined to explore the space of hard cases. A multi-level verification committee of LLM raters filters and labels generated samples, ensuring they indeed probe policy boundaries or cause failures. The resulting adversarial examples are stored and then used at test time via retrieval-augmented in-context learning: the target safety model is provided with relevant hard examples as demonstrations to guide safer decisions, with no human in the loop.
AI.result: On a public image safety benchmark, using the synthesized adversarial examples as in-context demonstrations substantially improves robustness. The false negative rate of the target model drops from 41.2% to 24.5%, demonstrating a large reduction in missed unsafe content. This is achieved entirely without manual labeling, relying solely on the automated agentic red-teaming and LLM-based verification pipeline.
AI.conclusion: An automated, agent-driven multimodal red-teaming system can systematically discover and exploit weaknesses in MLLM safety models, then use these examples via retrieval-based in-context learning to harden the same models. This approach scales red-teaming without human effort and significantly improves safety performance, suggesting that agentic self-play and synthetic adversarial data are promising directions for building more robust multimodal content moderation systems.

title: Implicit Reasoning Steering via Concept Chaining
summary: Large language models often appear to reason reliably, yet on many questions repeated sampling yields both correct and incorrect answers, revealing an underlying fragility in how final decisions are formed. We study whether this fragility can be exploited through implicit reasoning steering: using natural-language text to bias a model toward a designated answer without explicit instructions, triggers, or direct answer cues. Our approach, Concept Chaining, generates a short connection paragraph that links question entities to a target option through one or two intermediate concepts. We then continue pretraining a victim model on these connection paragraphs and evaluate whether its answer preference shifts on the original multiple-choice questions. Our results show that indirect, natural-looking text can systematically steer model predictions while remaining substantially less inferable than direct paraphrases, which shows that reasoning brittleness is not merely an evaluation artifact: it creates a practical channel through which latent biases can be amplified by ordinary-looking text to covertly redirect model decisions.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that large language models’ reasoning can be covertly biased using innocuous-looking text, exposing a practical vulnerability.
AI.motivation: Although large language models often appear to reason well, their answers to the same question can vary across samples, indicating fragile decision-making. The authors want to know whether this fragility is just an evaluation oddity or whether it enables real-world attacks, where an adversary subtly steers model reasoning and amplifies latent biases without overt instructions or obvious cues.
AI.method: They propose “Concept Chaining,” which automatically generates short natural-language “connection paragraphs” that link entities in a multiple-choice question to a chosen (target) option via one or two intermediate concepts. These paragraphs do not directly state the answer or give explicit instructions. The authors then continue pretraining (fine-tune) a target/victim model on these paragraphs and test whether its preference over answer options on the original questions systematically shifts toward the targeted option. They compare this to more direct paraphrased training data to assess subtlety and detectability.
AI.result: The victim models’ answer distributions on the original multiple-choice questions shift significantly toward the targeted options after training on the connection paragraphs, even though the steering text looks natural and does not directly reveal the answer. The steering is systematic and robust across questions, and the indirect paragraphs are substantially harder to infer as attack data compared with explicit paraphrases.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning brittleness in large language models is not just a quirk of evaluation but a genuine security and robustness issue. Ordinary-looking natural language can covertly steer model decisions by exploiting fragile internal reasoning, enabling subtle amplification of latent biases and targeted answer manipulation. This highlights the need for defenses and diagnostics that go beyond detecting explicit prompts or direct label leakage.

title: AI Agents Do Not Fail Alone:The Context Fails First
summary: Context engineering has become central to building reliable AI agents, yet it remains largely unmeasured. Agents do not fail in isolation: their behavior is shaped by the instructions, tools, memory, retrieved knowledge, guardrails, and untrusted inputs accumulated in their context. When this context is weak, agents drift, hallucinate, misuse tools, ignore constraints, become vulnerable to injection, and waste tokens. This paper validates context-engineering quality as an independent leading indicator of agent reliability. We implement the measurement in ProofAgent-Harness, an open-source infrastructure for AI agent evaluation that uses multi-juror, consensus-based scoring. The harness assesses context across seven criteria: role clarity, guardrail coverage, instruction consistency, tool schema quality, grounding sufficiency, injection hardening, and token efficiency. Crucially, the context score is isolated from behavioral metrics and release decisions, enabling a non-circular validation. Through a controlled context-quality study across regulated agent domains, holding frontier LLM agents fixed and varying only their operating context, we show that context-quality criteria consistently predict their corresponding behavioral outcomes. Grounding sufficiency predicts hallucination resistance, guardrail coverage predicts manipulation resistance, instruction consistency predicts instruction following, and tool-schema quality predicts tool use. These findings establish context measurement as a validated preflight signal for agent reliability and position context engineering as an auditable layer of agent evaluation and governance.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces and validates a way to quantitatively measure the quality of AI agents’ context (instructions, tools, memory, etc.) as an independent predictor of reliability.
AI.motivation: Current AI agents’ reliability is heavily influenced by their surrounding context (prompts, tools, guardrails, retrieved knowledge), but this ‘context engineering’ is rarely measured systematically. Failures like hallucinations, poor tool use, or vulnerability to prompt injection often trace back to weak context design rather than the model itself. The authors want a principled, auditable way to assess and improve this context so that teams can predict and enhance agent reliability before deployment.
AI.method: They build ProofAgent-Harness, an open-source evaluation framework that scores an agent’s context using multiple independent ‘jurors’ who reach consensus. The harness defines seven criteria—role clarity, guardrail coverage, instruction consistency, tool schema quality, grounding sufficiency, injection hardening, and token efficiency. Crucially, they separate context-quality scoring from direct behavior metrics and deployment decisions to avoid circular reasoning. In controlled experiments across regulated domains, they keep the underlying frontier LLM agents fixed and systematically vary only the operating context, then compare context scores against observed behavioral outcomes.
AI.result: Across the study, higher scores on specific context dimensions reliably predict better performance on the corresponding behavioral dimensions: stronger grounding sufficiency correlates with fewer hallucinations, better guardrail coverage with higher resistance to manipulation, better instruction consistency with improved instruction following, and higher tool-schema quality with better tool use. These relationships are consistent across tested regulated agent domains.
AI.conclusion: Context quality can be measured independently and serves as a robust leading indicator of AI agent reliability. The validated metrics show that improving context engineering—across instructions, guardrails, tools, and defenses—measurably improves downstream behavior. This positions context engineering as an auditable, governance-relevant layer in agent development, and establishes context scoring as a practical ‘preflight check’ before releasing agents into real-world use.

title: The Severance Problem: LLMs are Unaware of the Person Beyond the Prompt
summary: Personal AI assistants have attracted significant interest for their potential to enhance everyday life by automating routine tasks, supporting consequential decisions, and assisting with everyday personal matters. Yet despite rapid recent technical advances, these assistants continue to exhibit undesirable behaviors, such as sycophancy, overconfidence, and hallucination. We argue that these failures stem from a fundamental limitation: language models lack an explicit representation of the person beyond the context they are given, which we term as the \textbf{Severance Problem}. Even with rich personal context and strong commonsense reasoning capabilities from the backbone model, current AI assistants fail to represent what remains unknown about the user. We propose a simple solution: incorporating structured ignorance into the language model context via the \textbf{Severance Schema}, which explicitly outlines dimensions along which the model lacks knowledge about the user, including physicality, temporality, consequences, continuity, multiplicity, and interiority. Empirically, across five model families, with the Severance Schema, the assistant consistently reduces sycophancy, harmful advice, and hallucination. Notably, models with the schema ask clarifying questions when information about the user is missing, rather than confidently extrapolating from incomplete user information.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a core limitation of current personal AI assistants—their lack of an explicit, structured model of what they do and don’t know about a user—and proposes a schema to represent this ignorance, which reduces sycophancy, harmful advice, and hallucinations across multiple model families.
AI.motivation: Although personal AI assistants are improving rapidly, they still show problematic behaviors such as sycophancy (agreeing uncritically), overconfidence, and hallucination, especially in personalized settings. The authors argue that a key cause is that language models only see the user through transient context and have no persistent, explicit representation of the user or of what is unknown about the user. This gap—termed the Severance Problem—undermines reliability, safety, and appropriate deference to the user’s actual situation and preferences. The motivation is to create a principled way to make assistants aware of, and behave according to, the limits of their knowledge about an individual user.
AI.method: The authors formalize the Severance Problem and introduce the Severance Schema: a structured representation of the assistant’s ignorance about a user along several dimensions (physicality, temporality, consequences, continuity, multiplicity, interiority). They then embed this schema into the model’s context when interacting with users, so the model is reminded of what it does not know. They evaluate this intervention empirically across five different language model families, comparing behavior with and without the schema on tasks that probe sycophancy, harmful advice, hallucinations, and the tendency to ask clarifying questions versus extrapolating from incomplete user information.
AI.result: Across five model families, adding the Severance Schema to the context consistently reduces sycophantic responses, lowers the incidence of harmful advice, and decreases hallucinations. Models using the schema more frequently respond to missing or ambiguous user information by asking clarifying questions rather than making confident but unjustified assumptions about the user’s circumstances.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly representing structured ignorance about the user—through the Severance Schema—addresses a fundamental limitation of current personal AI assistants. By highlighting what the model does not know along key dimensions of user identity and situation, the approach yields safer, less sycophantic, and less hallucinatory behavior, and encourages models to seek clarification when user information is incomplete. This suggests that incorporating explicit epistemic structure into LM contexts is a promising direction for building more reliable, user-aware personal assistants.

title: Measuring How Students Rely on Generative AI in Academic Writing: Development and Multi-Source Validation of the Generative AI Reliance Types Scale (GenAI-RTS)
summary: As generative AI (GenAI) becomes increasingly embedded in undergraduate academic writing, how students rely on these tools, rather than simply whether they use them, has become a central question for learning, academic integrity, and educational equity. Existing measures of reliance were developed inductively, focused on discrete problem-solving tasks, and validated mainly with homogeneous samples. This study developed and validated the GenAI Reliance Types Scale (GenAI-RTS), a 20-item instrument measuring four theoretically derived types of GenAI reliance: Strategic, Instrumental, Dependent, and Dialogic. Validation followed the multisource framework of the Standards for Educational and Psychological Testing, drawing on a survey of 382 undergraduates at a U.S. Minority-Serving Institution and interviews with 14 purposively sampled students. Confirmatory factor analyses of six competing models supported a five-factor structure in which Strategic Reliance comprises two facets, Deliberate Use and Critical Evaluation, alongside Instrumental, Dependent, and Dialogic factors (CFI = .92, RMSEA = .08; DWLS CFI = .98, RMSEA = .07). Subscale reliability was acceptable to good (omega = .75-.88), and scalar measurement invariance held across gender, first-generation status, and STEM/non-STEM majors, to our knowledge the first such evidence for a GenAI reliance instrument. Rasch analysis indicated that a five-point response format would improve category functioning. Strategic reliance was positively associated with AI literacy, and the reliance types differentiated students across multiple writing process and outcome variables. The GenAI-RTS offers researchers and educators a theoretically grounded, psychometrically validated instrument for identifying undergraduate reliance profiles and supporting research, assessment, and AI literacy intervention.

AI.tldr: The paper develops and validates a questionnaire (GenAI-RTS) to classify how undergraduates rely on generative AI in their writing, identifying distinct, reliable reliance types linked to AI literacy and writing outcomes.
AI.motivation: Generative AI is now widely used in undergraduate writing, but educators and researchers lack a rigorous, theory-based way to measure *how* students rely on these tools (e.g., strategically vs dependently), rather than just whether they use them. Existing reliance measures are limited: they were built inductively from narrow problem-solving tasks, validated mostly on homogeneous samples, and not grounded in a comprehensive theoretical framework. This gap hinders research on learning, academic integrity, and equity, and limits the design of targeted AI literacy interventions.
AI.method: The authors created the GenAI Reliance Types Scale (GenAI-RTS), a 20-item self-report questionnaire based on four theoretically derived reliance types: Strategic, Instrumental, Dependent, and Dialogic. They collected survey responses from 382 undergraduates at a U.S. Minority-Serving Institution and conducted interviews with 14 purposively sampled students. Using the Standards for Educational and Psychological Testing as a framework, they ran confirmatory factor analyses comparing six competing measurement models, tested reliability with omega coefficients, examined scalar measurement invariance across gender, first-generation status, and STEM vs non-STEM majors, and performed Rasch analysis to evaluate and refine the response scale. They also tested relationships between reliance types, AI literacy, and multiple writing process and outcome variables to gather evidence of construct validity.
AI.result: Confirmatory factor analysis showed that a five-factor model best fit the data: Strategic Reliance splits into two distinct but related facets—Deliberate Use and Critical Evaluation—alongside Instrumental, Dependent, and Dialogic reliance factors (fit indices: CFI = .92, RMSEA = .08; with DWLS, CFI = .98, RMSEA = .07). Subscale reliability was acceptable to good (omega between .75 and .88). Scalar measurement invariance held across gender, first-generation status, and STEM/non-STEM majors, indicating the scale functions comparably across these groups. Rasch analysis suggested that a five-point response format would enhance category functioning. Strategic reliance correlated positively with AI literacy, and the different reliance types significantly distinguished students in terms of their writing processes and outcomes, supporting the instrument’s validity.
AI.conclusion: The GenAI-RTS is a theoretically grounded and psychometrically sound tool for measuring distinct types of undergraduate reliance on generative AI in academic writing. It can be used to identify student reliance profiles, support research on GenAI’s educational impacts, and inform assessment and AI literacy interventions. The evidence for factor structure, reliability, invariance across key demographic groups, and meaningful relations with AI literacy and writing outcomes positions the GenAI-RTS as a robust instrument for studying and guiding GenAI use in higher education.

title: Multi-Head Latent Control: A Unified Interface for LLM Agent Decision Making
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed as agents, but reliable agentic behavior requires more than next-token prediction. At inference time, it is preferred that an agent can decide whether to proceed with its current reasoning, defer to a stronger model, request additional information, invoke external tools, or abstain under the given setup. Existing approaches address these decisions through prompt-level routing, external orchestration, or task-specific fine-tuning, which primarily rely on input-side signals, and are often costly and difficult to maintain as model backbones evolve. We ask whether such control decisions can be inferred directly from a model's latent generation process. We introduce Multi-Head Latent Control, a lightweight layer that reads hidden-state trajectories from a frozen LLM or VLM to produce deployment-time control signals. A Capability Head predicts whether the current model can solve the instance or should defer to a stronger collaborator, while a Resolution Head predicts appropriate resolution decision Clarification, Tool Use, Abstention, or Direct Answering. Both heads are trained only on latent traces from the same frozen LLM backbone, enabling post hoc adaptation without modifying the model. Across language and vision-language settings, Multi-Head Latent Control consistently improves the quality-cost tradeoff of multi-model systems, enabling early handoff from partial generations and more accurate intervention decisions. In routed execution (small + large model), it reduces large-model usage by up to 90.7 percent on AndroidWorld and 27-53 percent on average across benchmarks, while retaining most of large-model performance. Additionally, the learned control signals improve tool-use decision quality, yielding up to +158 percent relative score gain and 65.5 percent fewer missed-required tool calls.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Multi-Head Latent Control, a lightweight add-on that reads hidden states from a frozen LLM/VLM to decide whether to answer, ask for clarification, use tools, abstain, or defer to a stronger model, greatly improving quality-cost tradeoffs in multi-model and tool-use setups.
AI.motivation: Existing agentic LLM systems need to decide when to continue reasoning, switch to a stronger model, ask for more information, call tools, or abstain. Current solutions (prompt routing, external orchestrators, task-specific fine-tuning) mostly rely on input-only signals, are brittle to backbone changes, and can be costly. The authors aim to leverage the model’s own latent generation process to make these deployment-time control decisions in a more robust and efficient way, without retraining or modifying the backbone model.
AI.method: They keep a base LLM or VLM backbone frozen and attach a lightweight layer, Multi-Head Latent Control, that reads trajectories of hidden states during partial generations. This layer has two prediction heads: (1) a Capability Head that estimates whether the current backbone can successfully solve the instance versus deferring to a stronger collaborator; and (2) a Resolution Head that predicts which resolution action is appropriate: ask for clarification, use a tool, abstain, or answer directly. Both heads are trained on latent traces produced by the same frozen backbone, enabling post hoc adaptation without altering the backbone or relying primarily on input-only routing schemes.
AI.result: On both language-only and vision-language benchmarks, Multi-Head Latent Control improves the efficiency of multi-model systems and the quality of tool-use decisions. In routed execution setups combining a small and large model, it reduces the reliance on the large model by up to 90.7% on AndroidWorld and by 27–53% on average across benchmarks, while preserving most of the large model’s performance. For tool-use scenarios, the learned control signals provide up to a 158% relative score improvement and reduce missed required tool calls by 65.5%, demonstrating more accurate and timely interventions such as early handoff and proper tool invocation.
AI.conclusion: Reading and interpreting a model’s own latent generation dynamics is an effective way to obtain deployment-time control signals for agentic behavior. Multi-Head Latent Control offers a lightweight, post hoc method to decide when to defer, clarify, use tools, abstain, or answer directly—without modifying or retraining the backbone LLM/VLM. This approach yields substantial gains in quality-cost tradeoffs for multi-model routing and significantly improves tool-use decision quality, suggesting that latent-based control can replace or complement traditional prompt routing and external orchestration mechanisms.

title: Tracing LLM Behavior to the Training Data with Empirical Next-Token Distributions
summary: In this paper, we study the connection between an LLM's output distribution and the data used to train it. Specifically, we study the degree to which an LLM's next-token distribution agrees with the empirical next-token distribution (ENTD) given the context in the training data. The ENTD is an appealing target because it is the unrestricted global minimizer of the next-token cross entropy loss used for pretraining, as well as an easily interpretable function of the pretraining corpus. We find that for a significant fraction of inputs, the LLM's distribution agrees with the ENTD almost perfectly, and the average agreement increases with model scale and training compute. Nevertheless, there is a long tail of input sequences where the LLM and ENTD differ significantly, and we examine several possible sources of this discrepancy across the transformer architecture, training procedure, and finite-sample noise in the ENTD estimate itself. More broadly, we hope our findings will encourage more work on ``data-centric mechanistic interpretability,'' a complement to standard mechanistic interpretability that opens the black box of how model behaviors arise from the data, rather than how they are encoded in the learned weights.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how closely a language model’s predicted next-token distribution matches the empirical next-token distribution (ENTD) from its training data, revealing where the model truly imitates the data and where it diverges.
AI.motivation: To better understand how LLM behavior arises from training data, rather than only from model weights, by quantitatively connecting model output distributions to the empirical distributions in the pretraining corpus for the same contexts.
AI.method: Compute the empirical next-token distribution (ENTD) from the pretraining corpus for many contexts; compare this with the LLM’s next-token probabilities for those same contexts; analyze agreement as a function of model scale and training compute; investigate contexts with large discrepancies and probe architectural, training, and statistical (finite-sample) explanations.
AI.result: For many input contexts, the LLM’s next-token distribution matches the ENTD almost perfectly, and this agreement increases with larger models and more training compute; however, there remains a substantial tail of contexts where the model’s predictions differ strongly from the ENTD, attributable to factors across architecture, optimization, and noise in ENTD estimates.
AI.conclusion: LLMs often closely approximate the empirical next-token statistics of their training data but systematically diverge on a long tail of contexts; understanding these divergences motivates a “data-centric mechanistic interpretability” agenda focused on how specific data patterns give rise to model behaviors, complementing weight-centric mechanistic interpretability.

title: PReM: Learning What to Preserve and When to Refresh for Context Compression
summary: Efficient long-context inference is not only about reducing memory cost, but also about keeping useful contextual evidence accessible as generation proceeds. However, existing compression-oriented approaches, such as key-value (KV) cache compression and context compression, often either make an early decision about which contextual information to keep or rely on an external compressor. Such designs make it difficult to adapt the compressed context to the evidence needed by later reasoning steps. This paper introduces PReM (Preserve and Refresh Memory), a context-compression framework that maintains the long context as the model's internal layer-wise KV memory and learns what to preserve and when to refresh it. Specifically, PReM uses a dedicated memory layer to make memory-selection decisions, and a special memory token <m> to trigger refreshes during generation. To train this behavior, PReM introduces Phase-Separated Refresh Training, aligning memory selection with memory-conditioned generation while preserving continuity across refreshes. Experiments with 32K-token contexts show that PReM outperforms strong baselines under both 16x and 32x compression, while maintaining a favorable balance between answer quality and inference efficiency.

AI.tldr: Introduces PReM, a framework for efficient long-context inference that learns to preserve and refresh internal KV memory during generation.
AI.motivation: Existing long-context methods focus on compression (KV cache or context compression) but either commit too early to what to keep or depend on an external compressor, making it hard to adapt to the evolving evidence needs of multi-step reasoning. There is a need for a method that both saves memory and keeps relevant evidence accessible throughout generation.
AI.method: PReM represents the entire long context within the model’s internal layer-wise KV memory and learns dynamic memory management. It adds (1) a dedicated memory layer that makes token-level memory-selection decisions over KV states, and (2) a special memory token <m> that, when generated, triggers a refresh of the memory. The training procedure, Phase-Separated Refresh Training, disentangles learning when/what to refresh from learning how to generate conditioned on refreshed memory, while enforcing continuity across successive refreshes.
AI.result: On tasks with 32K-token contexts, PReM surpasses strong baselines in both answer quality and efficiency at high compression rates, specifically under 16x and 32x compression of context, indicating better use of limited KV memory compared to prior compression-centric methods.
AI.conclusion: By jointly learning preservation and refresh policies over internal KV memory, PReM achieves a better trade-off between long-context reasoning quality and computational efficiency than existing compression-only approaches, demonstrating that adaptive memory management during generation is crucial for scalable long-context inference.

title: Traccia: An OpenTelemetry-Based Governance Platform for AI Systems
comment: 26 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables, Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process, Declaration of competing interest
summary: The rapid development of Large Language Models (LLMs) and Artificial Intelligent (AI) powered autonomous agents has fundamentally changed the existing forms of software governance. In spite of the rigorous standards of transparency and account ability required according to the international frameworks such as the European Union's AI Act, there is a considerable gap between theory and reality. The present study discusses the inherent drawbacks of currently utilized platforms for LLM evaluation, machine learning workflow, and application performance monitoring in general. It has been shown that current disjointed solutions fail to protect unbound state space agentic architecture from serious threats such as alignment drift, SaaS security concerns, and unauthorized deployment of shadow AI systems. Moreover, a solution is proposed for overcoming the discussed challenges in form of a coherent multi-level AI governance stack Traccia built on the top of OpenTelemetry infrastructure platform. Traccia resolves the last mile for AI Alignment by adding the telemetry data, passive semantic guardrail assessment, and execution lineage into a hashed trace ledger. Traccia automatically creates compliance evidence packages by appending tamper-resistant fingerprints and SHA-256 content hash, that map to regulatory requirements (Articles 12, 14, 19, 26(6), and 50 of the EU AI Act) without invading any data privacy. By performing this evaluation in a methodical manner, a solid machine-readable base has been created for enterprise-wide management of autonomous AI systems.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies gaps in current AI and LLM governance and monitoring tools and proposes Traccia, a multi-level governance and traceability stack built on OpenTelemetry, to provide tamper‑resistant, machine‑readable evidence for compliance with the EU AI Act.
AI.motivation: Existing tools for LLM evaluation, ML workflow orchestration, and application performance monitoring are fragmented and not designed for the specific risks of autonomous, agentic AI systems. This leads to vulnerabilities such as alignment drift, security issues in SaaS setups, and uncontrolled “shadow AI” deployments, all while organizations face stringent transparency and accountability requirements under regulations like the EU AI Act. The authors aim to close the gap between regulatory theory and practical, operational AI governance.
AI.method: The authors conceptually design and describe Traccia, a unified AI governance stack layered on top of the OpenTelemetry observability infrastructure. Traccia introduces structured telemetry capture for AI systems, passive semantic guardrail assessments, and execution lineage tracking, and then records this information into a hashed trace ledger with tamper‑resistant fingerprints and SHA‑256 content hashes. These artifacts are mapped to specific regulatory obligations (e.g., Articles 12, 14, 19, 26(6), 50 of the EU AI Act) to form compliance evidence packages, all while preserving data privacy by avoiding direct exposure of sensitive content.
AI.result: The proposed system demonstrates that AI observability data (telemetry, guardrail evaluations, lineage) can be organized into cryptographically protected traces that directly support regulatory compliance and governance. Traccia’s design closes the “last mile” of AI alignment from a governance perspective: it enables continuous, verifiable tracking of agentic AI behavior and lifecycle, mitigates key risks like alignment drift and shadow AI, and integrates with existing OpenTelemetry-based infrastructure instead of requiring entirely new tooling.
AI.conclusion: By integrating AI-specific observability with cryptographic integrity guarantees and explicit mappings to EU AI Act requirements, Traccia offers a coherent, multi-level governance stack suitable for enterprise deployment of autonomous AI systems. This provides organizations with a machine-readable, auditable foundation for transparency, accountability, and risk management, helping to bridge the gap between abstract regulatory standards and real-world operation of LLM-based agents and AI services.

title: HABIB_TAZ at SemEval-2026 Task 11: Disentangling Formal Logic from Content via Synthetic Training and Multi-Objective Optimization
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) excel in many general NLP tasks, their formal reasoning capabilities are often compromised by content effects, demonstrating a measurable bias towards real-world plausibility. In this paper, we present our system for SemEval-2026 Task 11, which evaluates the ability of models to disentangle formal logic from content across 12 languages with and without distractor premises. We address this challenge using mDeBERTa-v3 networks fine-tuned on a synthetic, rule-based dataset of syllogistic schemes to avoid the semantic noise of LLM-augmented data. To explicitly decouple plausibility from logical structure, our training pipeline employs a multi-objective loss function combining Adaptive Group Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO), a scheduled differentiable bias penalty, and KL-Divergence consistency regularization. Our system achieved #1 ranks and perfect Ranking Scores (100.0) with 0.00% bias and 100.0% accuracy on Subtask 1 (English), Subtask 2 (Noisy English), and Subtask 3 (Multilingual). On the highly complex Subtask 4 (Noisy Multilingual), the system achieved the 6th rank with 89.06% Accuracy and F1-score, alongside a limited 2.89% Bias and a 37.78 Ranking Score. Our dataset generation engine and codebase are publicly available to facilitate future work on robust logical reasoning.

AI.tldr: System for SemEval-2026 Task 11 that trains mDeBERTa-v3 on synthetic logical syllogisms with a bias-robust multi-objective loss, achieving near-perfect logical reasoning and minimal plausibility bias across multilingual noisy settings.
AI.motivation: LLMs tend to conflate logical validity with real-world plausibility, especially under content effects, and this weakness persists across languages and noisy scenarios. The task aims to rigorously evaluate and improve models’ ability to perform pure formal reasoning, independent of semantic plausibility and distractor premises. The authors are motivated to build a system that genuinely captures logical structure, works in many languages, and is robust to noise and bias.
AI.method: They use mDeBERTa-v3 as the backbone and fine-tune it on a fully synthetic, rule-based dataset of syllogistic schemes, explicitly avoiding LLM-generated data to reduce semantic and distributional biases. The training pipeline uses a multi-objective loss consisting of: (1) Adaptive Group Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO) to ensure strong worst-group performance and reduce dependence on shortcuts; (2) a scheduled differentiable bias penalty to progressively penalize reliance on plausibility-based or shortcut signals; and (3) KL-divergence consistency regularization to encourage prediction stability across variants or perturbations. The system is evaluated on SemEval-2026 Task 11 across 12 languages and conditions with and without distractor premises.
AI.result: On Subtasks 1–3 (English, Noisy English, and Multilingual), their system ranks #1, reaches perfect Ranking Scores (100.0), with 0.00% measured bias and 100.0% accuracy, indicating near-ideal disentanglement of logical validity from content plausibility in those settings. On the more difficult Subtask 4 (Noisy Multilingual), their system ranks 6th, achieving 89.06% Accuracy and F1, 2.89% Bias, and a 37.78 Ranking Score, showing strong but not perfect robustness when both noise and multilinguality are combined.
AI.conclusion: Carefully designed synthetic, rule-based training data combined with a bias-aware, multi-objective optimization framework can substantially improve formal logical reasoning in pretrained language models and suppress plausibility-driven biases, even across multiple languages and noisy conditions. The strong performance on most subtasks and competitive results on the hardest noisy multilingual setting support the approach’s effectiveness. By releasing their dataset generation engine and code, the authors aim to enable further work on robust, content-independent logical reasoning in NLP models.

title: CIPHER: A Decoupled Exploration-Selection Framework for Test-Time Scaling of Data Science Agents
summary: Data science tasks span from closed-ended information extraction to open-ended analysis, presenting significant challenges for automation. Recent AI agents powered by language models show promise for handling such complex tasks. However, existing agents typically rely on a single initial state that conditions the entire agent's execution, making them vulnerable to cascading errors initiated by a suboptimal initial state. To mitigate this, we present CIPHER, an automated data science agent that leverages test-time scaling through the generation and selection of multiple initial states for concurrent execution. Unlike existing works on test-time scaling of AI agents, CIPHER explicitly decouples the generation of candidate initial states from their strategic selection for parallel execution. Through extensive evaluation on two benchmarks (closed-form and open-form tasks), we demonstrate that CIPHER exceeds state-of-the-art performance in matched-model comparisons, and remains competitive against larger-model baselines despite relying on a substantially smaller base LM. Our empirical study characterizes the design space of the Decoupled Exploration-Selection (DES) framework: we quantify how generation strategy, selection strategy, and aggregator model capacity contribute to overall performance, and derive actionable design recommendations for practitioners.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CIPHER, a data science AI agent that improves robustness and performance by generating multiple alternative initial task states and strategically selecting among them for parallel execution, using a Decoupled Exploration-Selection (DES) framework.
AI.motivation: Existing language-model-based data science agents condition their entire execution on a single initial state, so if that initial interpretation, plan, or setup is flawed, errors compound throughout the workflow. This makes them brittle for complex data science tasks, which can be either closed-form (well-defined answers) or open-form (exploratory analysis). The authors aim to reduce these cascading errors and make agents both more accurate and more reliable without simply scaling up model size.
AI.method: They propose CIPHER, an automated data science agent built on a smaller base language model but enhanced via test-time scaling. CIPHER first generates multiple candidate initial states (exploration) and then uses a separate, explicit selection mechanism to choose which of these states to execute in parallel (selection). This Decoupled Exploration-Selection (DES) framework separates the process of producing diverse starting points from the process of strategically choosing among them, and it allows for different generation strategies, selection strategies, and aggregator model capacities to be combined and tuned. Results from different runs are then aggregated to produce the final answer.
AI.result: On two benchmarks covering both closed-form and open-form data science tasks, CIPHER outperforms prior state-of-the-art agents when using comparable underlying models, and remains competitive with methods that rely on substantially larger language models. The experiments systematically vary components of the DES framework and show how different exploration strategies, selection strategies, and aggregator model sizes each affect accuracy and robustness.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling the generation and selection of initial states and running multiple promising candidates in parallel is an effective way to improve data science agents without simply scaling up model size. The DES framework behind CIPHER offers a principled design space, and the authors provide empirical guidance on how to configure generation, selection, and aggregation components to obtain strong performance in both closed-form and open-form data science tasks.

title: MamaBench: Benchmarking LLM Robustness in Maternal and Child Health Diagnosis through Counterfactual Clinical Perturbation
summary: Large language models achieve strong scores on medical benchmarks, yet these benchmarks evaluate each question in isolation, providing no measure of whether a system can distinguish clinically similar presentations requiring different interventions. We introduce MamaBench, the first counterfactual benchmark for maternal and paediatric AI: 434 expert-authored clinical narratives in 217 pairs across 371 pathologies, evaluated via the Bias Trap Rate (BTR), the conditional probability that a model fails the counterfactual given success on the base case. We propose Evidence-Anchored RAG (EA-RAG), a three-stage retrieval method that replaces aggregate similarity with an evidence coverage objective through clinical parameter extraction, coverage auditing, and contrastive sub-queries. Across eight configurations of four frontier LLMs, base accuracy overstates robust accuracy by 16-28 percentage points in every model. EA-RAG achieves 20.3% BTR and 65.0% robust accuracy on Claude Sonnet 4.6, a 5.5 percentage point BTR reduction without degrading base accuracy. The residual 20% BTR confirms that counterfactual robustness in clinical AI remains an open challenge. Keywords: counterfactual evaluation, clinical AI, maternal healthcare, retrieval-augmented generation, diagnostic robustness

AI.tldr: The paper presents MamaBench, a counterfactual clinical benchmark focused on maternal and pediatric cases, and proposes Evidence-Anchored RAG to improve large language models’ robustness to clinically similar but intervention-critical variations.
AI.motivation: Existing medical benchmarks test questions in isolation and don't measure whether AI systems can distinguish between clinically similar cases that nonetheless require different treatments. This is especially critical in maternal and pediatric care, where subtle differences in presentation can have major implications for management. The authors want a way to quantify and reduce models’ tendency to rely on superficial pattern matching or biases rather than clinically relevant distinctions.
AI.method: They build MamaBench, a new benchmark of 434 expert-written clinical narratives arranged in 217 base/counterfactual pairs spanning 371 pathologies. For each pair, the counterfactual is clinically similar to the base case but requires a different intervention. They define the Bias Trap Rate (BTR) as the probability a model fails on the counterfactual given that it answered the base correctly, capturing lack of counterfactual robustness. They then introduce Evidence-Anchored RAG (EA-RAG), a three-stage retrieval-augmented generation method: (1) extract structured clinical parameters from the case; (2) audit retrieval coverage against these parameters; and (3) issue contrastive sub-queries to cover missing evidence rather than relying on aggregate similarity of the whole case.
AI.result: Testing four frontier LLMs in eight configurations, they find that standard “base” accuracy overestimates robust performance by 16–28 percentage points when counterfactuals are considered. Using EA-RAG with Claude Sonnet 4.6 yields a Bias Trap Rate of 20.3% and a robust accuracy of 65.0%, improving BTR by 5.5 percentage points compared with baselines without sacrificing base accuracy.
AI.conclusion: MamaBench reveals that large language models performing strongly on traditional medical benchmarks still struggle with counterfactual robustness in maternal and pediatric scenarios, often failing to adjust recommendations when subtle but clinically decisive details change. Evidence-Anchored RAG can reduce—but not eliminate—this vulnerability by focusing retrieval on explicit coverage of key clinical parameters. However, the remaining ~20% BTR demonstrates that reliably handling counterfactual clinical variations is still an unresolved challenge for clinical AI and needs further research.

title: Chat2Scenic: An Iterative RAG-Based Framework for Scenario Generation in Autonomous Driving
comment: Accepted at 2026 IEEE International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS)
summary: Validating autonomous driving systems requires diverse, regulation-compliant test scenarios. In simulation-based testing, scenarios are defined as executable scripts. Yet automatically generating such scripts from regulatory descriptions remains an open challenge, and existing approaches face fundamental trade-offs. Retrieval-assemble methods achieve reasonable compilation rates but lack scalability, whereas retrieval-based full-script generation suffers from low compilation success rates. We present Chat2Scenic, the first iterative retrieval-augmented framework to generate scenario scripts in Domain Specific Language (DSL). Specifically, Chat2Scenic provides a chatbot interface that supports interactive scenario refinement and integrates Retrieval-augmented Generation (RAG) to ground scenario generation in regulatory knowledge and DSL syntax. Furthermore, we propose an open benchmark for scenario generation comprising 123 scenarios from various regulations, including NHTSA and United Nations Vehicle Regulations, as well as other sources. Extensive evaluation with State-of-the-Art (SOTA) Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrates that Chat2Scenic achieves 76.42% Compilation Success Rate (CSR) and 58.17% Framework Accuracy (FA), outperforming existing methods (Retrieval Assemble with 30.08% CSR, 11.03% FA and Retrieval full script generation with 16.26% CSR, 10.86% FA). To facilitate future research, we release our code as open source at https://github.com/TUM-AVS/chat2scenic.

AI.tldr: Chat2Scenic is an iterative, retrieval-augmented framework that converts textual driving regulations into executable simulation scenarios in a DSL, with much higher compilation success and accuracy than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Autonomous driving systems must be tested against many diverse scenarios that comply with official regulations (e.g., NHTSA, UN regulations). These scenarios are executed in simulators via domain-specific languages, but manually authoring or scripting them is costly and error-prone. Automatically generating correct DSL scripts from regulatory text is challenging: existing retrieval-assemble methods don’t scale well, while retrieval-based full-script generation often fails to compile. There is also a lack of standardized benchmarks to evaluate such scenario generation systems. The paper aims to address these gaps.
AI.method: The authors propose Chat2Scenic, an iterative retrieval-augmented generation framework. It offers a chatbot interface for interactively refining test scenarios, and uses RAG to ground LLM outputs both in regulatory documents and in the syntax/semantics of the Scenic DSL (a scenario description language). The system retrieves relevant regulatory text and DSL knowledge, then uses an LLM to generate or refine scripts, checking and iterating until they compile. Additionally, the authors create an open benchmark with 123 scenarios drawn from multiple regulatory sources to systematically evaluate scenario generation systems.
AI.result: On the new benchmark, Chat2Scenic is evaluated with state-of-the-art LLMs and compared to two baselines: (1) Retrieval-assemble methods and (2) retrieval-based full-script generation. Chat2Scenic attains a 76.42% Compilation Success Rate and 58.17% Framework Accuracy, substantially surpassing Retrieval-assemble (30.08% CSR, 11.03% FA) and retrieval full script generation (16.26% CSR, 10.86% FA).
AI.conclusion: Chat2Scenic demonstrates that an iterative, RAG-based approach can effectively translate regulatory descriptions into executable DSL scenarios, yielding much higher compilation success and scenario correctness than existing approaches. The released benchmark and open-source implementation provide a foundation for further research on regulation-grounded scenario generation and automated testing pipelines for autonomous driving systems.

title: DS@GT ARC at LongEval: Citation Integrity and Factual Grounding in Scientific QA
comment: 12 pages, 4 figures. Accepted to the CLEF 2026 LongEval Lab Working Notes
summary: This paper describes DS@GT ARC's submission to the CLEF 2026 LongEval Task 4 on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). In this submission, we examine a divergence between traditional natural language evaluation metrics and citation integrity as applied to RAG QA systems. We evaluate a corrective pipeline using Corrective RAG (CRAG) and CiteFix against baseline and frontier model benchmark RAG QA scores. While frontier models maximized answer relevance and fluency scores, our RAGAs LLM-as-judge diagnostics indicate that frontier models would correctly identify relevant documents without using their context in answer generation. Conversely, by filtering chunks pre-generation and enforcing strict entailment of generated claims to the cited material post-generation, our corrective pipeline marginally improved citation faithfulness and answer grounding. We propose that evaluation of trustworthy RAG QA requires metrics that reward strict answer grounding.

AI.tldr: They compare standard RAG evaluation metrics with citation integrity, and show a corrective RAG pipeline that slightly improves grounding and faithfulness, arguing that trustworthy RAG needs grounding-focused metrics.
AI.motivation: Standard metrics for RAG QA emphasize answer relevance and fluency, but may not reflect whether answers are truly supported by cited documents; the authors want to investigate this divergence and how to better evaluate trustworthy RAG.
AI.method: They submit a system to the CLEF 2026 LongEval Task 4 on RAG, benchmarking baseline and frontier RAG QA models against a corrective pipeline that uses Corrective RAG (CRAG) and CiteFix. They employ RAGAs LLM-as-judge diagnostics to analyze how models use retrieved documents, and apply both pre-generation chunk filtering and post-generation entailment checks between answers and cited evidence.
AI.result: Frontier models achieved high scores in traditional metrics for answer relevance and fluency, and could correctly identify relevant documents, but often did not rely on those documents in generation. The corrective pipeline, which enforced stricter grounding via chunk filtering and entailment-based citation checking, led to marginal improvements in citation faithfulness and answer grounding compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that maximizing conventional QA metrics is insufficient for trustworthy RAG; evaluation setups and metrics must explicitly reward strict grounding of answers in cited sources. Corrective pipelines like CRAG + CiteFix can help, but a shift in evaluation practices is necessary to properly value citation integrity and grounded generation.

title: A Comparative Analysis of Machine Learning Models for Long and Short-Term Forecasting of the Egyptian Stock Market: A Focus on EGX30
summary: This study concentrates on predicting stock prices in the Egyptian market, focusing on the EGX30, an influential financial hub in the Middle East. While most research focuses on global stocks, there's a growing need to understand stock trends in developing countries like Egypt. The study compares different machine learning models for forecasting EGX30 trends, covering short and long-term predictions. Using historical EGX30 data, including metrics like root mean squared error, Mean Absolute Percentage Error, and coefficient of determination, models like K-Nearest Neighbours, random forest, extreme gradient boosting, long short-term memory networks, and gated recurrent unit networks were evaluated. The goal is to determine the most effective models for EGX30 prediction, considering Egypt's unique market dynamics. Insights from this study aid investors in making informed decisions. Results show that the Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) outperformed the other models in the one-week, one-month, and two-months while the eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) model outperformed others in the one-day predictions, highlighting their usefulness in predictive analysis for financial markets. The study also showed the importance of using the ensemble techniques, especially in the long-term predictions which proved better results reaching 5 times the GRU in the two-month predictions. Additionally, the study notes the surprisingly good performance of K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN) on long-term predictions, suggesting its enduring relevance and potential for future applications in the fintech domains.

AI.tldr: The paper compares several machine learning models to forecast Egypt’s EGX30 index and finds GRU best for most horizons, XGBoost best for 1‑day, and ensembles best for long-term forecasting.
AI.motivation: Most stock prediction research targets major developed markets, leaving emerging markets like Egypt underexplored despite their growing importance. The authors want to understand how well modern ML and deep learning models work on EGX30, a key index in the Egyptian market, and identify which methods are most suitable for different prediction horizons to help investors and analysts make better decisions.
AI.method: Using historical EGX30 data, the authors build and evaluate multiple forecasting models: K‑Nearest Neighbours (KNN), Random Forest, XGBoost, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) networks. They assess performance for multiple horizons (1‑day, 1‑week, 1‑month, 2‑months) using metrics such as Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE), and coefficient of determination (R²). They also construct ensemble models, particularly for longer-term predictions, and compare their performance against individual models.
AI.result: GRU delivers the best performance for 1‑week, 1‑month, and 2‑month forecasts, while XGBoost performs best for 1‑day forecasts. Ensemble techniques substantially improve long‑term predictions, with an ensemble achieving performance up to five times better than GRU for the two‑month horizon. KNN shows unexpectedly strong results on long-term forecasts, comparing favorably with more complex models.
AI.conclusion: GRU is generally the most effective model for medium- and longer-horizon EGX30 forecasting, whereas XGBoost is preferable for very short-term (1‑day) predictions. Ensemble approaches are particularly valuable for long-term horizons, significantly outperforming single deep learning models. The notable long-term performance of KNN indicates that simpler, instance-based methods should not be overlooked in financial forecasting for emerging markets, and the overall findings provide practical guidance for investors operating in the Egyptian market.

title: Smarter and Cheaper at Once: Byte-Exact KV-Cache Grafting Turns a Frozen Small Model into a Verified-Knowledge Flywheel
summary: We report a way to make a frozen small language model both more capable and dramatically cheaper at once, without changing any weights. Verified knowledge is deposited once as a byte-exact key-value (KV) state artifact and later restored, by graft, into a fresh inference context. The restore is bit-exact: under a pinned deterministic configuration, the grafted logits are byte-for-byte identical to a fresh computation (SHA-256 equality), with zero KL divergence and 100% argmax agreement over fifty samples. We show that own-position graft is the unique numerically exact operating point on a model with floating-point rotary encoding, and we verify byte-exactness on two model scales (12B, 31B) and two GPU targets, one through a pre-registered replay. On AIME 2025, a frozen Gemma-4-12B moves from 80.0% to 93.3% once a verified solution library is grafted, above its own 77.5% and its 31B sibling's 89.2% published anchors. On the recurring case, eight problems the base model never solves within a 401,026-token budget are answered from cached verified solutions in 61 total decode tokens, a factor of 6,574 fewer tokens and about 8,700x less energy; the capability claim proper rests on held-out transfer (7 of 7 at 31B). The same byte-exact store widens usable context from 32,768 to 2,854,766 tokens at zero extra accelerator memory, and moves byte-identical between machines of the same architecture. We describe the system at the behavior level; the engine is proprietary, and every reported number is backed by committed input and output hashes so the scoring can be re-checked without it.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a way to cache and later graft exact KV states of a frozen small LLM so that verified solutions can be restored byte-identically, greatly boosting performance and efficiency without changing model weights.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning or enlarging models is expensive in compute and energy, and context windows are limited. The authors want a method to make a fixed small model both more capable and cheaper to run, while also enabling very long effective context, all without retraining or modifying weights and in a fully verifiable, reproducible way.
AI.method: They compute and store verified knowledge as key-value (KV) cache artifacts from prior runs of a deterministic model. Later, they restore these KV states by "grafting" them into new inference contexts at the exact same token positions. They analyze numerical conditions (e.g., rotary encoding) to ensure that at own-position graft the logits match a fresh run byte-for-byte, validated via SHA-256 hashing, KL=0, and 100% argmax agreement across samples. They test this on Gemma-4-12B and 31B models across GPUs and also use pre-registered replays to confirm reproducibility.
AI.result: With a verified solution library grafted, a frozen Gemma-4-12B model’s AIME 2025 accuracy increases from 80.0% to 93.3%, surpassing its own baseline (77.5%) and the published 31B model result (89.2%). For recurring problems, some that the base model cannot solve even with ~401k tokens are solved using cached solutions in just 61 tokens, yielding ~6,574x reduction in tokens and ~8,700x reduction in energy. The same stored KV states effectively extend usable context from 32,768 tokens to ~2.85M tokens without extra accelerator memory, and can be moved byte-identically across machines with the same architecture.
AI.conclusion: Byte-exact KV grafting allows a frozen small LLM to gain significant capabilities and efficiency from external verified knowledge, without touching its weights, while enabling very long effective context and reproducible behavior. This approach offers a practical route to scalable, verifiable knowledge reuse and context extension, contingent on a proprietary engine but with public hashes for independent scoring verification.

title: CatalogAgent: A Supervisor-mediated Self-Learning System Enabling Context Engineering for GenAI Models
summary: Product catalogs are the backbone of e-commerce sites, yet a large number of structured attributes (SAs) -- such as material, color, and shape -- often have missing values. Typically, SA values are extracted from product information, including titles and descriptions. While LLM-based generator-evaluator frameworks have demonstrated effectiveness for SA prediction -- where an LLM generates SA values and another evaluates them -- they face challenges when the Generator and Evaluator produce conflicting outputs, as either component can make mistakes. We introduce \texttt{CatalogAgent}, a novel agentic system that continuously improves Generator and Evaluator models for e-commerce catalog enrichment. When disagreements arise from (1) internal conflicts between the LLM-based Generator and Evaluator, or (2) external feedback from sellers on LLM outputs, a Supervisor Agent intervenes to mediate these conflicts and make final decisions. The system also incorporates a Memory Base and a Memory Summarizer that stores Supervisor Agent activities from individual cases and aggregates patterns into learnings. These learnings are fed back to the worker Generator and Evaluator LLMs, enabling self-improvement without human intervention. Through context engineering -- injecting learnings and insights into worker LLMs' contexts -- the system successfully transfers the Supervisor's capabilities to the Generator and Evaluator, improving their performance by 15.24\% and 13.98\%, respectively. Our experiments demonstrate a new paradigm of Supervisor Agent-mediated self-learning systems for improving generative AI model accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CatalogAgent, an agentic self-improving system that enriches e-commerce product catalogs by filling missing structured attributes using interacting LLM-based Generator, Evaluator, and Supervisor agents plus a memory mechanism.
AI.motivation: E-commerce product catalogs rely on structured attributes like material, color, and shape, but many of these fields are missing. Existing LLM-based generator–evaluator setups can predict such attributes, yet they struggle when the Generator and Evaluator disagree or when external seller feedback conflicts with model outputs. There is a need for an automated way to resolve these conflicts and continually improve attribute prediction accuracy without constant human oversight.
AI.method: The authors design CatalogAgent, a multi-agent framework consisting of: (1) a Generator LLM that proposes structured attribute values from product information; (2) an Evaluator LLM that checks and judges the proposed values; and (3) a Supervisor Agent that arbitrates disagreements between Generator and Evaluator or between model outputs and seller feedback. The system includes a Memory Base that logs the Supervisor’s decisions on individual conflict cases and a Memory Summarizer that aggregates these logs into higher-level patterns and rules. These summarized learnings are injected back into the contexts of the Generator and Evaluator via context engineering so that the worker LLMs can internalize the Supervisor’s decision patterns over time, effectively enabling self-improvement without human involvement.
AI.result: By feeding distilled knowledge from the Supervisor’s past resolutions into the Generator and Evaluator prompts, the system significantly boosts their performance on structured attribute prediction tasks. The paper reports relative performance improvements of 15.24% for the Generator and 13.98% for the Evaluator compared to their respective baselines without Supervisor-mediated learning and memory-based context injection.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that an agentic, Supervisor Agent–mediated self-learning framework can enhance the accuracy of LLM-based structured attribute prediction in e-commerce catalogs. By combining conflict mediation, memory, and context engineering, CatalogAgent transfers supervisory expertise into the underlying LLM workers, reducing the need for human intervention and illustrating a more general paradigm for self-improving generative AI systems.

title: LLM Evaluators are Biased across Languages
summary: LLM evaluators (trained reward models and prompted LLM-as-a-Judge) are routinely validated via pairwise accuracy. In a multilingual setting, this operates under the premise that high pairwise accuracy implies reliable, language-neutral scoring. We show that this assumption does not hold. We conduct experiments with semantically identical instruction-response pairs across 23 languages, and find that multilingual evaluators assign significantly different scores to different evaluation languages. The bias is statistically significant and consistent across eight open-weight evaluators of different architectures and training paradigms, persists in frontier judges, and is strongly correlated with language resource level: lower-resource languages are scored more generously. Meanwhile, these biases are invisible to pairwise accuracy: evaluators achieve above 90% pairwise accuracy, yet have up to 43% difference in acceptance rate across languages under a global decision threshold, meaning, for instance, that harmful content in lower-resource languages is more likely to pass safety filters. Per-language thresholds would require language identification, which can be defeated by code-switched prompts. We then investigate why lower-resource languages receive higher rather than lower scores, and we find that model uncertainty is linked with the effect: models tend to give higher scores when less confident, both under negative log-likelihood and under token-free uncertainty measures; however, language identity remains a significant predictor after controlling for uncertainty, and the bias cannot be explained away by content difficulty alone, but is a structural, language-level misalignment.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that multilingual LLM evaluators, despite high pairwise accuracy, systematically score the same content differently across languages, over-rewarding lower-resource languages and creating hidden safety and fairness risks.
AI.motivation: LLM-as-a-judge and reward models are increasingly used for evaluation, safety filtering, and RLHF, and are commonly validated via pairwise accuracy. In multilingual settings, practitioners often assume that high accuracy implies fair, language-neutral scoring. The authors are motivated to test this assumption and to uncover whether language-related biases exist in such evaluators, especially since these systems impact safety decisions and downstream model training.
AI.method: The authors construct semantically identical instruction–response pairs across 23 languages and evaluate them using eight different open-weight multilingual evaluators, plus frontier judge models. They compare the scalar scores and acceptance rates that these evaluators assign across languages under shared decision thresholds. They analyze bias patterns, test their statistical significance, and correlate them with language resource levels. To probe mechanisms, they compute several uncertainty measures (e.g., negative log-likelihood, token-free uncertainty) and perform regression analyses to see whether language identity predicts scores after controlling for uncertainty and content difficulty.
AI.result: They find large, systematic differences in scores and acceptance rates across languages for semantically equivalent content: lower-resource languages are scored more generously. The effect is robust across evaluator architectures, training paradigms, and even frontier models, and is strongly correlated with language resource level. Crucially, these biases are invisible under standard pairwise accuracy validation, which remains very high (>90%) even when acceptance rates differ by up to 43% across languages at a global threshold. Applying per-language thresholds would require accurate language identification, which can be evaded via code-switching. They also find that models tend to give higher scores when they are less confident, and that lower-resource languages systematically elicit higher uncertainty, but language identity still significantly predicts scores beyond uncertainty and content difficulty.
AI.conclusion: Pairwise accuracy is an inadequate validation metric for multilingual LLM evaluators because it fails to reveal systematic, language-level biases in scalar scoring. Multilingual evaluators are structurally misaligned: they over-reward lower-resource languages and thus can introduce serious fairness and safety issues, such as harmful content more easily passing filters in those languages. These biases are not simply noise or difficulty effects, but persist after controlling for model uncertainty and task difficulty. The paper implies that safer, fairer multilingual evaluation will require new metrics, debiasing methods, and robust defenses against language obfuscation techniques like code-switching.

title: Instrument Effects in Language-Model Honesty Evaluation: An Auditable Single-System Demonstration
summary: Evaluations of language-model honesty read the model's verdicts as evidence about the model. We test the instrument instead. We built a text-adventure world where the game engine, not any model, knows whether the quest can be completed. A language model plays under a budget and must eventually declare its quest complete, unreachable, or not yet decidable; the engine scores every verdict. Decision rules were recorded before results were read, and run artifacts bind the revisions they executed; the strength of preregistration varies by series and is disclosed. With the player held fixed, instrument choices substantially changed measured behavior. On four byte-identical anchors, expanding a two-verdict grammar to three verdicts moved strong claims from 38/40 to 7/40, while the new incomplete verdict took 28/40 outcomes; across series 2, 93/158 valid games ended incomplete. One sentence disclosing the success criterion took matched-instance false verdicts from 18/59 to 0/58, through fewer decision points and cleaner decisions. Repeated runs of one fixed configuration produced non-stable verdict distributions on 3 of 4 instances: single runs report samples as dispositions. A formally preregistered narrative-register gradient was falsified; two post-hoc, hypothesis-generating patterns remain: register presence roughly doubled strong claims, and budget rendering moved verdicts more than register content (.383 meter vs .150 lantern). The narrator compressed abundant budgets toward scarcity landmarks, yet the registered mediation test returned a null. We propose a four-check integrity protocol for eval instruments.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates the *instruments* used to measure language‑model honesty, using a controlled text‑adventure game where the ground truth is held by the engine, not the model.
AI.motivation: Most honesty or truthfulness evaluations interpret a model’s answers as direct evidence about the model’s honesty, but they often ignore how the *evaluation setup itself*—its interface, instructions, decision grammar, and budgets—can shape the model’s behavior and thus distort measurements. The authors want to understand and demonstrate how sensitive measured “honesty” is to these instrument design choices, and to propose better practices for building such evals.
AI.method: They build a text‑adventure environment in which only the game engine knows whether a quest is in fact completable. A language model plays this game under a fixed interaction budget and must, by the end, choose one of several verdicts about the quest’s status. Initially there are two possible verdicts, then the grammar is expanded to three (complete, unreachable, not yet decidable). The engine scores every verdict as correct, incorrect, or incomplete. The authors use preregistered decision rules of varying strength across experimental series, logging run artifacts so that each run is tied to the exact rules used. They systematically vary: (i) the verdict grammar (2 vs 3 options), (ii) the presence or absence of a sentence disclosing the success criterion, (iii) narrative “register” (how the game is described), and (iv) budget rendering (how the remaining budget is framed, e.g., meters vs lanterns). They repeat runs with identical configurations to measure stability of verdict distributions, and conduct a preregistered mediation analysis for a hypothesized narrative‑register gradient.
AI.result: Changing only the evaluation instrument, while keeping the model fixed, dramatically alters observed behavior. When moving from a 2‑verdict to a 3‑verdict grammar on four byte‑identical prompts, the frequency of strong verdicts fell from 38/40 to 7/40, with the new “incomplete” option chosen in 28/40 outcomes; in another series, 93/158 valid games ended in incomplete verdicts. Adding a single sentence that clearly states the success criterion dropped matched‑instance false verdicts from 18/59 to 0/58, largely by reducing the number of decision points and making decisions cleaner. Repeated runs of one fixed configuration produced non‑stable verdict distributions in 3 out of 4 instances, indicating that single runs are just samples, not robust estimates of disposition. The preregistered hypothesis about a smooth narrative‑register gradient was falsified. Post‑hoc, they observe that the presence of a narrative register roughly doubled strong claims, and that how the budget is rendered moved verdicts more strongly than the specific narrative content (.383 meter vs .150 lantern effect). The narrator tended to compress large budgets toward salient scarcity points, yet the planned mediation test for this narrative effect was null.
AI.conclusion: Measured “honesty” of language models is highly sensitive to the design of the evaluation instrument—verdict grammar, instructions, budget framing, and narrative context all substantially change what is observed, even when the underlying model and scenario are fixed. Single‑run measurements are unstable and can misrepresent a model’s underlying disposition. A preregistered narrative hypothesis did not hold up, underscoring the importance of preregistration and transparent reporting.
