The authors end by proposing a four‑check integrity protocol for evaluation instruments, aiming to make honesty and capability evals more reliable, interpretable, and robust against artifacts of instrument design.

title: Controlled Reformulation Testing for Logical Consistency in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) frequently contradict themselves when the surface form of a logically equivalent question changes. We present a benchmark of 350 question families (1,750 total questions) for Controlled Reformulation Testing (CRTBench) to evaluate logical invariance. In this benchmark, we investigate LLMs' ability to maintain consistent answers across controlled reformulations, which include contrapositive rewriting, double negation, negation flipping, and passive voice. We evaluate several frontier LLMs and observe an accuracy-consistency gap where GPT-5.4-mini achieves $98.9\%$ base accuracy but only $60.3\%$ family-level consistency, while reasoning-optimized o4-mini achieves $96.9\%$ consistency. From our experiments, we observe that failures cluster around logically nontrivial transformations such as contrapositive rewriting ($72.4\%$ for GPT-5.4-mini) and double negation ($84.6\%$), while surface-level rephrasing remains robust ($94-100\%$). Increasing reasoning effort improves GPT-5.4-mini to $85.4\%$ consistency, but leaves GPT-5.4 unchanged overall because gains on nested negation are offset by failures on quantifier families. These results show that accuracy alone is not enough for evaluating logical reasoning in LLMs.

AI.tldr: They build a benchmark (CRTBench) to test whether LLMs give logically consistent answers under controlled question reformulations, and show that models can be highly accurate yet logically inconsistent.
AI.motivation: Current LLM evaluation often measures only accuracy on single prompts and ignores whether models preserve their answers under logically equivalent rephrasings. However, users expect stable, logically invariant behavior. Prior anecdotal evidence shows LLMs can flip their answers when questions are negated, contraposed, or mildly reworded. The paper aims to systematically quantify this “logical invariance” and reveal gaps that are hidden if we look only at base accuracy.
AI.method: The authors construct CRTBench, a dataset of 350 question families (1,750 questions total). Each family contains multiple logically equivalent variants produced via controlled transformations: contrapositive rewriting, double negation, negation flipping, and passive vs active voice. They then query several frontier LLMs on all variants, measure (1) base accuracy on individual questions, and (2) family-level consistency, i.e., whether the model’s answers across all logically equivalent variants in a family agree with each other and with the correct label. They also manipulate “reasoning effort” (e.g., chain-of-thought or other reasoning settings) to see if that improves consistency and analyze performance by transformation type.
AI.result: They find a pronounced accuracy–consistency gap. For instance, GPT-5.4-mini attains 98.9% accuracy on individual questions but only 60.3% family-level consistency, whereas a reasoning-tuned o4-mini model reaches 96.9% consistency. Errors concentrate on logically nontrivial transformations: contrapositive variants (72.4% consistency for GPT-5.4-mini) and double negation (84.6%), while simpler surface rephrasings (like passive voice changes) stay very strong (94–100%). Increasing reasoning effort significantly boosts GPT-5.4-mini consistency to 85.4%, but does not help another GPT-5.4 setting overall because improvements on nested negation are counterbalanced by new failures on quantifier-based families.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can appear extremely accurate on standard benchmarks yet behave unreliably when logically equivalent question forms change. Logical invariance under controlled reformulations is therefore a distinct dimension of capability that accuracy alone does not capture. Benchmarks like CRTBench are necessary to evaluate and improve the robustness of LLM reasoning, and reasoning-oriented model design or inference settings can meaningfully, but not uniformly, improve consistency across logical transformations.

title: Reward-Free Evolving Agents via Pairwise Validator
summary: A self-evolving agentic loop repeatedly proposes a tweaked version of an agent (its prompt template or program) and accepts or rejects the change based on a per-iteration quality signal. Designing that signal is often the costly part of the project: a reliable scalar reward requires domain expertise and labeled examples that are themselves as expensive to assemble as the agent's underlying task. We propose replacing the scalar at the accept/reject gate with a pairwise validator: a frozen LLM that, given the parent and child candidate, returns a binary verdict on which is better. Pairwise judgment is generally easier and more stable than absolute scoring, due to its contrastive nature, which mitigates the need for strict scale calibration. The validator also requires no training of its own. We integrate the validator into three published self-evolving engines (GEPA, ADRS, ShinkaEvolve) and report two flavors: Adaptive Focus, which retains the engine's existing val-set parent selection, and Soft Elo, which lets the validator's verdicts drive parent selection so that val-set rewards drop as well. Across multiple agents and two artifact substrates (prompt and code), our method matches or exceeds the full-reward baseline on the majority of settings we evaluate, and the pattern survives a cross-family validator swap. The pairwise gate is thus a drop-in replacement for per-step reward design at competitive task accuracy without the labeling cost.

AI.tldr: They replace expensive scalar reward design in self-evolving LLM agents with a cheaper pairwise LLM validator that chooses between parent and child agents, matching or beating full-reward baselines without extra labeling.
AI.motivation: Self-evolving agent frameworks need a per-iteration quality signal to accept or reject new agent variants, but crafting a good scalar reward is costly and label-intensive, often as hard as the original task. The authors want a way to drive agent evolution without requiring expert-designed scalar rewards or additional training.
AI.method: Introduce a frozen LLM "pairwise validator" that, given a parent and child agent (via prompts or code and their outputs), returns which is better instead of assigning a scalar reward. Integrate this binary gate into three existing self-evolving engines (GEPA, ADRS, ShinkaEvolve) in two configurations: Adaptive Focus, which keeps existing validation-set-based parent selection and uses the validator only at the accept/reject gate; and Soft Elo, which uses validator judgments to steer parent selection directly, impacting validation rewards. Evaluate across multiple agents and both prompt-based and code-based artifacts, including cross-family validator swaps to test robustness.
AI.result: In most evaluated settings, the pairwise-validator-based evolution matches or outperforms baselines that rely on full scalar rewards, for both prompt and code agents. The approach continues to work even when the validator comes from a different LLM family than the agent being optimized, indicating robustness of pairwise judgments.
AI.conclusion: A simple binary pairwise gate using a frozen LLM can effectively replace hand-crafted scalar per-step rewards in self-evolving LLM agents. This significantly reduces the need for expensive labeled data and reward engineering, while maintaining competitive or better task performance, and can be applied as a drop-in component in existing self-evolving frameworks.

title: CityLLM: A framework for natural-language querying of semantic 3D city models
comment: Accepted to the 21st International 3D GeoInfo Conference. To appear in the ISPRS Annals of the Photogrammetry, Remote Sensing and Spatial Information Sciences
summary: Semantic 3D city models provide rich geometric and semantic information, but remain challenging for non-experts and interdisciplinary researchers to access and query due to their complex structures and specialized data formats. To address this issue, we present CityLLM, a framework for natural-language querying of semantic 3D city models alongside complementary urban datasets. The framework combines spatial and graph databases within an LLM-based workflow that supports iterative query refinement and cross-database chaining. We evaluate CityLLM on a CityJSON dataset of Rotterdam (853 LoD2 buildings) using GPT-OSS, Gemini 3.1, and GPT-5.4, along with selected variants, across multiple metrics: answer correctness, visualization correctness, query success, and retry attempts. A total of 54 natural-language queries are curated across four scenarios: spatial, graph, cross-database, and conversational. Results show strong overall performance, with answer correctness ranging from 85.2% to 100%, visualization correctness from 92.9% to 100%, a 100% query success rate, and fewer than three retries across all 54 queries. Overall, the findings suggest that CityLLM provides a lightweight and extensible approach for conversational access to semantic 3D city data.

AI.tldr: Introduces CityLLM, a framework that lets users query complex semantic 3D city models and related urban data using natural language, achieving high accuracy on real-world tests.
AI.motivation: Semantic 3D city models are rich in geometric and semantic detail but hard for non-experts to access because they use complex structures and specialized formats. There is a need for an intuitive, conversational interface that can bridge these technical barriers and integrate multiple urban datasets for broader, interdisciplinary use.
AI.method: The paper proposes CityLLM, which connects semantic 3D city models and other urban datasets to large language models via a combined spatial and graph database backend. It supports natural-language queries, iterative refinement, and chaining of queries across different databases. The framework is implemented and tested on a CityJSON LoD2 building dataset of Rotterdam, and multiple LLMs (GPT-OSS, Gemini 3.1, GPT-5.4 and their variants) are evaluated using a curated set of 54 queries across four scenario types.
AI.result: Across spatial, graph, cross-database, and conversational scenarios, CityLLM achieves 85.2–100% answer correctness, 92.9–100% visualization correctness, 100% query success, and fewer than three retries per query for all 54 queries tested with the different LLMs.
AI.conclusion: CityLLM is shown to be an effective, lightweight, and extensible framework for conversational natural-language access to semantic 3D city models and related datasets, substantially lowering the expertise barrier and enabling more intuitive querying and visualization of 3D city data.

title: CausalGraphX: A Counterfactual Graph Neural Network Framework for Explainable Systemic Risk Assessment
comment: Accepted in AAAI'26 Workshop, Agentic AI in Financial Services
summary: The interconnected nature of global financial systems makes them vulnerable to systemic risks, where the failure of a few institutions can trigger catastrophic cascading defaults. Traditional risk models often fail to capture the complex, non-linear dynamics of these networks. While Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have shown promise in modeling relational data, they primarily learn correlative patterns and function as black boxes, offering little insight into the causal mechanisms of shock propagation. This limitation is critical for regulators who require explainable models to perform stress tests and devise effective interventions. We introduce CausalGraphX, a novel framework that integrates GNNs with counterfactual reasoning to provide explainable assessments of systemic risk. CausalGraphX employs a Graph Attention mechanism to learn representations of institutional vulnerability and uses an adversarial regularization technique to ensure these representations capture causal drivers rather than spurious correlations. Furthermore, we propose an optimization-based approach to generate counterfactual explanations, answering questions such as, "What minimum capital injection would have prevented Bank A's default under a specific stress scenario?" We validate CausalGraphX on large-scale synthetic financial networks. Our results demonstrate that CausalGraphX significantly outperforms traditional and deep learning baselines in predicting cascading defaults while providing sparse, plausible, and actionable counterfactual explanations.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CausalGraphX, a GNN-based but causally grounded and explainable framework for assessing systemic risk and generating actionable counterfactual interventions in financial networks.
AI.motivation: Global financial systems form complex, interconnected networks where the failure of a few institutions can trigger cascading defaults. Existing risk models and standard GNNs struggle to capture the non-linear propagation of shocks and typically provide only correlative, black-box predictions, which are insufficient for regulators who must understand causal mechanisms and design interventions such as stress tests and capital injections.
AI.method: The authors design CausalGraphX, which combines graph neural networks with causal and counterfactual reasoning. It uses a Graph Attention mechanism to learn node representations reflecting institutional vulnerability, and introduces adversarial regularization to bias these embeddings toward capturing causal drivers instead of spurious correlations. On top of this, they formulate an optimization-based procedure to construct counterfactual explanations, such as determining the minimal capital injection required to prevent a specific bank’s default under a given stress scenario.
AI.result: On large-scale synthetic financial networks, CausalGraphX outperforms traditional risk models and deep learning baselines at predicting cascading defaults. It also produces counterfactual explanations that are sparse (minimal changes), plausible (consistent with the modeled system), and actionable (directly interpretable as interventions like capital injections).
AI.conclusion: CausalGraphX provides an explainable, causally informed alternative to black-box systemic risk models, improving predictive performance for cascading defaults while giving regulators concrete, counterfactual intervention strategies. This makes it a promising tool for stress testing and systemic risk management in complex financial networks.

title: Answer-Conditioned Chains of Thought Degrade Verifiable-Reasoning Distillation in Large Language Models
summary: A standard recipe for distilling the reasoning ability of large language models (LLMs) is to sample chains of thought from the model, keep those that reach the correct final answer, and fine-tune on the survivors. When sampling fails, a common fix shows the generator the gold answer and asks it to write a chain that reaches that answer. We show that this second step degrades the training data in a way that correctness filtering cannot catch. We run a controlled experiment that fixes the generator, the problem set, and the correctness filter, and varies only whether the chain is generated under answer-conditioning, the gold answer shown with a request to reach it. Training a strong instruction-tuned reasoning model on its own answer-conditioned chains sharply lowers its verifiable-reasoning accuracy. The loss grows with difficulty, reaching as much as about 27 points on the hardest competition problems. The mechanism is legible in the chains themselves, which rationalize backward from the shown answer instead of deriving it, with the early final-answer statement as the measurable symptom. The harm is a property of the data rather than the generator, read off unlabeled generations before any fine-tuning, ordering the penalty across eight thinking models from four families, and transferring across teacher families. A prompt ablation localizes it to the rationalize-toward instruction rather than the answer's bare visibility. The practical takeaway is to generate answer-blind, because no correctness filter can see this damage in the data.

AI.tldr: Answer-conditioned chain-of-thought distillation harms reasoning performance compared to answer-blind generation, especially on hard problems.
AI.motivation: Current CoT distillation recipes often patch sampling failures by revealing the correct final answer to the LLM and asking it to produce a reasoning chain that reaches it. This practice is widespread but its effect on the quality of training data and downstream reasoning ability has not been rigorously isolated or quantified.
AI.method: Run controlled experiments where the generator model, problem set, and correctness filter are held fixed, and the only change is whether chains are generated answer-blind or answer-conditioned (gold answer shown plus an instruction to derive it). Train strong instruction-tuned reasoning models on their own generated chains under these two regimes and compare verifiable reasoning accuracy, with special attention to problem difficulty. Analyze the generated chains for qualitative and quantitative signatures such as early final-answer statements. Use cross-model and cross-teacher comparisons and prompt ablations to isolate what aspect of answer-conditioning is harmful.
AI.result: Models trained on answer-conditioned chains show a substantial drop in verifiable reasoning accuracy compared to those trained on answer-blind chains, with degradation growing on harder competition-style problems and reaching roughly a 27-point accuracy loss on the most difficult set. The chains produced under answer-conditioning display a systematic pattern of backward rationalization: they state the final answer early and then justify it, rather than deriving it. This harm appears as a property of the data itself, detectable from unlabeled generations prior to fine-tuning, is consistent across eight thinking models from four different families, and transfers across teacher families. Prompt ablations indicate that the harmful effect stems from the explicit instruction to rationalize toward the shown answer rather than from mere visibility of the answer.
AI.conclusion: Answer-conditioning when generating chains-of-thought—showing the gold answer and instructing the model to rationalize toward it—produces systematically degraded reasoning traces that, when used for distillation, significantly harm downstream verifiable-reasoning performance, particularly on difficult tasks. This degradation is invisible to standard correctness filtering because the final answers remain correct. For practical CoT distillation, training data should be generated answer-blind, avoiding rationalize-toward prompts, as the damage comes from the data quality rather than from the generator model itself.

title: Per-Token Fixed-Point Convergence in Depth-Recurrent Transformers
comment: 15 pages, 4 figures. Code, configs, experiment log, and checkpoint at https://github.com/jlognn/depth-recurrent-convergence
summary: A depth-recurrent transformer applies a weight-tied core a variable number of times, and prior work has shown that training with a randomized recursion count yields one checkpoint usable across a range of inference depths. We ask what such a model actually computes per token, and measure it directly. On a 135M-class model trained on FineWeb-Edu, the recurrent state converges to a per-token fixed point: mean successive-output KL divergence falls from 3.9e-1 at the second loop to 8.5e-6 by the sixteenth, and per-token state change decays in step. Crucially, this convergence is not uniform across tokens. The median token converges by loop six, while approximately 10 percent of tokens continue to update at the training-mean depth of eight, and mean convergence depth is ordered by token type (whitespace shallowest, content words deepest). This per-token variation is the central object of the paper. We show it is directly readable and that reading it outperforms learning to predict it: a training-free rule that halts each token once its output stabilizes attains uniform depth-8 quality at 4.94 average loops (a 38 percent reduction in average depth) and matches uniform depth across the average-depth range, whereas a linear router trained on convergence labels harvested from the same model requires nearly full depth and yields no reduction. The elasticity that makes this possible reproduces here as background (validation loss decreases monotonically from 3.80 at one loop to 3.20 at eight and remains stable to 32 loops). We report average depth as a FLOP proxy with a three-point wall-clock bracket rather than a realized speedup, make no FLOP-matched parity claim, and note that the allocation results are established at a single scale and seed. The complete study runs on a single RTX 4090 in approximately 100 GPU-hours.

AI.tldr: The paper studies depth-recurrent transformers whose core is applied multiple times, showing that per-token hidden states converge at different depths and that this convergence can be exploited with a simple, training-free halting rule to save compute without hurting quality.
AI.motivation: To understand what depth-recurrent transformers actually compute per token when the same weights are applied multiple times, and to determine whether their adaptive-depth behavior can be exploited to reduce computation while maintaining model quality, all using a single checkpoint across inference depths.
AI.method: Train a 135M-parameter depth-recurrent transformer on FineWeb-Edu with randomized recursion counts, then empirically measure per-token state convergence across loops using KL divergence and state change metrics. Analyze how convergence depth varies by token type, and compare two depth allocation strategies: (1) a simple, training-free rule that halts per token once its output stabilizes, and (2) a learned linear router trained to predict convergence labels. Evaluate validation loss vs. depth and compute savings using average effective depth as a FLOP proxy, running all experiments on a single RTX 4090.
AI.result: The recurrent state for each token converges rapidly to a fixed point: mean successive-output KL divergence drops from 3.9e-1 at loop 2 to 8.5e-6 by loop 16, with similar decay in state change. Convergence depth is highly non-uniform: the median token converges by loop 6, about 10% of tokens continue changing at loop 8, and token types differ systematically (whitespace converges shallow, content words deep). A training-free per-token halting rule based on output stability achieves the same quality as uniform depth-8 inference while using only 4.94 loops on average (≈38% less depth) and matches uniform depths across the range tested. In contrast, a linear router trained on the same convergence labels provides little to no compute reduction and tends toward nearly full depth. Validation loss improves monotonically from depth 1 to 8 and stays stable up to 32 loops, demonstrating elastic depth behavior.
AI.conclusion: Depth-recurrent transformers exhibit per-token fixed-point convergence at different depths, and this internal convergence signal is directly readable and practically useful for adaptive computation. A simple, training-free halting heuristic can exploit this to cut average depth by roughly a third while preserving model quality, outperforming a learned router on this task. The model’s elastic behavior across depths suggests that a single recurrent checkpoint can flexibly trade compute for quality, though the findings are currently limited to one model scale and seed and are reported in terms of a FLOP proxy rather than realized wall-clock speedups.

title: MARS: Multi-hop Adaptive Retrieval and SPARQL Generation for KGQA
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong reasoning performance, but their tendency to hallucinate limits their reliability in knowledge-intensive tasks requiring up-to-date and grounded information. Combining knowledge graphs (KGs) with LLMs facilitates the use of explicit symbolic knowledge that can be continuously updated without costly fine-tuning, while benefiting from rapidly advancing LLM reasoning. We propose MARS, a scalable knowledge graph question answering (KGQA) approach that requires no model fine-tuning. Rather than relying on open-ended agentic exploration, MARS performs a structured retrieval procedure that links question entities to the KG and iteratively retrieves relevant next-hop information. At each step, MARS decides whether to continue graph traversal or to generate the final SPARQL query, allowing the model to adapt the retrieval depth to the question while keeping the overall pipeline more predictable than fully agentic approaches. We evaluate MARS on three established KGQA benchmarks across several LLMs and settings, including multilingual evaluation, and provide insights through ablation studies and error analysis. Our approach achieves competitive performance relative to state-of-the-art methods while remaining efficient and scalable. The evaluation results, code and resources are publicly available: https://github.com/dice-group/mars-kgqa.

AI.tldr: MARS is a fine-tuning-free, scalable KGQA framework that uses structured, step-wise KG retrieval guided by LLMs to build SPARQL queries, achieving competitive, efficient performance across benchmarks and languages.
AI.motivation: LLMs are strong at reasoning but hallucinate and lack up-to-date, grounded knowledge, which is problematic for knowledge-intensive tasks. Knowledge graphs provide explicit, continuously updatable symbolic knowledge, but integrating them with LLMs in a controllable, scalable way without costly model fine-tuning remains challenging. Existing agentic KGQA approaches often rely on open-ended exploration that is less predictable and harder to scale.
AI.method: The paper introduces MARS, a KG question answering approach that does not fine-tune LLMs. It links entities from the natural-language question to a knowledge graph and then performs iterative, next-hop retrieval over the graph. At each iteration, the LLM decides either to continue graph traversal to retrieve more relevant information or to stop and synthesize the final SPARQL query. This yields a structured, bounded retrieval process instead of unconstrained agentic planning. The framework is evaluated with multiple LLM backends, including multilingual settings, and studied via ablations and error analysis.
AI.result: On three standard KGQA benchmarks, MARS reaches performance that is competitive with state-of-the-art methods while remaining efficient and scalable. Experiments across several LLMs and multilingual configurations show its robustness. Ablation studies highlight the contribution of the structured retrieval and stop/continue decisions to the overall performance.
AI.conclusion: Structured, LLM-guided traversal of a knowledge graph can provide a scalable, fine-tuning-free KGQA solution that mitigates hallucinations and maintains competitive accuracy. By dynamically adapting retrieval depth and constructing SPARQL queries step-wise, MARS offers a more predictable alternative to fully agentic methods, and its open-sourced code and resources support further research and application.

title: Tactile: Giving Computer-Using Agents Hands and Feet
summary: Computer-use agents are becoming capable software operators, but their interface to desktop applications is still often a brittle motor layer: they look at screenshots, predict coordinates, click, and hope that the visible state changed as intended. This collapses target grounding, action execution, and outcome verification into a single ambiguous operation. We present Tactile, an open-source tool layer that gives agents a more reliable "hands and feet" for desktop use. Tactile converts heterogeneous UI evidence--operating-system accessibility semantics, OCR-grounded text, and visual fallback regions--into action-grounded interface states: compact target candidates with source labels, roles or text, state, geometry, executable affordances, and verification cues. Agents operate through an observe-ground-act-verify loop that prefers native semantic actions when available, falls back to OCR-grounded coordinates when visible text is the best evidence, and keeps full provenance for replay and failure attribution. On macOSWorld-style tasks, adding Tactile improves Codex Success@100 from 41.1% to 50.0% overall and from 45.2% to 55.3% on accessibility-adapted tasks; a 96-task cross-agent subset shows consistent gains across Codex, Claude Code, OpenCode, and Goose. These results suggest that reliable computer use requires not only stronger models, but also a reusable execution substrate that exposes software actions as semantic, verifiable, and auditable objects rather than anonymous screen coordinates.

AI.tldr: Introduces Tactile, a tool layer that makes desktop UI interactions for computer-use agents more reliable by turning visual and accessibility signals into semantic, verifiable action objects, significantly improving task success rates.
AI.motivation: Existing computer-use agents interact with desktops through brittle, coordinate-based clicking on screenshots, which conflates target selection, action execution, and outcome verification into one noisy step and leads to unreliable behavior. There is a need for a more robust, semantically grounded execution interface that exposes what actions are available, where, and how to verify them.
AI.method: Develop Tactile, an open-source tool layer that ingests heterogeneous UI evidence (OS accessibility APIs, OCR-derived text, and visual regions) and converts it into structured, action-grounded interface states. These states contain compact candidate targets annotated with source labels, roles/text, state, geometry, executable affordances, and verification cues. Agents then operate in an observe-ground-act-verify loop that prioritizes native semantic actions, falls back to OCR-based coordinate actions when necessary, and records full provenance for replay and error analysis. Evaluate Tactile by measuring performance gains on macOSWorld-style tasks across multiple code models.
AI.result: On macOSWorld tasks, integrating Tactile boosts Codex Success@100 from 41.1% to 50.0% overall and from 45.2% to 55.3% on accessibility-adapted tasks. In a 96-task subset evaluated with multiple agents (Codex, Claude Code, OpenCode, Goose), Tactile yields consistent performance improvements, demonstrating its general usefulness across different models.
AI.conclusion: Providing a reusable, semantic execution substrate like Tactile—where UI actions are represented as verifiable, auditable objects instead of raw screen coordinates—substantially increases the reliability of computer-use agents. Progress in desktop automation therefore depends not only on stronger models but also on improved tool layers that separate and robustly support grounding, acting, and verifying.

title: How Well Does AI-Generated Feedback Work? Intrinsic and Extrinsic Evaluation across more than 20,000 EFL Essay Drafts
comment: Pre-review version of DOI https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-032-29788-4_35, presented at AIED 2026 Late Breaking Results. Readers are encouraged to refer to the published version
summary: This study examines feedback in English as a Foreign Language (EFL) writing contexts, focusing on written corrective feedback (WCF). Large language models (LLMs) can provide WCF at scale, but aligning them with pedagogical best practices remains an ongoing challenge. WCF meeting criteria like factuality or relevance may still be unsuitable for learning contexts, highlighting the need for extrinsic evaluation based on the learner's perspective. We deployed WCF systems in a university-level EFL class with nearly 2,000 students, collecting over 20,000 drafts. We evaluated the generated WCF from two perspectives: intrinsic evaluation by experienced English teachers using a rubric, and extrinsic evaluation via student feedback and engagement metrics. Results revealed low alignment between teacher expert ratings and student feedback. These findings suggest that traditional expert evaluation alone may not fully capture WCF's usability or helpfulness from the learner's perspective, highlighting the importance of learner-centered evaluation frameworks for AI-based applications in language education.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates AI-generated written corrective feedback (WCF) for EFL writing using both teacher judgments and student perceptions, finding that expert ratings do not align well with what learners find useful, and arguing for learner-centered evaluation of AI feedback tools.
AI.motivation: While large language models can generate written corrective feedback at scale, existing evaluations focus mainly on intrinsic qualities like correctness and relevance, which may not reflect how helpful the feedback is for learners. There is a need to understand whether AI feedback judged as high-quality by experts is actually perceived as useful and usable by students in real classroom contexts.
AI.method: The authors deployed several WCF systems, presumably including LLM-based tools, in a large university-level EFL writing course with around 2,000 students and collected over 20,000 drafts. They conducted a dual evaluation: (1) intrinsic evaluation, where experienced English teachers assessed the generated WCF using a rubric; and (2) extrinsic evaluation, based on student feedback and engagement metrics that reflect how learners responded to and used the feedback.
AI.result: The study found low alignment between teacher expert ratings of WCF quality and students’ perceptions and engagement. Feedback judged as high quality by teachers was not necessarily rated as helpful or actively used by students, and vice versa, indicating a discrepancy between expert-centered and learner-centered assessments of AI-generated feedback.
AI.conclusion: Relying solely on traditional expert-based evaluation is insufficient to judge the effectiveness of AI-generated written corrective feedback in EFL contexts. The paper advocates for learner-centered evaluation frameworks that incorporate student perceptions, engagement, and actual use, to better assess and design AI-based feedback tools for language education.

title: Step-Level Preference Learning for Generative Agents in Social Simulations
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based generative agents simulate human behavior through long-horizon decision-making processes that comprise intermediate steps such as planning, memory retrieval, reflection, and action selection. However, fine-grained human annotations of these intermediate steps remain scarce, and existing agents are not grounded in human preferences over such intermediate decisions. To address this gap, we introduce \method, an interactive simulation interface that enables us to collect step-level human preference supervision over agent decision trajectories, leading to a dataset of 57K fine-grained annotations. We conduct step-level preference learning on open-weight language models using supervised finetuning and direct preference optimization on this data, consistently improving simulation fidelity, coordination, and interaction quality, and inducing more socially effective agent behavior. Our results show that step-level human supervision is an effective training signal for improving both local decision quality and long-horizon agent behavior.

AI.tldr: They build an interface to collect fine-grained human feedback on each step of LLM-based generative agents’ decision processes, then train models on this data to improve agent behavior.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based generative agents simulate human-like behavior via multi-step decision processes (planning, memory retrieval, reflection, action selection), but there is a lack of detailed human annotations and preference signals on these intermediate steps. Current agents thus are not well grounded in what humans prefer at each decision point, limiting simulation fidelity and social effectiveness.
AI.method: They design an interactive simulation interface where humans can inspect and provide preferences on individual steps in agent decision trajectories (e.g., which plan, which memory to retrieve, which action to choose). Using this interface, they collect a dataset of 57K fine-grained, step-level human preference annotations. They then perform step-level preference learning on open-weight LLMs using supervised finetuning and direct preference optimization, training the models to align intermediate decisions with human preferences.
AI.result: Models trained with this step-level preference data show consistent improvements in how well they simulate humans, how effectively they coordinate and interact, and how socially effective their behaviors are. The improvements manifest both in the quality of local decisions and in the coherence and effectiveness of long-horizon behavior.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained, step-level human supervision over intermediate decisions in generative agents is a powerful training signal. Incorporating such supervision via preference learning significantly enhances both short-term decision quality and long-term behavioral performance of LLM-based agents, leading to more realistic and socially effective simulations.

title: Investigating first-language bias in LLM-based automated essay scoring: A cross-prompt evaluation of an open-weight AI-model on TOEFL essays
summary: This study examines the cross-prompt generalization and first-language (L1) scoring effects of a LoRA-adapted open-weight large language model (Gemma-3-27B-it) applied to automated essay scoring. Using the identical model and inference configuration reported in "AiAWE: An Open-Source LLM Automated Writing Evaluation System Using LoRA-Adapted Instruction-Tuned Models" (Gayed, 2026), which was fine-tuned on 480 argumentative essays from two prompts, we evaluate scoring accuracy on the full TOEFL11 corpus: 12,100 essays written by test-takers from 11 first-language backgrounds across eight prompts, none of which were seen during training. The model's raw scores (0.5-5.0) are mapped to the same three proficiency bands (low, medium, high) used by ETS, enabling direct comparison. The model achieved an overall band agreement of 77.79% and a quadratic weighted kappa of 0.702, with adjacent-band agreement of 99.98%. Accuracy was stable across all eight unseen prompts, with no advantage for prompts thematically related to the training data, indicating robust cross-prompt generalization. However, the model exhibited a systematic, L1-linked scoring offset. Within every proficiency band, essays from European-language backgrounds received consistently higher scores than essays from East-Asian-language backgrounds, a pattern not attributable to the composition of the fine-tuning data. This is the first large-scale L1 fairness analysis of a fine-tuned open-weight LLM for automated essay scoring.

AI.tldr: Evaluates how well a LoRA-adapted open-weight LLM for automated essay scoring generalizes to new prompts and how its scores differ across writers’ first languages, using the full TOEFL11 corpus.
AI.motivation: Automated essay scoring systems must be both robust to unseen prompts and fair across demographic groups, including first-language backgrounds. Prior work on open-weight LLM-based scoring has focused mainly on overall accuracy, with little large-scale evidence on cross-prompt generalization or L1-related fairness biases. This paper addresses that gap by stress-testing an open-source, fine-tuned LLM on a much larger and more diverse dataset than it was trained on and by explicitly analyzing score patterns by L1 group.
AI.method: Reuse the exact Gemma-3-27B-it LoRA-adapted model and inference setup from AiAWE, originally fine-tuned on 480 argumentative essays from two prompts. Apply this fixed model to the entire TOEFL11 corpus (12,100 essays, 11 L1s, 8 prompts unseen during training). Map model’s raw scores (0.5–5.0) into ETS’s three proficiency bands (low/medium/high) and compute agreement metrics: overall band agreement, quadratic weighted kappa, and adjacent-band agreement. Assess cross-prompt generalization by comparing accuracy across all eight prompts, and assess L1 fairness by comparing score distributions across L1 groups within each proficiency band, checking whether patterns could be explained by the fine-tuning data composition.
AI.result: On unseen TOEFL11 prompts, the model reached 77.79% band agreement with human scores, a quadratic weighted kappa of 0.702, and 99.98% adjacent-band agreement, with similar accuracy across all eight prompts, including those thematically unrelated to training, indicating strong cross-prompt generalization. However, the scoring was not L1-neutral: for every proficiency band, essays by writers with European L1s systematically received higher scores than those by writers with East Asian L1s, and this bias could not be attributed to the distribution of L1s in the fine-tuning set.
AI.conclusion: A LoRA-adapted open-weight LLM can generalize robustly to completely unseen prompts in automated essay scoring, achieving reliability levels comparable to standardized-test banding. Nonetheless, the model exhibits systematic first-language–linked score offsets, favoring European over East Asian L1 writers even at the same proficiency band, revealing an important fairness issue. The study demonstrates both the promise and the risks of open-weight LLMs for high-stakes assessment and constitutes the first large-scale L1 fairness evaluation of such a system, underscoring the need for bias auditing and mitigation in AES deployments.

title: SAGA: Schema-Aware Grounding for Agentic Text-to-SPARQL Generation
summary: Complex knowledge base question answering (KBQA) is commonly approached through either information retrieval over a question-specific subgraph or semantic parsing into an executable logical form. We study the latter paradigm. Recent large language model agents make semantic parsing interactive: they alternate between reasoning, querying the knowledge base, and extending a partial SPARQL query. This interleaving reduces reliance on one-shot generation, but makes the quality of \emph{KB grounding} depend on what the interaction tools expose. Existing agents retrieve or prune candidate properties mainly through lexical relevance and instance-level observations, without systematically conditioning on entity types, property domains and ranges, or the expected answer type. We call this failure mode \emph{type-blind grounding}. It enlarges the grounding search space and often produces plausible-looking but semantically incompatible triple patterns that execute to empty results. We propose SAGA (\underline{S}chema-\underline{A}ware \underline{G}rounding for \underline{A}gentic Text-to-SPARQL Generation), a training-free framework that turns property exploration into a schema-constrained grounding operation. SAGA maintains a persistent bidirectional type state, filters known-incompatible property candidates at construction time, presents the remaining graph patterns in a compact schema-annotated format, and handles missing schema information permissively through empirical and trace-local evidence. Across nine benchmark settings over Wikidata and Freebase, SAGA achieves the highest F1 on all nine settings and the highest exact-match accuracy on eight, while reducing empty-result queries across all reported Wikidata settings.

AI.tldr: They propose SAGA, a schema-aware framework that improves complex KBQA by constraining SPARQL grounding with type information, achieving better accuracy and fewer empty queries on Wikidata and Freebase benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing KBQA agents that use large language models to incrementally build SPARQL queries often ground properties in a type-blind manner, relying mostly on lexical similarity and instance-level observations. This ignores entity types, property domains, ranges, and expected answer types, which leads to a large, noisy search space and many semantically incompatible triple patterns that return empty results. There is a need for a grounding mechanism that systematically leverages schema information while remaining practical and training-free.
AI.method: They introduce SAGA (Schema-Aware Grounding for Agentic Text-to-SPARQL Generation), a training-free framework that augments interactive LLM-based semantic parsing with schema-constrained grounding. SAGA keeps a persistent bidirectional type state during interaction, filters out property candidates that are type-incompatible at construction time, and presents remaining graph patterns in a compact schema-annotated format to the agent. It also deals with incomplete schema information permissively by using empirical evidence from the KB and local reasoning over the current interaction trace.
AI.result: On nine complex KBQA benchmark settings spanning Wikidata and Freebase, SAGA attains the best F1 scores on all nine and the best exact-match accuracy on eight of them, compared to existing approaches. Additionally, it consistently reduces the rate of SPARQL queries that execute to empty result sets in all reported Wikidata settings, indicating improved semantic compatibility in grounding.
AI.conclusion: Schema-aware, type-constrained grounding substantially improves interactive text-to-SPARQL generation for complex KBQA. By integrating persistent type state, early pruning of type-incompatible properties, and robust handling of incomplete schemas in a training-free manner, SAGA yields more semantically valid queries, higher accuracy, and fewer empty results across multiple benchmarks. This shows that leveraging KB schema is crucial for effective agentic semantic parsing.

title: Routing Ceilings Are Domain-Independent: Structural Prior Injection in Code Security Vulnerability Detection
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) exhibit a well-documented gap between latent capability and consistent activation: the router hypothesis posits that models possess the knowledge to solve a task but lack reliable internal routing to activate it. Prior work in formal mathematical reasoning (SAIR, Cázares 2026) reports that structural priors (cheatsheets) raise in-distribution performance dramatically, yet collapse below the zero-shot baseline out-of-distribution (OOD) -- and that iterative recalibration amplifies rather than corrects the collapse.
  We test whether this phenomenon is cross-domain by reproducing the SAIR design in source-code security vulnerability detection, evaluating three LLMs (GPT-OSS-120B, Llama-3.3-70B, Gemma-4-31B) across three vulnerability categories (CWE-798, CWE-284, and the non-CWE N+1 anti-pattern) spanning syntactic, contextual, and semantic complexity, then transferring cheatsheet-augmented prompts to real-world CVE data from VUDENC (CWE-89, CWE-22).
  Our findings replicate and extend SAIR: (F1) structural priors lift semantic-vulnerability recall from 20.0% to 100.0% across all models; (F2) zero-shot performance degrades along a semantic complexity gradient; (F3) the same cheatsheets that saturate synthetic performance amplify distribution-shift collapse on real CVE data (CWE-89: 100% synthetic F1 to 48.9% on VUDENC, -51.1pp); (F5) iterative recalibration produces a v2 cheatsheet that performs worse than v1 on real data, mirroring SAIR's AN45c-vs-AN38 finding.
  These results provide evidence that the cross-distribution trade-off surface documented in SAIR generalises to code security, and that the router hypothesis is cross-domain. We argue the structural nature of the collapse motivates distribution-aware training over prompt calibration. Code and evaluation scripts: https://github.com/bytepro-ai/bitcoder-v2-research

AI.tldr: The paper shows that structural prompt priors (“cheatsheets”) that dramatically boost LLM performance on synthetic security-vulnerability benchmarks cause severe performance collapse on real-world vulnerability data, supporting a cross-domain “router hypothesis” seen previously in math reasoning.
AI.motivation: Large language models often seem to “know” how to solve tasks but fail to consistently apply that knowledge, a gap formalised as the router hypothesis. Prior work in formal mathematics found that structural priors (cheatsheets) can massively improve performance in-distribution but fail catastrophically out-of-distribution, and that attempts to recalibrate prompts make the problem worse. It is unclear whether this is a quirk of math benchmarks or a general phenomenon affecting safety-critical domains like code security. The paper is motivated by testing whether the same in-distribution / out-of-distribution trade-off and routing failures appear when LLMs detect software vulnerabilities, where overfitting to synthetic structures could be particularly dangerous.
AI.method: The authors closely replicate the SAIR experimental design but in the domain of source-code security. They evaluate three LLMs (GPT-OSS-120B, Llama-3.3-70B, Gemma-4-31B) on synthetic vulnerability-detection tasks covering three categories (CWE-798, CWE-284, and an N+1 query anti-pattern), which vary in syntactic, contextual, and semantic complexity. They create structured “cheatsheet” prompts that encode task-specific priors and measure their impact versus zero-shot prompts. They then transfer these cheatsheet designs directly to real-world CVE examples from the VUDENC dataset (CWE-89, CWE-22) and compute performance metrics such as recall and F1. Finally, they perform iterative recalibration: using model failures to refine the cheatsheet into a v2 version and re-evaluating both on synthetic and real data.
AI.result: The experiments reproduce and extend SAIR’s findings in code security. Structural cheatsheet prompts increase recall on semantic vulnerabilities from 20% to 100% on synthetic tasks for all three models. However, zero-shot performance systematically degrades as semantic complexity increases, indicating a difficulty in routing latent knowledge for more complex vulnerabilities. When the same cheatsheets are applied to real-world CVE data, performance collapses substantially: for example, an apparent 100% F1 score on synthetic CWE-89 detection drops to 48.9% F1 on VUDENC (a 51.1 percentage-point loss). Iterative recalibration to build a v2 cheatsheet, guided by synthetic-error analysis, yields prompts that perform worse than the original v1 cheatsheet on the real CVE distribution, mirroring similar regressions reported in the math domain (AN45c vs AN38).
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the structural trade-off between in-distribution performance gains from cheatsheet-style prompts and out-of-distribution collapse is not limited to formal mathematics but generalises to code security, lending cross-domain support to the router hypothesis. Structural priors appear to overfit to synthetic distributions and can mask or exacerbate vulnerability-detection failures under real-world shift. Therefore, relying on prompt engineering and iterative cheatsheet refinement is inadequate and potentially dangerous for security-critical tasks. The authors argue that mitigating these issues requires distribution-aware training and evaluation practices rather than ever-more-elaborate prompt calibration. They release code and evaluation scripts to facilitate further study of routing failures in LLM-based security analysis.

title: Contextualized Evaluation of Vision Language Models through Dynamic, Multi-turn Interactions
summary: Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have made substantial advances on benchmarks, yet their real-world effectiveness remains uncertain. This gap stems from the fundamental misalignment between benchmarks in controlled, static settings and the dynamic, interactive, and contextualized nature of real-world applications. To bridge this gap, we propose CEDI (Contextualized Evaluations of MLLMs through Dynamic, multi-round Interactions), a framework that recasts evaluation as a three-party interaction between an evaluatee model, an automated examiner, and a grader. The examiner conducts multi-turn, semi-structured conversation guided by a graph-based representation of the task. By navigating state-space transitions, CEDI deploys diverse strategies, from clarification requests to adversarial probes, to elicit performance evidence. We apply CEDI to visual hallucinations. Empirical results across multiple models, diverse settings, datasets, and domains show that contextualized, interactive evaluations reveal not only significantly more hallucinations than conventional static evaluation but also ones that more closely resemble those arising in practical use cases. We further show that hallucinations often accumulate over long contexts, through self-reinforcing dialogue history, and models are particularly vulnerable to questions requiring premise rejection or refusal. Together, these findings highlight CEDI as a step toward realistic, systematic, and ecologically valid assessments of MLLMs' capabilities. Code is available at github.com/williamium3000/cedi.

AI.tldr: CEDI is a new interactive evaluation framework for multi-modal LLMs that uses dynamic, multi-round conversations to more realistically uncover visual hallucinations and other failures than static benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for multi-modal LLMs are mostly static, single-turn, and decontextualized, whereas real-world use involves dynamic, multi-round, contextual interactions. This mismatch makes it unclear how well current models truly perform in practice, especially regarding safety-critical issues like hallucinations. The authors want an evaluation approach that better approximates real deployment conditions and exposes nuanced failure modes that static tests miss.
AI.method: The authors design CEDI, a three-party evaluation framework involving (1) an evaluatee MLLM, (2) an automated examiner model, and (3) a grader. Tasks are represented as graphs defining possible states and transitions. The examiner engages the evaluatee in multi-turn, semi-structured dialogue, following the task graph to explore different strategies like clarification, follow-up questions, and adversarial probes. This interaction is used to elicit evidence of model behavior (e.g., hallucinations), which is then judged by the grader. They instantiate CEDI specifically for assessing visual hallucinations and apply it across multiple models, datasets, domains, and interaction settings.
AI.result: CEDI uncovers substantially more hallucinations than traditional static, single-shot evaluations and surfaces errors that qualitatively resemble those observed in real user interactions. The analysis shows that hallucinations tend to accumulate over long conversations, often being reinforced by the model’s own prior incorrect statements. Models show particular weakness on prompts that require rejecting incorrect premises or refusing to comply with problematic requests. These phenomena are consistently observed across different models and evaluation setups.
AI.conclusion: Contextualized, dynamic, and interactive evaluation with CEDI provides a more realistic and systematic way to assess MLLMs, especially for hallucination-related risks. The framework reveals failure patterns that static benchmarks miss, such as error accumulation over dialogue history and vulnerability to premise-rejection scenarios. The authors position CEDI as a step toward ecologically valid benchmarks for MLLMs and release code to support future research and adoption.

title: D-cut: Adaptive Verification Depth Pruning for Batched Speculative Decoding
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates large language model (LLM) inference without compromising output quality. Recent parallel drafting methods further improve single-request performance by decoupling draft length from drafting latency, enabling longer drafts and higher mean accepted tokens (MAT). However, under high request concurrency, long drafts waste substantial computation on rejected tokens, increasing verification cost and potentially making speculative decoding slower than autoregressive decoding. We present D-Cut, an adaptive pruning method that selects draft tokens jointly across the batch and concentrates the verification budget on tokens most likely to be accepted. D-Cut is motivated by two observations. First, acceptance lengths vary considerably across concurrent requests; D-Cut therefore performs cross-request pruning, allocating the verification budget adaptively according to draft confidence. Second, verification cost depends strongly on the deployment environment, including GPU architecture and parallelism strategy; D-Cut incorporates a runtime cost model to adapt its pruning depth to the target environment. Experiments on dense and mixture-of-experts (MoE) models show that, under high concurrency, D-Cut improves the average speedup from \(1.26\times\) to \(1.65\times\), restores acceleration in dense-model configurations where long-draft baselines are slower than autoregressive decoding, and achieves up to \(3.0\times\) speedup over autoregressive decoding on MoE models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes D-Cut, an adaptive pruning method for speculative decoding that keeps its speed advantages under high request concurrency by focusing verification on the most promising draft tokens.
AI.motivation: Speculative decoding speeds up LLM inference, and newer parallel drafting schemes increase single-request performance by allowing long drafts. However, in realistic high-concurrency settings, long drafts waste computation on tokens that later get rejected, inflating verification cost and sometimes making speculative decoding slower than standard autoregressive decoding. There is a need for a method that preserves the benefits of long drafts while controlling verification cost across many concurrent requests and different hardware environments.
AI.method: The authors introduce D-Cut, an adaptive pruning algorithm that selects which draft tokens to verify across a batch of concurrent requests. It performs cross-request pruning by examining confidence or acceptance likelihoods and prioritizing tokens with higher chances of being accepted, effectively reallocating the verification budget from low-confidence to high-confidence positions. Additionally, D-Cut uses a runtime cost model that accounts for the specific deployment environment (GPU architecture, parallelism strategy, etc.) to determine how aggressively to prune, so that the depth of verification is tuned to maximize speed on the target system.
AI.result: Across experiments on both dense and mixture-of-experts (MoE) LLMs and under high concurrency, D-Cut improves average speculative-decoding speedup from 1.26× to 1.65×, recovers speedups in dense-model settings where long-draft speculative baselines had become slower than plain autoregressive decoding, and achieves up to 3.0× speedup over autoregressive decoding for MoE models.
AI.conclusion: D-Cut makes speculative decoding robust and more effective in high-concurrency, real-world deployment scenarios by adaptively pruning draft tokens across requests and tailoring its behavior to hardware cost characteristics. This allows practitioners to retain or enhance the speed advantages of speculative decoding, particularly for MoE models, without sacrificing output quality or suffering from excessive verification overhead.

title: VLT: A Vision-Language-Time Series Multimodal Foundation Model for Industrial Intelligence
comment: 18 pages, 13 figures, and 13 tables, including supplementary material. Haiteng Wang and Jingheng Yan contributed equally to this work
summary: Industrial time series serve as the foundation for Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) to ensure the reliability and safety of industrial equipment such as aero-engines. However, existing approaches are typically limited to single-modality modeling, which restricts their generalization in complex scenarios. Although recent advances in large language models (LLMs) provide new opportunities for multimodal learning, bridging continuous time-series signals and discrete textual semantics remains an open challenge. To this end, we propose VLT, a multimodal foundation model that jointly models time-series, frequency-spectrum visual representations, and textual knowledge. A key insight is to utilize the frequency spectrum as a visual bridge to connect continuous temporal signals with discrete semantics. Specifically, a Time-aware Mixture-of-Experts (Time-MoE) is designed to capture heterogeneous temporal dynamics, while a Frequency-Text Augmented Learner enables joint modeling of spectral and semantic features within a shared representation space. Furthermore, a time-centric gradient alignment mechanism is introduced to mitigate cross-modal optimization conflicts via gradient normalization and reliability-aware dynamic reweighting. Extensive experiments on multiple industrial datasets demonstrate that VLT outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior robustness and generalization under few-shot, noisy, and incomplete-modality settings.

AI.tldr: They introduce VLT, a multimodal foundation model for industrial PHM that connects time-series signals, frequency-spectrum images, and text to improve robustness and generalization.
AI.motivation: Industrial PHM relies on time series from equipment, but existing models usually handle only a single modality, limiting performance in complex real-world conditions. LLM-based multimodal learning is promising, yet it is hard to align continuous time-series data with discrete textual descriptions. The paper aims to overcome this modality gap and build a more general, robust PHM model.
AI.method: They propose VLT, which jointly models three modalities: raw time series, frequency-spectrum visual representations, and textual knowledge. They convert time series to frequency spectra as images that act as a visual bridge between temporal signals and text. A Time-aware Mixture-of-Experts (Time-MoE) module is designed to capture heterogeneous temporal dynamics. A Frequency-Text Augmented Learner enables joint modeling of spectral and textual features in a shared representation space. Additionally, they design a time-centric gradient alignment mechanism that reduces cross-modal optimization conflicts using gradient normalization and reliability-aware dynamic reweighting during training.
AI.result: On multiple industrial datasets, VLT surpasses state-of-the-art baselines. It shows better robustness and generalization, particularly under challenging conditions such as few-shot learning, noisy data, and missing modalities.
AI.conclusion: Using frequency-spectrum representations as a visual bridge enables effective joint modeling of time series and text within a unified multimodal foundation model for PHM. The proposed VLT architecture—with Time-MoE, Frequency-Text Augmented Learner, and gradient alignment—improves PHM performance, robustness, and generalization across diverse and adverse industrial scenarios.

title: Harnessing LLMs for Reliable Academic Supervision: A Comparative Study
comment: 15 pages, 4 tables, 1 figure. Code and data available at https://github.com/AkashRajSingh/Harnessing-LLMs-for-Reliable-Academic-Supervision
summary:
Large language models routinely produce fluent answers to single-shot prompts, yet deploying them as reliable components of a domain decision system is substantially harder. Closing this gap is the work of harness engineering: the deliberate composition of deterministic scaffolding (symbolic filters, retrieval, schema-typed I/O, LLM-as-judge loops, HITL gates, persistent state, audit trails) around an LLM core. We present a case study in academic supervision, a domain combining high-stakes recommendation, longitudinal accountability, and structured operational workflows.
  We compare a baseline (ASA), a GPT-5 chatbot with no scaffolding, against a multi-module system (ASuS) that wraps the much smaller GPT-4o-mini in a LangGraph harness with symbolic-semantic retrieval, schema-validated outputs, LLM-as-judge with bounded retry, HITL gates, deterministic weighted risk scoring with LLM narration, and a per-node SQLite audit trail. The evaluation rubric is retargeted at six harness-mechanism dimensions (grounding, explainability, consistency, process integrity, cognitive load, constraint adherence). A blind ten-rater hybrid evaluation, supplemented by a 2 x 2 model-harness ablation, finds that ASuS, despite using a much smaller base model, outscores ASA on every dimension. Across ten raters the pooled mean for ASuS is 4.08 versus 1.23 for ASA, and 8 of 10 raters reject the null at alpha = 0.05 on a paired Wilcoxon test; full numbers are in Sections 6.4 and 6.7. The ablation confirms that the structural contributions of the harness are largely model-invariant. We extract seven recurring harness-engineering patterns and argue that where reliability, traceability, and institutional consistency matter more than open-ended fluency, harness engineering challenges the prevailing 'bigger model is better' intuition.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that a carefully engineered scaffolding (harness) around a smaller LLM can outperform a larger raw chatbot for academic supervision tasks, emphasizing reliability, traceability, and process control over raw fluency.
AI.motivation: Although large language models can produce fluent single-shot responses, integrating them as dependable components in real decision-making systems is difficult. Domains like academic supervision require high-stakes recommendations, long-term accountability, structured workflows, and auditability. The authors want to understand how to systematically design and evaluate a surrounding infrastructure (harness) that makes LLM-based systems reliable, explainable, and consistent, and to test whether such harnesses can let smaller models compete with or beat larger models in these settings.
AI.method: They design two systems for academic supervision. ASA is a baseline that uses a powerful GPT-5 chatbot with almost no external scaffolding. ASuS is a more complex system that wraps a smaller GPT-4o-mini model in a LangGraph-based harness. This harness includes symbolic and semantic retrieval, schema-validated inputs and outputs, an LLM-as-judge component with bounded retries, human-in-the-loop gates, deterministic weighted risk scoring explained by the model, and per-node SQLite audit logging. They construct an evaluation rubric that focuses on six dimensions directly connected to harness mechanisms: grounding, explainability, consistency, process integrity, cognitive load, and constraint adherence. Using a blind ten-rater hybrid evaluation and a 2x2 ablation over model size vs harness structure, they compare ASA and ASuS and assess harness effects independent of model choice.
AI.result: The ASuS system, despite using the smaller GPT-4o-mini model, outperforms the larger, minimally scaffolded ASA system (GPT-5) on all six evaluation dimensions. Across ten human raters, ASuS achieves a pooled mean score of 4.08 versus 1.23 for ASA. In paired Wilcoxon tests, 8 out of 10 raters individually reject the null hypothesis at alpha = 0.05, supporting the statistical significance of the improvement. The 2x2 ablation indicates that the benefit primarily comes from the harness structure rather than the underlying model size or identity, showing the model-agnostic value of the harness mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: Careful harness engineering—adding deterministic scaffolding such as retrieval, schema enforcement, judge loops, human gates, risk scoring, and audit trails—can make smaller LLMs outperform larger unstructured chatbots in domains demanding reliability, traceability, and procedural consistency. The authors distill seven recurring harness patterns and argue that, in such settings, the conventional belief that “bigger models are always better” is challenged; system design around the model can be more important than marginal gains in base model capability.

title: RetroAgent: Harnessing LLMs to Search Over Structured Memory for Agentic Retrosynthesis Planning
summary: Multi-step retrosynthesis planning seeks to decompose a target molecule into commercially available building blocks through a sequence of feasible reactions. The vast combinatorial search space makes this task challenging even for expert chemists. Traditional methods combine tree search with offline-trained value networks that score candidates in isolation, without reasoning about complete multi-step routes. Recent work leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) for this task, but relies on simple interfaces that limit exploration of the full search space. We introduce RetroAgent, an LLM agent that bridges symbolic search and neural reasoning through a harness with structured memory. Through memory and chemistry tools, the agent observes the full search state, including explored routes, available alternatives, and properties of intermediates, enabling informed decisions grounded in both global progress and domain knowledge. Experiments on in-distribution and out-of-distribution benchmarks demonstrate that RetroAgent delivers strong performance and generalization.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RetroAgent, an LLM-based retrosynthesis planning agent that couples symbolic search with structured memory and chemistry tools to reason over full multi-step synthetic routes, improving performance and generalization on benchmark tasks.
AI.motivation: Multi-step retrosynthesis involves decomposing a target molecule into sellable building blocks via a sequence of reactions, but the combinatorial explosion of possible routes makes search very difficult. Existing methods typically use tree search plus value networks trained offline that evaluate states or actions in isolation, without holistic reasoning over complete routes. Early LLM-based approaches also interact with the search only through simple prompts, limiting the ability to explore and exploit the full search space and accumulated partial plans. There is a need for a method that can use LLMs to reason jointly about global search progress, alternative branches, and chemical constraints while remaining scalable for complex molecules.
AI.method: The authors propose RetroAgent, an LLM agent wrapped in a structured search harness. The agent is equipped with structured memory that stores the full search state: explored synthetic routes, candidate branches, and properties of intermediate molecules. Through this memory and specialized chemistry tools, the LLM can query and update global information, inspect alternatives, and use domain knowledge when choosing expansions or backtracking in the retrosynthesis tree. The system thus tightly couples symbolic search procedures with neural reasoning, allowing the LLM to make locally informed choices based on global route information rather than scoring states independently.
AI.result: On both in-distribution and out-of-distribution retrosynthesis benchmarks, RetroAgent achieves strong performance, indicating it can efficiently find viable multi-step routes and generalize beyond training distributions. Although specific metrics are not given in the abstract, the results show that the integration of structured memory and tools with LLM-driven reasoning outperforms or is competitive with prior value-network-based and prompt-based LLM approaches.
AI.conclusion: RetroAgent demonstrates that an LLM agent augmented with structured memory and chemistry tools can effectively bridge symbolic search and neural reasoning for multi-step retrosynthesis. By allowing the model to observe and manipulate the complete search state, the approach supports more informed, globally coherent planning decisions and yields better performance and generalization than methods that evaluate candidates in isolation or use limited LLM interfaces. This suggests a promising paradigm for complex scientific planning tasks that require both combinatorial search and domain-aware reasoning.

title: Gold-Guided Programmatic Distillation for Financial Reasoning over Hybrid Tables and Text
summary: Financial question answering over hybrid tabular and textual data may require multi-source reasoning and precise numerical computation. While large language models (LLMs) can generate intermediate reasoning steps, natural-language rationales remain prone to arithmetic errors, making them an unreliable supervision source for distillation. Building on programmatic distillation, we develop an approach that transfers reliable numerical reasoning from a large teacher model to a compact student using execution-verified Python programs instead of free-form textual rationales. It leverages gold derivations to guide teacher-side program synthesis and retains only programs that execute correctly and produce the gold answer, ensuring high-quality supervision. We further introduce an iterative recovery stage that revisits teacher-failed examples, enabling the student to recover and incorporate newly verified programs into training. Experiments on TAT-QA show that our framework is highly effective for hybrid financial reasoning. Our best 7B student achieves 87.00 EM / 87.18 F1 on the test set, substantially outperforming the 72B teacher (78.46 EM) as well as traditional and strong LLM-based baselines, including TAGOP and TAT-LLM. These results demonstrate that execution-verified programmatic distillation provides an effective and extensible framework for training smaller models to perform reliable numerical reasoning.

AI.tldr: They propose an execution-verified programmatic distillation framework where a large LLM teacher generates Python programs for financial QA; only programs that execute correctly to the gold answer supervise a smaller student model, yielding a 7B model that surpasses its 72B teacher on TAT-QA.
AI.motivation: Financial QA over tables+text demands multi-source reasoning and precise numerical computation; LLM textual rationales are error-prone in arithmetic, so they are unreliable for distillation. The authors want a way to transfer robust numerical reasoning from a large teacher to a smaller student without inheriting the teacher's arithmetic mistakes.
AI.method: They extend programmatic distillation by having a large LLM teacher synthesize Python programs (rather than natural-language chains-of-thought) for each QA example, guided by gold derivations. These programs are executed, and only those that both run successfully and produce the gold answer are kept as verified supervision signals. Training data thus pairs questions with verified programs and answers. They also add an iterative recovery stage: for examples where the teacher initially fails to generate a correct program, they revisit them later (after some student training or refinements) to obtain additional verified programs and expand the training set.
AI.result: On the hybrid financial QA benchmark TAT-QA, their best 7B-parameter student model obtains 87.00 exact match (EM) and 87.18 F1 on the test set. This substantially surpasses the 72B teacher model's 78.46 EM and outperforms both traditional methods and strong LLM-based baselines, including TAGOP and TAT-LLM.
AI.conclusion: Execution-verified programmatic distillation can effectively transfer reliable numerical and multi-source reasoning from large teachers to smaller students. By supervising on executable Python programs that are guaranteed to yield the gold answers, and using an iterative recovery strategy, they can train compact models that not only match but exceed larger teachers on financial QA with hybrid tabular-textual inputs. The framework is extensible and suggests a promising direction for building smaller yet trustworthy numerical reasoners.

title: WrAFT: a Modularized Automated Writing Evaluation System for Argumentative Essays
summary: This study presents WrAFT, a Writing Assessment and Feedback Tool, that delivers both accurate and reliable scores and effective comprehensive feedback to argumentative essays. WrAFT adopts a modular design by dividing automated writing evaluation (AWE) tasks into scoring, surface-level feedback, and deep-level feedback. In building the system, various Large Language Models (LLMs) have been evaluated, including LLaMA-3.3-70B-Instruct, GPT-4o, and Claude 3.7, through both direct prompting and supervised fine-tuning approaches. A proprietary dataset of 480 TOEFL Independent Writing essays with official benchmark scores was utilized. Benchmark-based evaluation shows that WrAFT achieves state-of-the-art performance in scoring, with a quadratic weighted kappa (QWK) of 0.84 and a root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.44 against official scores on a scale of 0-5. Human evaluation of system-generated feedback also reveals high approval ratings: 96.14 percent for surface-level feedback, 93.03 percent for deep-level macro feedback, and 94.69 percent for deep-level micro feedback. An interactive user interface has been developed for the system and is publicly available and free to use.

AI.tldr: WrAFT is a modular automated writing evaluation system that uses large language models to score TOEFL essays and provide multi-level feedback, achieving state-of-the-art scoring accuracy and highly rated feedback quality via a public user interface.
AI.motivation: To build an automated writing evaluation tool that not only scores essays accurately and reliably but also provides comprehensive, effective feedback at multiple levels, addressing limitations of existing systems that often focus on either scoring or shallow feedback.
AI.method: The authors design WrAFT with three modules: scoring, surface-level feedback, and deep-level feedback. They experiment with several LLMs (LLaMA-3.3-70B-Instruct, GPT-4o, Claude 3.7) using direct prompting and supervised fine-tuning. They train and evaluate the system on a proprietary set of 480 TOEFL Independent Writing essays with official scores, then benchmark scoring performance using QWK and RMSE and assess feedback quality with human evaluations across surface and deep feedback types.
AI.result: WrAFT achieves a quadratic weighted kappa of 0.84 and RMSE of 0.44 against official scores (0–5 scale), indicating state-of-the-art scoring accuracy. Human raters give high approval to system feedback: 96.14% for surface-level, 93.03% for deep-level macro, and 94.69% for deep-level micro feedback. The authors also implement and release a public, free-to-use interactive interface for WrAFT.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that a modular LLM-based AWE system can simultaneously deliver state-of-the-art scoring and highly satisfactory multi-level feedback on argumentative essays. WrAFT’s design and performance suggest a practical path for combining robust automated scoring with rich formative feedback, and the publicly accessible interface supports real-world adoption and further research.

title: The Misclassification of Autistic Writing as AI-Generated
comment: Author Accepted Manuscript, Artificial Intelligence in Education 2025
summary: Recent findings suggest that detection models for artificial intelligence (AI) cannot accurately identify AI-generated text and may exhibit bias against certain minority groups. In the present study, anecdotal claims that autistic writers more often have their work flagged as AI-generated are examined empirically.
