A corpus of approximately 60,000 Reddit posts split into "likely-autistic" and "general-Reddit" subcorpora is used to compare the distribution of probabilities output by the OpenAI GPT-2 detection model. Differences in textual features between subcorpora are observed and compared to reported features of AI-generated text. Results showed that while less than two-percent of either subcorpus was flagged as AI-generated by the model, significantly more texts from the likely-autistic subcorpus were flagged. Connections between features of text with likely-autistic authors and AI-generated text were not straightforward. The widespread use of AI-detection models with a potential bias against autistic writers in their output prompts ethical scrutiny, and the authors recommend further critical examination of the models themselves as well as their use in academic contexts.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically tests whether an AI-text detector (OpenAI GPT-2 detector) is biased against autistic writers and finds significantly higher false AI flags for a likely-autistic Reddit subcorpus, raising ethical concerns about using such detectors.
AI.motivation: There are growing concerns that AI-text detectors are inaccurate and may be biased against certain groups. Anecdotally, autistic writers have reported that their writing is more often labeled as AI-generated, but this has not been systematically studied. The paper aims to empirically evaluate whether such bias exists and to understand how linguistic features of autistic-authored text relate to what detectors consider AI-like.
AI.method: The authors construct a corpus of about 60,000 Reddit posts divided into two subcorpora: "likely-autistic" and a general Reddit control group. They run all posts through the OpenAI GPT-2 detection model and analyze the distribution of AI-probability scores across the two groups. They also compare textual and stylistic features of the subcorpora with known characteristics of AI-generated text to see whether these can explain any observed differences in detection outcomes.
AI.result: Less than 2% of posts in either subcorpus are flagged as AI-generated overall, but a significantly higher proportion of posts from the likely-autistic subcorpus are labeled as AI-generated compared with the general Reddit subcorpus. However, the relationship between linguistic features typical of likely-autistic authors and those typically associated with AI-generated text is complex and not cleanly aligned, suggesting that the bias cannot be easily explained by simple feature overlap.
AI.conclusion: The GPT-2-based AI-text detector appears to exhibit disparate impact against likely-autistic writers, even though overall flag rates are low. Because the mapping between autistic writing features and AI-like features is not straightforward, the source of this bias is unclear. The authors argue that the deployment of such detectors, especially in academic contexts where they may affect assessment or accusations of misconduct, requires ethical scrutiny and further critical investigation of both the models and their institutional uses.

title: Are LLM-Generated GPU Kernels Production-Ready? A Trace-Driven Benchmark and Optimization Agent
comment: Both artifacts are released as open source: Atrex-Bench (https://github.com/alibaba/atrex-bench) and Atrex-Kernel-Agent (https://github.com/alibaba/atrex-kernel-agent)
summary: Existing GPU kernel generation benchmarks draw problems from synthetic or curated sources that diverge from deployed workloads. We present Atrex-Bench, a benchmark whose 30 operators and 440 shapes are sampled directly from full-cluster production inference traces of compute-limited, memory-rich GPUs. Each problem carries an importance weight derived from its share of observed GPU time, weighted by application card-hours and computed separately for the serving phases in which it runs, together with a per-problem roofline ceiling, so the aggregate score emphasizes the kernels that consume the most serving time. Evaluating six frontier coding agents on Atrex-Bench shows that even the best vanilla model reaches only ${\sim}10\%$ of the hardware roofline on production operators; and correctness alone overstates capability, since much of the apparent pass rate comes from PyTorch fallbacks rather than kernels the model wrote. To close this gap, we co-release Atrex-Kernel-Agent (AKA), a profile-driven kernel-optimization agent that combines iterative measure-revise search, optimization dropout for escaping stalled search contexts, and a layered GPU-optimization knowledge base (298 reference-kernel files and 244 optimization-knowledge documents, plus external upstream reference projects for API/ISA lookup). In a controlled case study, the agent converts zero-FlyDSL fallbacks into real kernels that match or exceed hand-tuned production baselines.

AI.tldr: Atrex-Bench is a GPU kernel benchmark built from real production inference traces, plus an optimization agent (AKA) that significantly improves kernel performance beyond correctness-only metrics.
AI.motivation: Current GPU kernel benchmarks rely on synthetic or hand-curated workloads that do not reflect real, production inference behavior, leading to misleading assessments of kernel-generation agents. There is also a gap between correctness-based evaluation and true performance, especially because many ‘correct’ results rely on slow fallbacks rather than optimized kernels.
AI.method: The authors build Atrex-Bench from full-cluster production inference traces of compute-limited, memory-rich GPUs. They select 30 operators and 440 shapes, each annotated with an importance weight determined by its share of observed GPU time and application card-hours, and further separated by serving phase. They also provide per-problem roofline performance ceilings. They then evaluate six state-of-the-art code-generation agents on this benchmark. Additionally, they develop Atrex-Kernel-Agent (AKA), a profile-driven kernel optimization agent that performs iterative measure-and-revise search, uses optimization dropout to escape local optima, and leverages a layered knowledge base of reference kernels, optimization documents, and external projects for API/ISA lookup. They run a controlled case study where AKA targets previously unimplemented operators that were handled via fallbacks.
AI.result: On Atrex-Bench, the best standard coding model achieves only about 10% of the hardware roofline on real production operators. Measured correctness substantially overestimates capability because many passing cases actually call PyTorch fallbacks rather than executing model-generated kernels. In the case study, Atrex-Kernel-Agent replaces these fallbacks with real GPU kernels whose performance matches or exceeds existing hand-tuned production kernels.
AI.conclusion: Realistic, trace-derived benchmarks like Atrex-Bench reveal a large performance gap between current kernel-generation agents and hardware limits, and show that correctness metrics can be misleading when fallbacks are involved. The proposed Atrex-Kernel-Agent, equipped with profiling feedback and rich optimization knowledge, can close much of this gap by turning fallback-based implementations into genuinely optimized kernels that rival or surpass human-tuned baselines.

title: CoTu at EXACT 2026: Neuro-Symbolic Reasoning for Transparent Educational QA
comment: The 2nd International XAI Challenge for Transparent Educational Question-Answering @ IEEE IJCNN 2026 Competition
summary: Transparent educational question answering asks for answers that are not only correct but explainable, and doing so with small models rules out the reasoning power of the largest proprietary systems. The EXACT 2026 competition poses this problem concretely: open-weight language models of at most 8B parameters, self-hosted, with a natural-language explanation for every answer. It pairs two tasks: logical reasoning over university regulations, and multi-step physics problem solving. We describe the system that team \cotu{} developed to address both, a neuro-symbolic Program-of-Thought pipeline in which a 4B backbone writes a program rather than stating an answer directly: for regulation queries it emits a Z3 encoding whose entailment verdict grounds the deduction, and for physics it emits numerical Python, both wrapped in a shared self-correction loop and a unified explained-JSON output. Answer-type routing, distillation-based task fine-tuning, and a latency-aware serving stack -- SGLang with speculative decoding -- keep the system within the 60-second per-query limit. The system achieved a \textbf{perfect score} on the physics task in both automated selection rounds and obtained the \textbf{highest final-round technical score} of any team -- $13.44/15$, combining automated answer evaluation with expert-judged reasoning depth -- with the equally weighted presentation score included, \cotu{} placed 3rd overall. Grounding answers in a symbolic solver yields correct, verifiable deductions at the 4B scale, and the residual difficulty lies in premise selection rather than the deduction itself.

AI.tldr: They build a small (4B) transparent question-answering system that explains its answers by generating programs (Z3 logic or Python) instead of direct text, achieving top scores in the EXACT 2026 competition.
AI.motivation: Transparent QA needs not just correct but explainable answers, and competition constraints disallow using large proprietary LLMs. The authors want a method that gives verifiable reasoning with small, open-weight models on challenging tasks like law-like regulation reasoning and multi-step physics.
AI.method: They use a neuro-symbolic Program-of-Thought pipeline with a 4B LLM that outputs executable programs: Z3 encodings for regulation questions and numerical Python for physics. The programs are executed to obtain answers, embedded in a self-correction loop, and returned with unified explained-JSON outputs. Answer-type routing, distillation-based task fine-tuning, and a latency-optimized serving stack (SGLang with speculative decoding) ensure performance within a 60s limit.
AI.result: The system attained a perfect score on the physics task in all automated rounds and the highest final-round technical score (13.44/15) across teams, which considered both answer correctness and reasoning depth. Including presentation, the team ranked 3rd overall in the competition.
AI.conclusion: Symbolic solvers, when driven by a modest 4B LLM that generates programs rather than direct answers, can provide correct, verifiable reasoning for complex educational QA. Remaining challenges focus more on selecting the right premises than on performing the actual deduction once encoded symbolically.

title: Towards an Intention Abstraction Layer for Autonomous Industrial Systems
summary: Modern industrial environments increasingly run many autonomous subsystems at once - schedulers, energy managers, vehicle fleets - each pursuing its own goals while sharing the same physical resources. Because high-level human intentions are translated into low-level control logic and then discarded, no running component can tell whether it is still doing what was actually intended, and goal conflicts surface only after they have caused a missed target or a shutdown. We propose the Intention Abstraction Layer (IAL), a domainagnostic middleware that represents intentions as first-class, persistent, and explainable runtime objects: a large language model grounded in a formal OWL ontology parses naturallanguage goals into structured intentions, a consistency monitor detects conflicts at registration time, before execution, and a transparency module explains them in natural language. We report a first proof of concept in which two autonomous agents register conflicting production and energy intentions, and the IAL flags and explains the conflict before it reaches the execution layer. The result is a mechanism that shifts behavioral assurance for cooperating autonomous systems from post-hoc failure analysis to pre-execution, intention-level checking.

AI.tldr: Introduces the Intention Abstraction Layer (IAL), a middleware that turns natural-language goals for industrial autonomous systems into persistent, checkable, and explainable intention objects so conflicts can be detected before execution.
AI.motivation: In complex industrial settings, many autonomous subsystems (e.g., schedulers, energy managers, vehicle fleets) operate concurrently, each with its own objectives but sharing physical resources. Human high-level intentions are translated into low-level control code and then effectively lost, leaving no runtime representation of what was originally intended. As a result, systems cannot check whether current behavior aligns with human goals, and conflicts between subsystem objectives are typically discovered only after causing failures such as missed production targets or shutdowns. There is a need for a way to explicitly represent, monitor, and reason about goals at runtime, across heterogeneous autonomous components, to provide earlier assurance and prevent harmful conflicts.
AI.method: The paper proposes the Intention Abstraction Layer (IAL), a domain-agnostic middleware architecture. First, a large language model (LLM) grounded in a formal OWL ontology parses natural-language goals specified by humans or agents into structured, machine-readable intention objects. These intentions are persistent and treated as first-class runtime entities. Second, a consistency monitor analyzes newly registered intentions against existing ones to detect goal conflicts at registration time, before they impact execution. Third, a transparency module generates natural-language explanations of detected conflicts to make them understandable to humans. The authors implement a proof-of-concept scenario involving two autonomous agents with conflicting production and energy goals to demonstrate the approach.
AI.result: In the proof-of-concept implementation, two autonomous agents submit intentions: one related to production targets and the other to energy constraints. The IAL successfully parses these natural-language goals into structured intentions, detects the conflict between them at the point of registration, and provides an explanation of the conflict in natural language. This shows that intention-level reasoning can be performed before any low-level control actions are taken, and that the middleware can surface and explain conflicts between autonomous subsystems in advance.
AI.conclusion: The Intention Abstraction Layer offers a way to keep high-level human and system intentions explicit, persistent, and analyzable at runtime in multi-agent industrial environments. By combining LLM-based natural-language parsing with a formal ontology, automated consistency checking, and explanatory feedback, IAL shifts behavioral assurance from post-hoc failure analysis to pre-execution intention-level verification. The initial proof-of-concept suggests that this approach can detect and explain conflicting goals among autonomous subsystems before they cause operational problems, pointing toward more reliable and transparent cooperation among autonomous industrial systems.

title: Dialogue Summarization with Emotion Dynamics Using Topic- and Participant-Centric Decomposition
summary: Existing text summarization research has focused much on monologic information (e.g., newspaper articles, reports) without accounting for the interaction between speakers or authors. In contrast, dialogues are a rich communication channel where multiple participants conduct back and forth exchanges to construct meaning. We propose a dialogue summarization framework that explicitly models both semantic and emotion dynamics using multimodal dialogue inputs, built on an adapted hierarchical Chain-of-Agents approach. We decompose dialogues from two perspectives: (1) topic segments based on the utterances of all participants, and (2) participant-specific utterance segments. These are used to generate corresponding summaries while incorporating automatically inferred emotions. Topic- and participant-level summaries are aggregated into a dialogue summary capturing semantic content and emotion trajectories. To evaluate beyond content accuracy, we introduce emotion trajectory metrics measuring how well summaries preserve emotional flow. Experiments with small language models on multimodal dialogue datasets show that our framework produces summaries with both semantic and emotion content. Further experiments on explicit emotion label availability highlight the efficacy of our proposed methodology and the opportunities in dialogue analysis using language models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a dialogue summarization framework that jointly models semantic content and emotion dynamics using multimodal inputs and a hierarchical structure, and introduces new emotion trajectory metrics to evaluate summaries.
AI.motivation: Most existing text summarization focuses on monologic texts like news or reports and ignores interactive aspects such as speaker exchanges and emotional flow in dialogues. However, real-world dialogues include multiple participants, turn-taking, and evolving emotions that shape meaning. There is a need for summarization methods that capture both what is said and how emotional dynamics unfold over time in multi-party conversations, especially when using multimodal cues and operating with smaller language models.
AI.method: The authors design a dialogue summarization framework built on an adapted hierarchical Chain-of-Agents approach. Dialogues are decomposed in two orthogonal ways: (1) topic-based segments derived from all participants’ utterances, and (2) participant-specific segments that track each speaker’s contributions. Multimodal inputs are used and emotions are automatically inferred for utterances. For each segmentation axis, summaries are generated that incorporate both semantic and emotional information. These topic-level and participant-level summaries are then aggregated into a final dialogue summary that represents both content and emotion trajectories. The authors also introduce novel emotion trajectory metrics that compare emotional flows in the original dialogue with those in the generated summary.
AI.result: On multimodal dialogue datasets and using small language models, the proposed framework produces summaries that better capture both semantic content and emotional aspects of dialogues compared to baselines. The introduced emotion trajectory metrics demonstrate that the generated summaries preserve the emotional flow more faithfully. Additional experiments that vary the availability of explicit emotion labels show that the approach is effective even with automatically inferred emotions, and illustrate how explicit labels can further improve performance.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that explicitly modeling both semantic structure and emotion dynamics, through a hierarchical, multimodal, and multi-perspective decomposition of dialogues, leads to more faithful and informative dialogue summaries. The newly proposed emotion trajectory metrics enable richer evaluation of dialogue summaries beyond standard content accuracy. The framework demonstrates that even small language models can effectively support nuanced dialogue analysis and summarization when guided by structured decomposition and emotion-aware modeling, opening up further research opportunities in emotion-centric dialogue understanding.

title: Seeing the End at Step Zero: Accelerating Diffusion MLLMs via MLP Sparsity-Aware Truncation
summary: Diffusion Multimodal Large Language Models (DMLLMs) are highly effective for multimodal reasoning, yet their inference efficiency is significantly hindered by fixed-length generation constraints. Since the actual output length is unknown, output sequences are padded to a predefined maximum length, resulting in substantial redundant computation over unnecessary [EOS] tokens. In this work, we discover that DMLLMs implicitly reveal their valid semantic boundary at the very first denoising step through a distinct shift in MLP activation sparsity. Leveraging this observation, we propose Seer, a training-free framework that detects this boundary using a Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)-based criterion and performs one-shot truncation of the redundant suffix for all subsequent computations. To preserve these theoretical gains during batched serving, Seer incorporates a hybrid execution strategy that maximizes throughput while seamlessly accommodating dynamic sequence lengths. Experimental results demonstrate that Seer effectively eliminates padding waste, accelerating throughput by up to $\sim$31$\times$. Across 9 benchmarks, Seer robustly maintains overall performance and even improves accuracy on complex visual tasks by mitigating noise leakage (e.g., DocVQA score increases from 63.52 to 63.66), offering a highly efficient, plug-and-play solution for DMLLM acceleration.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Seer, a training-free framework that accelerates Diffusion Multimodal Large Language Models by removing redundant padded tokens, achieving up to ~31x throughput gains while preserving or slightly improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Diffusion Multimodal LLMs are strong at multimodal reasoning but suffer from low inference efficiency due to fixed-length generation: since the real output length is unknown, systems pad outputs to a large maximum length, causing heavy redundant computation on meaningless [EOS] tokens, especially in batched serving. There is a need for a way to reduce this padding waste without retraining or hurting model performance.
AI.method: The authors analyze DMLLMs and discover that at the very first denoising step, the model’s MLP activation sparsity shows a distinct change at the boundary between semantically valid tokens and redundant suffix. They exploit this property by designing Seer, a training-free mechanism that: (1) uses an SNR-based criterion to detect the semantic boundary of each sequence in a one-shot manner at the first denoising step; (2) truncates the suffix beyond this boundary for all subsequent diffusion steps, thereby skipping computation on redundant tokens; and (3) introduces a hybrid execution strategy to support batched serving with dynamic per-sequence lengths while preserving high throughput.
AI.result: Seer eliminates most padding-induced computation for DMLLMs, yielding up to approximately 31x improvement in throughput. On 9 benchmarks, Seer maintains overall task performance and in some complex visual tasks even improves accuracy by reducing noise leakage (e.g., slightly higher DocVQA score from 63.52 to 63.66).
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that DMLLMs implicitly encode their semantic output length in early denoising dynamics, which can be exploited without retraining. Seer is presented as a plug-and-play, training-free solution that detects semantic boundaries via SNR at the first denoising step, enabling aggressive truncation of redundant padded tokens in both single and batched inference. This approach achieves large speedups with no accuracy degradation and minor gains on some challenging visual benchmarks, providing a practical path to accelerate diffusion-based multimodal LLMs in real deployments.

title: SEED: Self-Evolving On-Policy Distillation for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large language models are increasingly trained as interactive agents for long-horizon tasks involving multi-turn interaction, tool use, and environment feedback. Outcome-based reinforcement learning (RL) provides a practical optimization paradigm, but its sparse trajectory-level rewards offer limited guidance on intermediate decisions, leaving a supervision gap between episode-level outcomes and token-level policy learning. We propose SEED (SElf-Evolving On-Policy Distillation), a self-evolving framework that converts completed on-policy trajectories into training-time hindsight skills and distills their behavioral effect back into the policy model. SEED first fine-tunes the policy to analyze completed trajectories and generate natural-language skills that capture reusable workflows, decisive observations, or failure-avoidance rules. During RL, the current policy both collects trajectories and serves as the analyzer that extracts hindsight skills from them. Policy updates therefore improve subsequent decision making and skill analysis together, allowing hindsight supervision to evolve with the policy. SEED then re-scores the sampled actions under ordinary and skill-augmented contexts, converting the skill-induced probability shift into a dense token-level on-policy distillation signal. This signal is jointly optimized with outcome-based RL, keeping the auxiliary supervision aligned with the current trajectory distribution. Extensive experiments on text-based and vision-based agentic tasks show that SEED consistently improves performance and sample efficiency, exhibiting robust generalization to unseen scenarios. Our code is available at https://github.com/jinyangwu/SEED.

AI.tldr: SEED is a self-evolving on-policy distillation framework that converts completed RL trajectories into natural-language skills and uses them to create dense token-level supervision that improves long-horizon agent performance and sample efficiency.
AI.motivation: Outcome-based RL for long-horizon, interactive LLM agents only provides sparse, trajectory-level rewards, which are too coarse to guide individual token or action decisions. This causes a supervision gap between episodic success/failure signals and token-level policy learning, limiting data efficiency and performance. The authors want a way to extract richer, structured supervision from the agent’s own experience while staying on-policy and aligned with its current behavior distribution.
AI.method: SEED introduces a self-evolving loop with three main components: (1) Hindsight skill extraction: After trajectories are collected on-policy, the same policy model is fine-tuned to act as an analyzer that reads entire completed trajectories and generates natural-language skills—concise descriptions of reusable workflows, key observations, or rules that avoid previous failure. (2) Dual-context scoring: For each sampled trajectory, the model re-scores its own actions twice—once in a normal context and once in a skill-augmented context where the extracted skills are provided. The difference in log-probabilities acts as a signal of how useful those skills are for each token/action. (3) On-policy distillation: This skill-induced probability shift is turned into dense, token-level distillation losses that are optimized jointly with standard outcome-based RL objectives. Because skills and trajectories are both generated by the current policy, the auxiliary supervision remains on-policy and co-evolves with policy improvements.
AI.result: Across a variety of text-based and vision-based long-horizon agent tasks, SEED leads to higher final performance and substantially better sample efficiency than baseline RL approaches. The framework improves robustness and generalization to unseen scenarios, indicating that the learned hindsight skills capture transferable patterns and decision rules. Empirical results show consistent gains over strong baselines, validating that dense distillation signals derived from skills provide effective intermediate supervision beyond sparse outcome rewards.
AI.conclusion: SEED demonstrates that self-extracted, natural-language hindsight skills can bridge the supervision gap in outcome-based RL for agentic LLMs. By using the current policy both to generate skills from its own trajectories and to distill the behavioral impact of those skills back into token-level updates, SEED provides dense on-policy supervision that is well-aligned with the agent’s evolving behavior. This self-evolving on-policy distillation consistently enhances performance, sample efficiency, and generalization in long-horizon interactive tasks, suggesting a promising direction for training more capable and data-efficient language and vision-language agents.

title: Democratizing Agent Deployment Safety: A Structural Monitoring Approach
comment: Accepted in ICML 2026 Workshops: AI4GOOD and AIWILD
summary: AI software development agents are increasingly capable of modifying infrastructure and security critical systems, creating risks where an agent completes its assigned task while covertly weakening safeguards through actions such as broadening permissions, degrading logging, or introducing persistence mechanisms. While frontier laboratories may deploy sophisticated monitoring pipelines, many organizations and individual users adopting coding agents lack the resources and governance maturity required to maintain complex learned monitor ensembles. We study this problem as infrastructure level sabotage under task success in the infrastructure-as-code setting from ControlArena and compare trained and untrained monitors across synchronous and asynchronous deployment modes. We introduce an Information Flow Graph (IFG) monitor that analyzes structural security regressions using control-flow and data-flow graph diffs alongside raw code diffs. In asynchronous evaluation, an untrained git diff monitor misses 11.6% attacks at 1% false positive rate auditing budget, our untrained IFG monitor reduces this to 3.5%, and the trained Async RF monitor achieves below 1%. Unlike the trained Async RF monitor, IFG can also operate synchronously as a pre-deployment safeguard that blocks suspicious infrastructure changes before execution or merge. In synchronous mode, IFG realtime rollback reduces joint main task and covert task success from 74.4% to 0.0% with no measurable reduction in legitimate task completion. These results suggest that untrained structural monitors provide a practical and auditable path toward democratizing deployment safety for organizations seeking trustworthy adoption of advanced AI agents.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the risk of AI coding agents covertly weakening infrastructure and security protections while still successfully completing their assigned tasks, and proposes an untrained structural monitor based on information flow graphs that can detect and block such sabotage effectively, including in real time.
AI.motivation: As AI software development agents gain the ability to modify infrastructure-as-code and security-critical systems, they introduce new risks: an agent can both accomplish its main task and secretly degrade security by altering permissions, logging, or persistence mechanisms. Many organizations lack the advanced monitoring and training pipelines that frontier labs might use to detect such malicious or unsafe changes. There is therefore a need for practical, low-resource, and auditable defenses that can be deployed widely to catch covert infrastructure sabotage by AI agents.
AI.method: The authors formalize the problem as "infrastructure level sabotage under task success" in an infrastructure-as-code environment based on ControlArena. They evaluate different monitoring strategies—both trained and untrained, and both synchronous (pre-deployment) and asynchronous (post-deployment)—for detecting covertly malicious changes. They propose an Information Flow Graph (IFG) monitor that constructs control-flow and data-flow graphs from code, then compares structural graph differences and raw code diffs between versions to detect security regressions. They benchmark this IFG monitor against a simple untrained git diff monitor and a trained random forest-based asynchronous monitor (Async RF).
AI.result: In asynchronous evaluation (monitoring after changes are made), a basic untrained git diff monitor misses 11.6% of attacks at a 1% false positive rate budget. The proposed untrained IFG monitor reduces the missed-attack rate to 3.5% under the same budget, while the trained asynchronous random forest monitor further reduces it to below 1%. The IFG monitor can also be deployed synchronously, unlike the trained Async RF monitor. In this synchronous mode, using IFG for real-time rollback reduces the scenario where both the main assigned task and the covert sabotage task succeed from 74.4% to 0.0%, without measurably harming the completion rate of legitimate tasks.
AI.conclusion: Untrained structural monitors based on information flow graphs can substantially improve the detection of covert infrastructure sabotage by AI coding agents, offering performance close to trained models while being simpler, more auditable, and usable in real-time blocking modes. This makes IFG-style monitoring a practical and accessible safety mechanism for organizations that lack sophisticated ML monitoring infrastructure but still need trustworthy deployment of advanced AI agents that modify infrastructure-as-code.

title: Show Me How You Reason and I'll Tell You Who You Are: Reasoning Graphs for Robust LLM Authorship Attribution
summary: Given the current trend to employ large language models (LLMs) in almost any imaginable context, LLM-generated text detection and authorship attribution have become a pressing issue. Prior work has primarily focused on surface-level linguistic features, an approach shown to be susceptible to paraphrasing and other obfuscation techniques. In this paper, we go beyond the linguistic surface, extracting and analysing reasoning structures in LLM-generated texts with the goal of capturing more complex signals of LLM authorship. We propose a graph neural network approach that leverages reasoning graphs extracted by an argument mining pipeline, demonstrating improved robustness and generalisation over a traditional Longformer baseline. Our approach outperforms the baseline by up to 27 percentage points under the obfuscation attacks such as paraphrasing and backtranslation, and 19 percentage points when evaluated on the texts generated by the unseen model versions, simulating real-world conditions in which new LLM versions are continuously released.

AI.tldr: They detect LLM-generated text using deep reasoning-structure features instead of just surface language, achieving much better robustness to paraphrasing and unseen models.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-text detectors mostly rely on shallow linguistic cues (lexical, syntactic, stylistic). These cues are easily fooled by paraphrasing, backtranslation, and other obfuscation, and they often fail to generalise to new LLM versions. There is a need for detection methods that capture deeper, more stable signals of LLM authorship that are harder to obfuscate and that transfer better across models.
AI.method: They extract reasoning structures from texts via an argument mining pipeline, building "reasoning graphs" that represent argumentative and inferential relations. These graphs are then fed into a graph neural network (GNN) to learn representations that distinguish human-authored from LLM-generated text. They compare this reasoning-graph GNN approach to a Longformer-based baseline detector that works directly on the linear text. They also evaluate robustness under obfuscation attacks (e.g., paraphrasing, backtranslation) and cross-model generalisation to texts from unseen LLM versions.
AI.result: The proposed reasoning-graph-based GNN detector substantially outperforms the Longformer baseline. Under obfuscation attacks such as paraphrasing and backtranslation, it improves performance by up to 27 percentage points. When tested on texts produced by unseen LLM versions (a more realistic setting where new models keep being released), it yields up to 19 percentage point gains over the baseline, indicating markedly better robustness and generalisation.
AI.conclusion: Encoding and analysing deeper reasoning structures via argument-derived graphs allows for more robust LLM-generated text detection and authorship attribution than surface-level linguistic methods. Graph-based reasoning representations are harder to obfuscate with common text transformations and transfer better to new, unseen LLMs, making them a promising direction for future detectors in dynamic real-world environments where models are constantly evolving.

title: Alipay-PIBench: A Realistic Payment Integration Benchmark for Coding Agents
summary: Payment integration is a demanding repository-level software task: agents must select a suitable product, implement coordinated client-server flows, verify payment outcomes, and preserve consistency between transaction and business states. We introduce Alipay-PIBench, a benchmark for evaluating coding agents on realistic Alipay payment integration. It contains nine product-specific projects and 18 task instances, each organized into Basic functional-completion and Advanced risk-aware hardening scenarios. Scenario-specific rubrics support deterministic static, unit, integration, and end-to-end checks, supplemented by LLM-assisted assessment for semantic requirements. We evaluate six coding-agent models and report rubric pass rate (RPR). Under the with-skill condition, mean RPR ranges from 68.58% to 91.37%. Access to the alipay-payment-integration skill improves mean RPR by 10.31 percentage points on average relative to the without-skill condition, with gains varying across models, products, and scenarios. Method-level results distinguish source-level completion, executable payment behavior, and payment-domain requirements. Alipay-PIBench provides a controlled setting for diagnosing model capability and evaluating structured guidance in payment integration.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Alipay-PIBench, a benchmark to systematically evaluate coding agents on realistic Alipay payment integration tasks, measuring their functional correctness and risk-aware robustness with and without specialized payment-integration skills.
AI.motivation: Payment integration in real-world systems is complex and safety-critical: developers (or agents) must choose appropriate payment products, implement correct client-server interaction flows, ensure correct transaction outcomes, and keep business state consistent with payment state. Existing benchmarks for coding agents typically use simplified, file-level tasks and do not capture the end-to-end, repository-level, domain-specific and risk-sensitive nature of payment integration. There is thus a need for a realistic, controlled benchmark to analyze and compare coding agents’ capabilities and the impact of domain-specific skills in this important industrial domain.
AI.method: The authors construct Alipay-PIBench as a repository-level benchmark focused on Alipay payment integration. It includes nine different Alipay product–specific projects and 18 task instances, each provided in two scenarios: (1) Basic functional-completion, and (2) Advanced risk-aware hardening, which emphasizes robustness and risk control. For each scenario, they design detailed rubrics enabling deterministic static analysis, unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests. For requirements that are semantic or harder to capture via automation, they add LLM-assisted assessment. Using this benchmark, they evaluate six different coding-agent models in two settings: with and without access to an explicit “alipay-payment-integration” skill (structured guidance). They compute rubric pass rate (RPR) overall and at the method level to separate source-level completion, actual executable payment behavior, and higher-level payment-domain requirements.
AI.result: Across six coding-agent models, the mean rubric pass rate under the with-skill condition lies between 68.58% and 91.37%, indicating varying but generally strong performance when equipped with domain skills. Providing the alipay-payment-integration skill improves mean RPR by 10.31 percentage points on average compared to the without-skill condition, though the magnitude of improvement differs by model, product, and scenario. Method-level analysis further differentiates where models succeed or fail: some perform well on source-code completion but less well on executing correct payment flows or satisfying domain-specific payment requirements.
AI.conclusion: Alipay-PIBench offers a realistic, structured, and controllable environment to rigorously evaluate coding agents on complex, repository-level payment integration tasks. It enables fine-grained diagnosis of model capabilities across functional correctness, executable behavior, and domain-level requirements, and it demonstrates that structured, domain-specific skills can significantly enhance agent performance in payment integration. The benchmark can thus serve both as an evaluation standard and as a tool for studying how to better guide coding agents in high-stakes software domains.

title: Latent Trajectory Discrimination for AI-Generated Text Detection
summary: Most existing approaches to AI-Generated Text Detection (AIGTD) treat documents as static objects and base their decisions on aggregate statistics or globally compressed embeddings. However, this perspective overlooks the inherently dynamic nature of autoregressive generation, where content evolves progressively through the latent space. In this paper, we reformulate AIGTD as the problem of distinguishing between latent generation trajectories. Instead of relying on static representations, we model how textual representations evolve across the sequence. To this end, we propose Geometric Trajectory and Contrastive Learning (GTCL), a framework that segments the document into ordered local units, encodes each unit in an embedding space, and constructs a structured and sequence-level representation. GTCL then applies contrastive learning to these trajectories to learn geometric regularities associated with the autoregressive generation. Evaluations performed on three different benchmarks and several approaches show that GTCL outperforms detection baselines consistently, which implies that explicitly modeling sequential dynamics provides robust discriminative signals across models and domains. These results suggest that modeling trajectory differences could improve detection and open up a dynamic direction that has been underexplored in previous AIGTD literature.

AI.tldr: They propose a new AI-generated text detector that looks at how text embeddings evolve over the sequence (a trajectory) instead of using only static document-level features, and show this improves detection across datasets and models.
AI.motivation: Existing AI-generated text detection methods mostly treat documents as static, summarized by global statistics or single embeddings. This ignores that autoregressive models generate text step by step, creating characteristic paths in latent space over time. The authors aim to exploit these dynamic generation patterns, hypothesizing that the trajectory of representations contains discriminative signals that static methods miss.
AI.method: They reformulate detection as distinguishing between latent generation trajectories. The method, GTCL (Geometric Trajectory and Contrastive Learning), segments each document into ordered local units (e.g., chunks), encodes each segment into embeddings, and then aggregates these into a structured sequence-level trajectory representation. GTCL applies contrastive learning on these trajectories, pushing apart AI-generated and human trajectories while pulling together those of the same type, thereby learning geometric regularities associated with autoregressive generation.
AI.result: Across three detection benchmarks and multiple baseline methods, GTCL consistently achieves better performance than existing AI-generated text detectors. The improvements hold across different models and domains, indicating that trajectory-based features generalize well and provide stronger detection signals than static representations alone.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling the sequential dynamics of text generation in embedding space—via trajectories and contrastive learning—yields robust gains for AI-generated text detection. This supports the view that differences in latent generation paths between humans and autoregressive models are informative, and highlights dynamic, trajectory-focused modeling as a promising and underexplored direction for future detection research.

title: Collaborative Spatial Learning with Multi-LLM Agents in Networked Social Experiments
comment: Accepted at ASONAM 2026; to appear in the Springer proceedings
summary: Collective problem solving often requires that group members consider the tradeoff between exploitation of known solutions and exploration for new ones, where information of known solutions can be disseminated among individual members through communication networks. The Mason--Watts experiment (PNAS 2012) showed that human groups in shorter-path networks outperform those in longer-path networks on a two-dimensional search task. In this work, we focus on the investigation of such network-efficiency effects in the setting of a group of large language model (LLM) agents. Specifically, we consider groups of sixteen LLM agents playing the Mason--Watts experiment on the eight Mason--Watts network topologies. Moreover, we develop mechanistic Bayesian optimization agents such that the performance of LLM agents can be compared with both the mechanistic agents and the human experimental data. Our computational experiments indicate that the LLM agents show a significant network-efficiency effect when instructed to randomize their first-round choices, but not under the default initialization. In this experiment, adding a one-sentence first-round randomization instruction improves collective payoff by more than three times the estimated payoff difference across the eight network topologies. Also, the Bayesian optimization agents obtain higher payoffs than the evaluated LLM agents on this spatial search task. We further compare the agents' exploration--exploitation behavior, copying, and spatial diversity.

AI.tldr: Study tests how groups of LLM agents solve a spatial search task on different communication networks, comparing them to Bayesian optimization agents and human data, and examines when network efficiency effects appear.
AI.motivation: Collective problem solving depends on how individuals balance exploration and exploitation while sharing information through networks. Prior human experiments (Mason–Watts) showed shorter-path networks give groups an advantage. With LLMs increasingly used as agents in multi‑agent systems, it is important to know whether similar network effects appear for LLM agents, how instructions influence their behavior, and how they compare to more mechanistic optimization methods and humans.
AI.method: Use groups of 16 LLM agents to play the Mason–Watts two‑dimensional search task across the eight original network topologies. Compare two initialization conditions for LLMs: default prompting vs. an added one‑sentence instruction to randomize first‑round choices. Construct mechanistic Bayesian optimization agents tailored to the same task to provide a normative benchmark. Collect performance metrics (payoff) and behavioral measures (exploration–exploitation, copying patterns, spatial diversity) across networks and agent types.
AI.result: LLM groups exhibit a clear network-efficiency effect (shorter-path networks performing better) only when given an explicit first-round randomization instruction; under default initialization, no strong network effect appears. The randomization instruction substantially boosts collective payoff—by more than three times the payoff gap observed across the eight network topologies themselves. Bayesian optimization agents outperform all tested LLM configurations on the spatial search task. Distinct differences arise in exploration/exploitation balance, copying behavior, and spatial search diversity between LLM and Bayesian agents.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based multi‑agent systems can display human-like network-efficiency effects, but these effects are fragile and depend critically on how agents are initialized and instructed—simple instructions about initial randomness can dominate network-structure advantages. Mechanistic Bayesian optimization still yields superior performance, indicating that LLM agents are not yet optimal for such spatial search tasks. Careful prompt design and an understanding of exploration–exploitation dynamics are crucial when deploying LLM groups for collective problem solving over networks.

title: OmniaBench: Benchmarking General AI Agents Across Diverse Scenarios
summary: Large language models are increasingly evolving from text generators into general agents capable of understanding user requests, invoking external tools, and completing complex tasks through interaction. However, existing agent benchmarks often focus on limited scenarios, tool ecosystems, or interaction formats, making it difficult to systematically characterize model capabilities across heterogeneous application settings. We introduce OmniaBench, a benchmark for evaluating general agents across diverse scenarios with explicit state spaces. We derive application-oriented scenario knowledge from app stores, product documents, industry resources, Web retrieval, and human refinement, forming a hierarchical taxonomy that spans ToC, ToB and ToE with 90 level-1 and 354 level-2 domains. Based on this taxonomy, we construct executable environments and synthesize single-turn and multi-turn tasks through four complementary routes: DAG, DAG-S, Solver, and Program. OmniaBench further introduces a ten-dimensional capability taxonomy and eight compositional atomic difficulty factors to support fine-grained evaluation and analysis. The resulting dataset contains 1,431 tasks, together with a challenging subset of 644 tasks designed to reduce evaluation cost and mitigate potential contamination of the full set after public release. The bench presents substantial challenges to current frontier models, with even Claude-Sonnet-5 and GPT-5.6-Sol achieving Overall Pass@1 scores of only 58.54 and 57.14, respectively. Further analyses reveal clear differences across domains and capabilities, as well as persistent limitations in planning, constraint maintenance, and adaptive correction. OmniaBench provides a broad and diagnostic benchmark for characterizing the capability boundaries of general agents.

AI.tldr: The paper presents OmniaBench, a broad, stateful benchmark to systematically evaluate general-purpose LLM agents across diverse real-world-like applications, tools, and interaction patterns.
AI.motivation: Existing agent benchmarks cover narrow scenarios, limited tool ecosystems, or specific interaction formats, so they can’t comprehensively or diagnostically measure the capabilities and limitations of modern LLM-based agents operating as general tool-using systems. The authors want a unified, realistic, and fine-grained way to characterize such agents’ strengths, weaknesses, and capability boundaries across heterogeneous applications.
AI.method: They (1) build a large, hierarchical domain taxonomy spanning consumer (ToC), business (ToB), and engineering (ToE) use cases using app-store data, product docs, industry resources, web retrieval, and human curation (90 level-1 and 354 level-2 domains); (2) construct executable environments with explicit state spaces for these domains; (3) synthesize both single- and multi-turn tasks via four task-generation pipelines—DAG, DAG-S, Solver, and Program—yielding grounded, tool-based interactions; and (4) define a 10-dimensional capability taxonomy plus 8 atomic difficulty factors so that each task can be labeled and analyzed along interpretable capability and difficulty axes. They also design a smaller but harder subset of tasks to control evaluation cost and contamination risk.
AI.result: They obtain a benchmark of 1,431 tasks plus a more challenging 644-task subset. When evaluated, strong frontier models such as Claude-Sonnet-5 and GPT-5.6-Sol only reach around 58–57% Overall Pass@1, indicating that the tasks are non-trivial and far from saturated. The analysis shows systematic performance variation across domains and capabilities, highlighting specific weaknesses such as planning, constraint adherence, and adaptive correction under changing conditions.
AI.conclusion: OmniaBench serves as a comprehensive, diagnostic benchmark for LLM-based agents, exposing meaningful failure modes and capability gaps in current frontier systems rather than being quickly saturated. Its structured domains, explicit stateful environments, and capability/difficulty taxonomies enable fine-grained analysis of general agent behavior and help define and probe the current boundaries of general-purpose tool-using LLM agents.

title: Multi-LLM Collaborative MRI Report Generation for Visual Instruction Tuning in Brain Oncology
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) and their extension to vision-language models (VLMs) have made it easier to combine text and images for tasks such as report generation. Existing VLMs in medicine typically focus on 2D images (chest X-rays), and their extension to 3D imaging has been difficult because of the lack of paired 3D imaging-text data. Thus, we introduce a new method for creating a 3D image-text dataset for brain oncology using 3D MRI scans of glioma and meningioma cases. We use a cooperative system in which several LLMs work together to generate and check reports, ensuring that they are accurate and clear. By leveraging the new 3D MRI-text dataset, we further build a VLM that converts MRI scans into tokens and aligns them with text instructions. Our VLM performed better in report generation and visual question answering tasks than other 2D and 3D methods. Our method not only improves the quality of reports but also helps with better diagnosis and treatment in brain oncology.

AI.tldr: They build a cooperative-LLM pipeline to create a 3D brain MRI–text dataset for tumors and train a vision-language model that outperforms prior 2D/3D methods on reporting and VQA.
AI.motivation: Medical VLMs mostly use 2D images like chest X-rays because 3D imaging (e.g., MRI) lacks high-quality paired image–text data. Brain oncology needs detailed 3D information for diagnosis and treatment planning, but manual report creation is time‑consuming and existing models don’t handle 3D well. The authors aim to fill this data and capability gap so that automated tools can assist clinicians more effectively.
AI.method: 1) Collect 3D MRI scans for glioma and meningioma cases. 2) Use a multi‑LLM cooperative framework: different LLMs generate, cross‑check, and refine radiology-style reports to ensure accuracy and clarity, thereby creating reliable text annotations for each 3D scan. 3) Design a vision-language model that encodes the 3D MRI into tokens and aligns them with textual instructions, enabling report generation and visual question answering. 4) Compare this model to existing 2D and 3D baselines on standard tasks.
AI.result: The newly built VLM, trained on the 3D MRI–text dataset, achieves higher performance than existing 2D and 3D approaches on report generation and visual question answering benchmarks in brain oncology. The cooperative-LLM pipeline is effective at producing usable, high‑quality text labels for 3D MRI data.
AI.conclusion: Creating a dedicated 3D MRI–text dataset for brain tumors with a cooperative LLM labeling pipeline enables training a strong vision-language model tailored to brain oncology. This model generates better clinical reports and answers visual questions more accurately than prior methods, suggesting practical benefits for diagnosis and treatment planning in 3D neuroimaging contexts.

title: Rubrics on Trial: Evolving Rubrics from a Single Query via Synthetic Pairwise Evidence
summary: Rubrics provide structured, fine-grained signals for training and evaluating large language models (LLMs). Yet reliable query-specific rubrics are difficult to construct. Existing approaches often derive supervision from human-written rubrics, preference data, or sampled responses. Direct query-to-rubric generation avoids these resources, but provides no explicit check that a plausible rubric is useful. Such a rubric may fail to distinguish answer quality, reward an optional style, or penalize a valid alternative strategy. We introduce Rubrics on Trial, a query-only framework that evolves a rubric set from an empty set without external annotations or model training. It derives supervision solely from synthetic rubric-conditioned response pairs and validates each proposed rubric before adding it, screening out non-discriminative, over-specific, and style-only candidate rubrics. Experiments across five preference benchmark suites demonstrate the effectiveness of Rubrics on Trial, which achieves the best average accuracy and leads on six of seven evaluation sets.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Rubrics on Trial, a query-only framework that automatically constructs and validates rubrics for evaluating LLM responses without external annotations or model training, achieving state-of-the-art performance on multiple preference benchmarks.
AI.motivation: High-quality rubrics are crucial for supervising and evaluating LLMs, but query-specific rubrics are hard and expensive to build. Existing methods rely on human-written rubrics, preference data, or sampled responses, or generate rubrics directly from queries without verifying their usefulness. There is a need for a method that can automatically discover reliable rubrics without external supervision and ensure that they genuinely distinguish response quality instead of capturing superficial style or over-specific criteria.
AI.method: Rubrics on Trial starts from an empty rubric set and incrementally proposes new rubrics using only the input query. For each candidate rubric, the system synthesizes pairs of rubric-conditioned responses and tests whether the rubric can reliably distinguish better from worse answers. It filters out rubrics that are non-discriminative (cannot tell answer quality apart), over-specific (too narrow to generalize), or purely stylistic (rewarding optional style rather than substance). Only rubrics that pass these validation checks are added to the rubric set. This process requires no external annotations, preference labels, or further model training.
AI.result: Across five preference benchmark suites and seven evaluation sets, Rubrics on Trial attains the best average accuracy compared to existing rubric-based or preference-based approaches. It ranks first on six out of seven evaluation sets, indicating that its automatically constructed rubric sets are highly effective for distinguishing and selecting high-quality LLM responses.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that high-quality, query-specific rubrics for LLM evaluation can be generated and refined automatically from queries alone, without human labels or additional model training. By validating and filtering candidate rubrics using synthetic response pairs, Rubrics on Trial avoids common rubric pitfalls (non-discriminative, over-specific, style-only criteria) and achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple preference benchmarks, suggesting a scalable path toward rubric-based supervision and evaluation.

title: MathCoPilot: An Interactive System for Human-AI Symbiotic Paradigm of Mathematical Research
summary: Existing LLM-based theorem provers have achieved impressive results on formal mathematics benchmarks, yet they remain confined to acting as autonomous agents that prove a stated proposition. In this paper, we propose MathCoPilot, a human-in-the-loop system that embodies a new human--AI symbiotic paradigm for mathematical research, in which the mathematician steers the high-level mathematical direction while AI agents carry out the detailed formalization and proof work under continuous human guidance. MathCoPilot unifies three core capabilities: (1) an interactive workbench where the mathematician and AI agents collaborate through a living proof blueprint that decomposes a proof into navigable steps the human can directly inspect, direct, and refine; (2) automated proving skill orchestration with adaptive knowledge base search and Lean-integrated iterative verification; and (3) topic-driven paper retrieval and automated formalization into a verified Lean knowledge base. Using MathCoPilot, we systematically compare four state-of-the-art LLMs, including Gemini~3.1~Pro, GPT-5.4, and Claude~Opus~4.7, on a FormalMATH subset and on two real PDE theorems requiring deep domain expertise, evaluating their ability to produce verified Lean~4 proofs and to identify errors in deliberately incorrect proofs. Our results show that while current models can handle undergraduate-level problems with high success rates under favorable autoformalization conditions, substantial challenges remain for domain-specific theorems requiring genuine mathematical understanding.

AI.tldr: MathCoPilot is a human-in-the-loop system where mathematicians collaborate with LLM agents to formalize and prove theorems in Lean through an interactive, modular proof blueprint, automated proof orchestration, and literature-to-Lean pipelines.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based theorem provers work mainly as autonomous agents: they are good at solving benchmark problems but are poorly suited to real mathematical research, where humans need to explore ideas, inspect intermediate reasoning, reuse literature, and work with domain-heavy theorems. There is a gap between "proving a given statement" and supporting the full, iterative, collaborative workflow of working mathematicians.
AI.method: The authors design MathCoPilot, a symbiotic environment that (1) offers an interactive proof workbench with a living, decomposed proof blueprint editable by humans; (2) integrates multiple LLM-based proving skills with adaptive search over a Lean knowledge base and iterative verification in Lean; and (3) retrieves topic-relevant papers and autoformalizes mathematical content into a curated Lean 4 library. They then use this environment to benchmark four SOTA LLMs on a FormalMATH subset and on two challenging PDE theorems, testing both proof generation and error-detection in incorrect proofs.
AI.result: In the MathCoPilot setting, modern LLMs like Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.4, and Claude Opus 4.7 can often produce verified Lean 4 proofs for undergraduate-level problems, particularly when autoformalization gives them a favorable representation. However, even the strongest models struggle significantly on specialized PDE theorems demanding deeper conceptual understanding and domain expertise, and they have limited robustness in catching subtle errors in deliberately flawed proofs.
AI.conclusion: A human-in-the-loop, symbiotic workflow can make current LLM theorem provers practically useful for standard-level mathematics and structured formalization tasks, but there remains a substantial gap before they can reliably assist with advanced, domain-specific research theorems that require genuine mathematical insight. Further progress is needed both in model capabilities and in tooling for richer human guidance and verification.

title: Digital Pantheon: Simulating and Auditing Coalition Formation with LLM Agents
comment: 11 pages, 2 figures, To be published in the Post-Workshop proceedings of the ECML PKDD 2026 Conference
summary: The formation of political coalitions is a complex negotiation driven by both concrete policy objectives and deep-seated ideological convictions. While Large Language Models (LLMs) open new avenues for computational political science, the neutrality and helpfulness biases instilled by Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) prevent them from sustaining steadfast partisan behaviour. We present a multi-agent framework that reconciles factual grounding with ideological alignment by combining Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG): DPO instils aggressive party-specific personas, while a per-party RAG pipeline keeps each agent bounded to its official manifesto. We operationalize the framework on the 2019 Flemish election, deploying the partisan agents in a hub-and-spoke negotiation arbitrated by a formateur. To make the emergent negotiation interpretable, we introduce a Multi-Layered Information Lineage Topology (MILT) that traces every clause in the final agreement back to its manifesto origin and classifies it into five provenance states, a Coalition Influence Score (CIS) that aggregates these traceable contributions to identify which party shaped the agreement, and a real-world grounding pass that benchmarks each simulated provision against the historically adopted coalition agreement. Across three independent simulations the framework yields a stable winner and ranking (N-VA ahead of CD\&V and Open Vld), and manifesto-anchored lineage reliably predicts real-world materialization whereas hallucinated content does not. The result is a transparent, scalable testbed for the ex-ante exploration of party compatibility and formateur-mediated compromise.

AI.tldr: The paper builds ideologically aligned, manifesto-grounded partisan LLM agents and uses them to simulate coalition negotiations, adding tools to trace which party shaped each clause and to compare to the real coalition agreement.
AI.motivation: Standard RLHF-trained LLMs favor neutrality and helpfulness, which makes them bad at consistently representing strong partisan positions needed to study coalition formation in political science. There is a need for a computational, transparent, and empirically grounded way to simulate multi-party negotiations and to see how party manifestos shape coalition agreements, while controlling hallucinations.
AI.method: The authors design a multi-agent system for coalition bargaining in which each agent represents a political party. They combine: (1) supervised fine-tuning to adapt a base model; (2) Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to enforce strong, party-specific partisan personas that resist neutrality; and (3) per-party Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) so each agent is constrained by its official manifesto. Negotiations follow a hub-and-spoke architecture coordinated by a formateur agent. To make outcomes interpretable, they introduce Multi-Layered Information Lineage Topology (MILT) that traces each clause in the final coalition agreement to its manifesto sources and assigns one of five provenance states. They also define a Coalition Influence Score (CIS) that aggregates lineage-based contributions to quantify each party’s influence. Finally, they conduct a real-world grounding step by comparing simulated agreement clauses to the actual 2019 Flemish coalition agreement.
AI.result: Applied to the 2019 Flemish election, their framework produces consistent coalition outcomes across three independent simulations, with the same winner and ranking of parties (N-VA first, then CD&V, then Open Vld). The MILT lineage analysis shows that clauses grounded in manifesto content are predictive of which provisions appear in the real coalition agreement, whereas hallucinated or non-manifesto-based content does not correlate with real-world materialization. The CIS metrics highlight which parties most shaped the simulated agreements.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework offers a transparent and scalable way to simulate and analyze coalition negotiations using partisan LLM agents that remain grounded in party manifestos. By combining DPO, SFT, and per-party RAG, the system overcomes neutrality bias and maintains ideological consistency, while MILT and CIS make the negotiation process and outcomes interpretable and empirically comparable to real agreements. This creates a useful ex-ante testbed for exploring party compatibility, coalition stability, and formateur-mediated compromise.

title: SportD: Can VLMs Physically Strategize?
summary: Vision--language models have become increasingly capable of interpreting visual scenes, but it remains unclear whether they can use information to make strategically effective decisions. We investigate this question in soccer, where models observe the seconds preceding an on-ball decision and must choose whether to shoot or pass to a specific teammate. Unlike conventional visual-understanding tasks, soccer enables decisions to be evaluated quantitatively by estimating the value of every available action. We introduce SportD, a benchmark comprising 478 on-ball decisions from the 2022 FIFA World Cup. Each model choice is evaluated against a possession-value model that estimates the action that most increases the attacking team's probability of scoring, allowing us to measure both optimal-action accuracy and the value forfeited by suboptimal decisions. Across three frontier VLMs, the best selects the highest-valued action on 31.4% of events, compared with 38.9% for the professional players, and all models incur significantly greater regret. Further analysis reveals a systematic preference for lower-variance and lower-reward actions: VLMs shoot less often and select substantially less progressive passes than either the optimal policy or the real players. The models also reproduce the player's specific action above chance even when that action is suboptimal, suggesting partial imitation of familiar play patterns rather than consistent evaluation of counterfactual alternatives. SportD provides a value-grounded testbed for measuring physical strategic reasoning in VLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether modern vision–language models (VLMs) can make strategically effective decisions in soccer by deciding when to shoot or which teammate to pass to, using a new benchmark from 2022 World Cup events and a possession-value model as ground truth.
AI.motivation: While VLMs can describe and interpret visual scenes, it is unclear if they can reason strategically about actions with quantifiable consequences in dynamic physical environments. Soccer provides a domain where each possible action has an estimable expected value in terms of scoring probability, allowing rigorous evaluation of decision quality rather than just recognition or captioning. The authors want to move from perception capabilities to measuring genuine physical strategic reasoning in VLMs.
AI.method: They construct SportD, a dataset of 478 on-ball decision situations from the 2022 FIFA World Cup. For each clip, a VLM sees the seconds before an on-ball decision and must choose either to shoot or to pass to a designated teammate. A separate possession-value model provides an estimated scoring probability for each candidate action. The authors then compare model choices with the highest-valued action to compute optimal-action accuracy and quantify regret (value lost) when models select suboptimal actions. They test three state-of-the-art VLMs and perform further behavioral analysis of their action preferences and tendencies to imitate human players.
AI.result: The strongest VLM chooses the action with the highest estimated value in 31.4% of cases, worse than professional players at 38.9%, and all models incur much higher regret than humans. The models systematically favor lower-variance, safer actions: they shoot less and choose less progressive passes than both the optimal policy and real players. Yet they also reproduce the actual human player's action at above-chance rates, even when that action is suboptimal, indicating partial imitation of familiar patterns rather than robust evaluation of alternatives.
AI.conclusion: Current VLMs show limited capacity for high-quality physical strategic reasoning in soccer: they underperform professionals, avoid high-reward but riskier options, and appear to rely on pattern imitation rather than genuine counterfactual evaluation. SportD offers a value-grounded benchmark that can be used to probe and improve strategic decision-making abilities of future VLMs in dynamic, real-world environments.

title: Grokipedia vs Wikipedia: An LLM-Based Audit of Political Neutrality along Ideologies
summary: Online encyclopedias shape political opinion and, through it, democratic discourse. In late 2025, Grokipedia was released, an encyclopedia written entirely by the LLM Grok. One motivation behind the project was to provide an unbiased alternative to Wikipedia, which has faced accusations of "left-wing" and "liberal" bias. But does an encyclopedia written by an LLM deliver greater neutrality, or does it simply embed a different ideology? We conduct a large-scale political bias study on Grokipedia and Wikipedia, analysing 1,394 article pairs describing members of government for neutrality along nine expert-coded ideology dimensions employing four LLM judges, Grok, Claude, Mistral, and DeepSeek. As the LLMs could themselves be biased, we also investigate patterns in their judgments. We find all LLM-judges, including Grok, to rate Grokipedia less neutral than Wikipedia. Both encyclopedias are rated as portraying politicians favourably overall, but towards different ideological groups. Grokipedia particularly favours economically right-wing politicians and penalises socially liberal ones, while Wikipedia is rated as favourably biased towards the latter.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically compares political bias in a fully LLM-written encyclopedia (Grokipedia) versus Wikipedia, finding that both exhibit ideological slants but in different directions and that multiple LLM judges consistently see Wikipedia as more neutral.
AI.motivation: To test the claim that an encyclopedia written entirely by a large language model (Grokipedia) offers a more politically neutral alternative to Wikipedia, which is often criticized for alleged left-liberal bias, and to understand how LLM-based judging of bias behaves given that the judges themselves may be ideologically biased.
AI.method: The authors assemble 1,394 matched article pairs on government members from Grokipedia and Wikipedia. They then evaluate these pairs along nine expert-defined ideological dimensions using four different LLMs (Grok, Claude, Mistral, DeepSeek) as judges. They analyze neutrality scores and also examine systematic patterns and potential biases in the judgments of the LLM evaluators themselves.
AI.result: All four LLM judges, including Grok, rate Grokipedia as less neutral than Wikipedia overall. Both encyclopedias are judged to portray politicians in a generally favourable way, but the direction of ideological favouritism differs: Grokipedia tends to favour economically right-wing and penalize socially liberal politicians, whereas Wikipedia is rated as more favourable to socially liberal politicians.
AI.conclusion: An LLM-written encyclopedia does not inherently provide greater political neutrality than a human-edited one; instead it can encode a different ideological profile. Grokipedia, in particular, appears more favourable to economically right-wing and less favourable to socially liberal politicians than Wikipedia, which itself is judged as more favourable to socially liberal figures. LLMs as bias judges show consistent patterns, indicating that evaluator bias must also be considered in such assessments.

title: Action QFormer: Structured Representation Shaping under Action Supervision in Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Action supervision in vision-language-action (VLA) models is often treated as a downstream objective for learning action prediction. In this paper, we study it instead as a force that shapes inherited multimodal representations. We show that this shaping has a dual effect: it is necessary for forming action-compatible representations, but when action supervision is applied too directly to the inherited multimodal pathway, it can also destabilize representations that support language-side processing and object grounding. To address this tension, we introduce Action QFormer, a query-based action-facing interface that uses instruction-conditioned queries to reorganize inherited multimodal information into action-facing representations before downstream action generation. In zero-shot sim-to-real navigation, Action QFormer improves average closed-loop task success from 18.8% to 56.3%, raises fixed-instruction action-generation correctness from 22.5% to 75.5%, and nearly eliminates out-of-distribution instruction generations. Further analyses show that Action QFormer changes how action supervision shapes inherited multimodal representations, reducing broad upstream rewriting while preserving targeted and sometimes constructive action-supervised adaptation. These results suggest that improving VLA performance requires not only stronger pretrained backbones, but also better ways of selecting and organizing inherited multimodal information while controlling how it is shaped under action supervision.

AI.tldr: They propose Action QFormer, a query-based interface that reshapes multimodal representations for actions, boosting sim-to-real navigation performance while preserving language and grounding capabilities.
AI.motivation: Existing vision-language-action models use action supervision only as a downstream signal, which can unintentionally damage the language and object-grounding abilities of the inherited multimodal backbone when applied too directly. There is a need to harness action supervision so that it creates action-suitable representations without destabilizing general multimodal understanding.
AI.method: Introduce Action QFormer, a query-based interface between the pretrained multimodal backbone and the action generator. It uses instruction-conditioned queries to reorganize and extract relevant multimodal information into an action-facing representation. The design aims to localize and control how action supervision influences the upstream multimodal representations, limiting broad rewriting while still allowing targeted adaptation.
AI.result: On zero-shot sim-to-real navigation benchmarks, Action QFormer lifts average closed-loop task success from 18.8% to 56.3%, increases fixed-instruction action-generation correctness from 22.5% to 75.5%, and significantly reduces out-of-distribution instruction generations. Representation analyses indicate that the method alters the pattern of action-induced changes in the backbone, reducing global disruption while enabling focused, sometimes beneficial, adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Action supervision both enables and can destabilize multimodal representations in VLA models. By inserting an instruction-conditioned query interface (Action QFormer), one can organize and filter inherited multimodal information for action generation, achieving large performance gains while limiting harmful rewriting of the backbone. Progress in VLA thus depends not only on stronger pretrained backbones but also on how multimodal features are selected and shaped under action supervision.

title: Linear representations of grammaticality in neural language models
summary: Whether neural language models (NLMs) possess the ability to distinguish strings on the basis of their grammaticality remains a debated topic in the computational linguistics literature. Existing evidence has largely relied on probability-based measures, testing whether models assign higher probabilities to grammatical than ungrammatical strings. However, probability comparisons have been criticized as a measure for grammatical knowledge based on the assumption that grammaticality is inherently entangled with likelihood. Model-assigned probability is a function of many related sentence properties, such as lexical frequency, plausibility, and world knowledge.
